key_people: Shawn Joseph, Interim Superintendent of Schools
Judith White, PGCPS Chief Academic Officer
Alexandra Tyukavina, Paint Branch PTA Secretary
organizations: Prince George’s County Board of Education
51753.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to send a letter to the Prince George’s County Board of Education in support of continued funding for the Chinese immersion program at Paint Branch Elementary School. The program has become an important part of the broader educational ecosystem serving College Park residents, and changes that significantly reduce immersion opportunities could undermine years of investment by educators, students and the community. According to Paint Branch PTA Secretary Alexandra Tyukavina, the existing program does not allow parents to opt their children out of immersion, and she proposes adding an opt-out option to make the program more equitable, saying 'the opt-out option must be added to make the program more equitable'.

key_people: Sami Saeed, public policy major at the University of Maryland
David Dorsch, College Park resident and landlord
Nick DiSpirito, UMD's student liaison to the city council
51534.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved an ordinance that prevents landlords from asking tenants to renew a lease sooner than 180 days from the end of the current lease. The ordinance was introduced in September and was supported by students who shared stories of landlords approaching them as soon as a month after move-in, threatening to raise rent or replace them with other tenants unless they renewed their leases. As Sami Saeed, a public policy major at the University of Maryland, stated, 'The stress that that caused me … trying to find anyone to live with for next year. I had to call 50 people. … That is the situation renters are faced with. That is the situation I was faced with. It happened to me.'

key_people: Donald Hays, Chairman of the BPAC Subcommittee
organizations: College Park’s Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee (BPAC)
51397.ai_summary: A citizen-led committee, the College Park’s Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee (BPAC), resubmitted a year-old report on school bus stop safety to the College Park City Council, recommending changes to protect elementary school children. The report included safety evaluations for 75 elementary school bus stops and recommended upgrades such as adding bike lanes and installing a sidewalk at St. Andrews Place and Duke Street, which Donald Hays, the chairman of the BPAC subcommittee, described as 'the most dangerous place for elementary school children to wait for their bus that could be fixed tomorrow.' The committee's efforts are aimed at encouraging officials to make changes to improve safety at school bus stops.

key_people: Maxine Gross, member of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project and fifth-generation Lakeland resident
51400.ai_summary: The Lakeland neighborhood in College Park is planning to add 10 murals to highlight its history, as part of a decades-long effort to preserve the community's heritage. According to Maxine Gross, a member of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project, 'It's just a way for folks to be able to see the community that we have been talking about for a number of years,' and 'We're talking about what that community was like [before urban renewal], and help people to understand the asset that was lost and help them to understand the commitment for rebuilding.' The project is a joint effort among city officials, community members, and University of Maryland partners, with a total budget of $28,382, and is expected to be completed by the end of 2026.

51390.ai_summary: Three housing policy students from the University of Maryland suggested ways to ease neighborhood tensions near the university, citing the 'studentification' of established neighborhoods and degradation of community quality of life. As Frances Chen said, 'There has … been an issue with neighborhood degradation, with many students and party houses creating a lot of trash as well as general noise complaints within neighborhoods.' Councilmember Alan Hew emphasized that the university should take more responsibility for teaching its students how to be good neighbors, stating 'The university well knows that, after two years living on campus, students are expected to live off campus, They’re expected to live in our community.'

51376.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering expanding permit parking to restrict parking in residential neighborhoods. Parking Enforcement Officer Jim Miller proposed a study to examine whether parking should be permit-restricted on additional streets throughout the city, saying 'It’s a matter of how that restricted parking is utilized.' Miller also proposed charging an annual fee for each permit, citing administrative costs, but former Councilmember Susan Whitney expressed concerns that paid street parking could lead to less green space and increased stormwater runoff, saying 'We want people who need their cars to be able to park them, and we want people who can get by with one car to do that and to be encouraged to do that.'

51441.ai_summary: The College Park City Council agreed to recommend that Prince George’s County amend a parking ordinance to allow a developer to build a four-story, 87-unit apartment building for older residents on Branchville Road. The project, called Branchville Crossing, will provide 'a scaled-down, senior affordable project that we believe fits not only the needs of the city but of the county,' said Matthew Tedesco, a lawyer representing Cruz Development Corp. 'There is a real need for senior housing in College Park,' said Councilmember Kelly Jordan, 'We’re very happy this is being brought before us.'

key_people: Tom Dernoga, Prince George’s County Councilmember
51373.ai_summary: Maryland Governor Wes Moore has proposed a change in zoning law to allow for more affordable homes, with the goal of addressing the state's housing shortage of roughly 100,000 homes. According to House Speaker Joseline Peña-Melnyk, 'Maryland has a housing shortage of roughly 100,000 homes... When people can’t afford to live here, we lose workers. We lose families. And we lose the economic growth that allows us to fund schools, transportation and public safety.' State Senator Jim Rosapepe and Prince George’s County Councilmember Tom Dernoga have expressed concerns about the proposal, with Dernoga calling it a 'sprawl multiplier' that would worsen congestion and strain public safety resources.

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Erica Johnson, Sgt. and coordinator of the Prince George’s County Police Department’s Citizens’ Police Academy
51366.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember Alan Hew has graduated from three different citizen police academies, where he learned about police operations and community relationships. Hew said, 'There’s a lot of speculation of how they operate and I wanted to be able to speak confidently in support of the departments that operate within our city.' The training has helped him contribute to council discussions about safety and understand police operations, and has also given him a chance to network with police officers and address issues such as a recent surge in graffiti around the city.

51316.ai_summary: A dangerous winter storm named Fern brought a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain to the Route 1 corridor, prompting local jurisdictions to mount snow removal and emergency response efforts. Prince George’s County Executive Aisha Braveboy stated, 'The county’s snow response operation is fully active. Crews are working around the clock to clear and treat the roadways and public safety remains are top priority.' College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir reminded residents to report icy sidewalks and unsafe conditions to the city and to check when the streets they live on were last plowed through an interactive map published by the city.

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Alex Posorske, Veo Policy and Partnerships Manager for the East Coast
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
51475.ai_summary: The use of electric scooters and bikes in College Park and University Park has increased by 62% between fall 2024 and fall 2025, prompting Veo to implement stricter parking rules. As City Councilmember Alan Hew noted, riders who park outside of designated areas are charged for an extra 20 minutes of use, with Hew saying, 'This is just almost malicious.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated, 'We are very happy that we have this micromobility system in College Park because this is in line with our strategic sustainability goal,' highlighting the importance of reducing car usage in the city.

51408.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has become younger with the election of three newcomers, including Daniel Oates, Kelly Jordan, and Holly Simmons, who bring different experiences and perspectives to the council. As Oates noted, 'To me what’s interesting is less [about] the relative ages than the new people coming on the council and bringing different experiences and different perspectives from that vein.' The new council members believe that their fresh perspectives will help the city think about its future and address issues such as utility bills, local schools, and safety, with Jordan saying 'We’ll all have lots of questions and … when you’re used to something, comfortable with something, new questions can bring new ideas for how you’re used to looking at it.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
organizations: U.S. Bureau of City Labor Statistics
key_issues: economic impact on College Park residents
51404.ai_summary: One year into President Donald Trump's second term, College Park is grappling with the impact of executive orders, employee layoffs, funding cuts, and new policies. According to Mayor Fazlul Kabir, the city's residents, economy, and plans for future development have been affected, with many federal employees and contractors losing their jobs or going without pay during the 43-day government shutdown. As Kabir stated, 'Neighbors have had to take jobs that they are not trained for, and with much lower pay,' and 'I know that they're concerned and they're struggling,' prompting the city to increase its financial assistance program to $100,000 to accommodate more residents.

key_people: Joseline Peña-Melnyk, Speaker of the Maryland House of Delegates
Adrienne A. Jones, Former Speaker of the Maryland House of Delegates
organizations: U.S. Attorney’s Office for the District of Columbia
House Health and Government Operations Committee
50899.ai_summary: Joseline Peña-Melnyk, a College Park resident and former College Park City Councilmember, was sworn in as the 109th Speaker of the Maryland House of Delegates. Peña-Melnyk, who became the second woman speaker and the first Afro-Latina to hold that position, said 'This moment belongs not just to me, but to every child catching rain in a pot.' She replaces Speaker Adrienne A. Jones, who served in that role for almost seven years, and has stated her intention to collaborate with both Democrats and Republicans as an 'inclusive' leader.

key_people: Edouard Haba, PGCMA President and Hyattsville City Council member
Monica Casañas, PGCMA Vice President and Mayor of Colmar Manor
50826.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has decided to pause its membership in the Prince George’s County Municipal Association (PGCMA) after a former treasurer allegedly embezzled over $30,000. Councilmember Jacob Hernandez stated, 'It’s our taxpayer money... Why would we give money to an organization that is clearly lacking integrity?' PGCMA President Edouard Haba said the organization will submit to financial reviews and has formed a financial oversight committee to prevent future thefts, noting 'We all want all of us to stay together and move forward' and that the association's value is 'camaraderie and networking' among elected municipal leaders.

key_people: Carly Zapernick, Managing Director of Construction and Development at Article Student Living
Julie Bringman, Pastor of Hope Lutheran Church
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park Councilmember (District 2)
50763.ai_summary: College Park officials pressed a Chicago-based student housing developer to include affordable units in a mixed-use building planned for Guilford Drive. The developer, Article Student Living, outlined its proposal for a 192-unit complex that will include a new 17,000-square-foot church to replace the one that will be torn down during construction. As Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'The catch is that you must have affordable housing in it,' and Councilmember Denise Mitchell emphasized, 'We have a vision moving forward, and we’re not going to negate that vision,' highlighting the city’s goal to bring more affordable housing to College Park.

key_people: Nisha Kumar, University of Maryland student
Scott Tebbe, University of Maryland student
Lisa Miller, President of the Prince George's Property Owners Association
Nick DiSpirito, UMD student liaison to the council
Amira Abujuma, UMD student liaison to the council
50602.ai_summary: Nearly 30 people, mostly University of Maryland students, asked the College Park City Council to prevent landlords from pressuring tenants to renew leases before they have lived in their units for six months. Students like Nisha Kumar and Scott Tebbe shared their experiences, with Kumar saying 'We've just moved in for the semester and we already have to figure out next year... Who's going to take my spot? … Will I have to pay over $1,000 a month for a place I'm not even living in?' and Tebbe stating 'This was a decision I wasn't ready to make, and I felt trapped.' The proposed ordinance aims to protect students from pressure to sign 'predatory' lease-renewal agreements far in advance.

key_people: Samantha McLoughlin, Parent Teacher Organization (PTO) President
Carl Franceski, General Manager of Sales and Operations for Coca-Cola Consolidated
50509.ai_summary: The local bottler for Coca-Cola gave each of Hollywood Elementary School’s 301 students new sneakers, socks, and school supplies through Coca-Cola Consolidated’s Back-to-School with Purpose initiative. As Samantha McLoughlin, PTO President, said, 'We believe our kids deserve just as much as the opportunities that better-funded schools get' and 'Our kids are just as smart, just as gifted.' Principal Carlos Johnson has partnered with parents and the community to provide support to students and their families, which City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez noted, saying 'He’s gone above and beyond to make sure he’s providing meaningful outreach.'

key_people: Helen Reniere, Resident of Attick Towers
Glenn Reniere, Engineer at Vitro Corp.
Charlotte Davis, Staff Member at Attick Towers
50310.ai_summary: Helen Reniere, a resident of Attick Towers, celebrated her 100th birthday and received a proclamation from the College Park mayor and City Council, recognizing her kindness and perseverance. Reniere stated 'I get up in the morning, I take my medicine and … I guess you got to put Jesus first' as her secret to living to 100. The celebration highlights the importance of community support for seniors in College Park, as Reniere credits her friendship with Charlotte Davis, a staff member at the apartment complex, as the 'best thing in my life'.

organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC)
50276.ai_summary: The College Park City Council sent a letter to the Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC) supporting the construction of a bridge connecting Old Calvert Road Park to the Northeast Branch Trail. Councilmember Ray Ranker said, 'We don’t want to spend another month waiting,' and 'We want to get [construction] started … tomorrow,' as his constituents have waited a decade for a bridge to connect the park to the trail system. The estimated cost for the proposed bridge is about $2 million, and the council considered a less-expensive option to modify the Campus Drive bridge, but preferred the new bridge option for safety reasons.

key_people: Gavin Neubauer, University of Maryland public policy student and task force member
Christina Martin Kenny, College Park Estates resident and task force member
organizations: College Park Child Care Task Force
key_issues: support for home-based child care providers
50298.ai_summary: The College Park Child Care Task Force proposed ways to lower child care costs, including offering grants to home-based child care providers and property tax credits to established centers. 'If you’re able to even increase the amount of businesses here that can survive and care for children, we’re going to be a real standout city in this entire metropolitan area,' said Gavin Neubauer, a task force member. The task force's report recommends nearly 20 actions to lower child care costs and increase availability in the city, where the number of child care providers has dropped by 15.5% since 2020.

key_people: Jatinder Khokhar, Director of Public Services
key_issues: increased fines for city code violations
50304.ai_summary: The College Park City Council introduced an ordinance to increase fines for city code violations, including construction, fire safety, housing, noise, and unruly social gatherings, to align with a Maryland law allowing municipalities to fine up to $5,000. Director of Public Services Jatinder Khokhar and City Attorney Stephanie Anderson presented the proposal, which aims to ensure fairness and consistency of enforcement. Councilmember John Rigg noted that fines for noise violations should be lower than those for unruly social gatherings, saying 'Noise should be bad, but unruly social gatherings almost always include noise and it includes a lot more.'

50292.ai_summary: The Nov. 4 election in College Park marked the first time 16- and 17-year-olds were allowed to cast ballots in city elections, with six teenagers voting. Mayor Fazlul Kabir won 1,480 votes in his unopposed re-election bid. The top vote-getters for council seats were District 1 incumbents Jacob Hernandez and Alan Hew, who also ran unopposed.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director
50876.ai_summary: Wonder food hall, featuring takeout and delivery, has opened its first Maryland location in College Park on Baltimore Avenue. According to College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, 'The city has been working hard, and our economic development staff in particular have been working hard to try to really bring an exciting concept here,' and 'We want them to be successful, and we want them to be here for a long time.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir said it was exciting to see Wonder establish its first Maryland location in College Park, stating 'It’s a milestone and College Park is very lucky to have them.'

key_people: Holly Simmons, Deputy Zoning Manager for the city of Rockville and College Park City Councilmember
Kelly Jordan, Co-President of the Berwyn District Civic Association and College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Daniel Oates, Past President of the Calvert Hills Citizens Association and College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Denise Mitchell, Mayor Pro Tem and College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
50191.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has three new members after the November election, with Holly Simmons and Kelly Jordan winning seats in District 2 and Daniel Oates winning the seat in District 3. Jordan said, 'It's a good feeling. I'm happy to be able to serve the residents and excited about the next two years.' Simmons stated that her first action will be to 'listen and learn. I think there's a lot of important work that's underway in the city.' The new council members will take office after the current members' terms expire in December.

key_people: Brencis D. Smith, former council member
Holly Simmons, College Park City Council candidate
Christopher Gill, College Park City Council candidate
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Council candidate
50185.ai_summary: The results of the local election in College Park are uncertain, with some races being very close. In College Park District 2, Holly Simmons seems likely to have secured one of two seats on the council, while Christopher Gill and Kelly Jordan are only one vote apart for the second seat. According to the Laurel Board of Elections webpage, Brencis D. Smith may have an edge over incumbent Christine Johnson in the race for the city’s one At-Large council seat in Laurel.

key_issues: restorative justice for the Lakeland community
50017.ai_summary: The three candidates for two vacant College Park City Council seats in District 2, Chris Gill, Kelly Jordan, and Holly Simmons, discussed their priorities at a candidates' forum hosted by College Park Here & Now, including restorative justice for the Lakeland community and affordable housing. Simmons stated, 'I remember [at] one of the events that I attended for restorative justice in Lakeland, a gentleman said, ‘I want my 40 acres and a mule’, and I know that urban renewal is not the same thing as slavery. However, I think that the reparations are very important.' The candidates are running to fill the seats of former councilmembers Llatetra Brown Esters and Susan Whitney, and their election will impact the city's approach to community issues.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
49848.ai_summary: College Park voters will elect a mayor and eight City Councilmembers in November, with early voting taking place on November 2 and Election Day voting on November 4. Fazlul Kabir, the current mayor, is running for re-election and has led efforts to strengthen the city's finances, open new community spaces, and welcome businesses that create jobs. Kabir has also championed the development of more than 300 units of affordable housing and backed the construction of the College Park Woods Clubhouse, aiming to build a 'stronger, more vibrant College Park'.

key_people: Dan Oates, District 3 City Council candidate
Ray Ranker, District 3 City Council candidate
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
49703.ai_summary: District 3 City Council candidates Dan Oates and Ray Ranker discussed their plans to improve public safety and increase resident involvement at a forum. Oates and Ranker oppose creating an independent police force in College Park, with Ranker stating that it would raise taxes, and instead support researching other ways to improve public safety. Mayor Fazlul Kabir noted that while College Park is a safe place to live, law enforcement could be improved, and he recommended increasing the presence of Prince George’s County police or creating a small, city-run police department, but Oates said 'I think jumping to the answer of ‘we need a police force’ is premature.'

key_people: Christopher Gill, IT Project Manager and City Council Candidate
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
49536.ai_summary: Three candidates, including Christopher Gill, Kelly Jordan, and Holly Simmons, are running for two District 2 City Council seats in College Park. Gill, an IT project manager, wants the city to become a place for people in all phases of life, where 'People can move here as a young professional, as a student, stay here as a first-time homeowner, become a parent if they want, stay here to be a retiree if they want.' He also expressed concern about housing affordability and the trend of owner-occupied homes being flipped into rentals, saying 'I don’t want people to be pushed out of their community.' The election is set to take place on November 4.

key_people: Kelly Jordan, District 2 city council candidate
organizations: Prince George' County Board of Elections
49533.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted 5-3 to lower the voting age for city elections from 18 to 16, allowing 16- and 17-year-olds to vote for mayor and city councilmembers. As 16-year-old Lucas Rigg said, 'I think it lets the people who didn’t choose to live here have a say' and Lovisa Jordan, also 16, noted 'I think it’s cool... I think that it’s an option that we can get our voices and opinions heard.' This change makes College Park the ninth Maryland city to allow teens to vote, with some teens eager to participate and others, like Lazarus McCarthy, unaware of the impact of the elections on their lives.

key_people: Danny Copeland, Vice President for RST Development
Orlando Velez, Director of Operations and Housing Initiatives
David Varnado, Senior Program and Operations Manager
organizations: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)
locations: College Park–University of Maryland Metro Station
49529.ai_summary: The city of College Park offers affordable housing options for residents, including the Flats at College Park, a 317-unit apartment complex with income-restricted units, and the College Park Community Preservation Trust, which sells homes to buyers who earn less than 140% of the area's median income. According to Danny Copeland, Vice President for RST Development, the Flats at College Park was financed in part through the federal Low-Income Housing Tax Credit program. The city also offers programs like Live+Work College Park, which provides zero-interest loans to full-time employees who purchase a home in the city, as explained by David Varnado, Senior Program and Operations Manager.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Chair of the Restorative Justice Commission
50287.ai_summary: A city-appointed committee, the Restorative Justice Commission, has urged the College Park City Council to address the harm done to the Lakeland neighborhood during urban renewal in the 1970s and 80s by offering financial compensation, housing priority, and free college tuition. As Maxine Gross, the chair of the commission, said, 'When you talk to people who experienced Lakeland before urban renewal, they speak of a vibrant community, a place for caring people who work together to meet everyone’s needs.' The commission's recommendations aim to help restore the community and provide relief to those who were displaced, with Mayor Fazlul Kabir stating that city staff will help identify what action the city can take to meet the community's goals.

51542.ai_summary: College Park officials are considering annexing multiple properties, including the soon-to-be-vacant Beltsville Agricultural Research Center (BARC), to expand the city's boundaries. As Councilmember Jacob Hernandez stated, 'We're kind of just barely in the initial stages of talking about it,' and Mayor Fazlul Kabir emphasized that 'Each parcel's property is unique, and the potentials are also unique,' and that the city would consider annexing land to 'protect it, for economic development or to boost population.' The annexation discussions will require input from residents, property owners, and government officials, with Kabir noting that 'It is a residents-driven process.'

key_people: Teresa Way-Pezzuti, Director of Human Resources
49170.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering a proposal to double the part-time salaries of councilmembers from $10,000 to $20,000 a year. As Councilmember Jacob Hernandez said, 'Everyone on this dais works incredibly hard for the residents that we care for... The responsibility can be awesome, but taxing.' The proposed salary increase, which would cost the city $90,000 a year, aims to help cover the costs for the officials, with Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell noting that 'We do it for the love and passion of being part of the community.'

key_people: Miriam Bader, Director of Planning and Community Development
49106.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to require owners of vacant homes and commercial property to register their empty buildings with the city, with a $25 registration fee and fines for non-compliance. According to College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, 'Vacant properties can negatively impact our community... they can become safety hazards, attract vandalism and contribute to blight.' City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez agreed, stating 'It supports improving public safety... It protects property values.'

key_people: Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
49102.ai_summary: A city-commissioned noise analysis by RK&K found that traffic noise is 'clearly audible in the front yards' on Edmonston Road and warrants a more detailed study and potential noise mitigation by the State Highway Administration. 'I'm not surprised; I've been living it,' said Maria Cordone, a resident who owns one of the homes where the study took place, 'The annoyance of the noise is significant and … it does disturb our quality of life.' College Park City Councilmember Ray Ranker said the city is preparing to plant 80-plus trees and shrubs between Edmonston Road and Kenilworth Avenue to help with sound and beautification.

organizations: Prince George’s County Historic Preservation Commission
49098.ai_summary: Five first-time candidates and five incumbents have filed to run for seats on the College Park City Council in the Nov. 4 election. Mayor Fazlul Kabir will run unopposed for re-election, while districts 1 and 4 will see incumbents running without opposition. The election will determine representation for districts 2 and 3, with a range of candidates vying for available seats.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
49093.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, who is also a volunteer paramedic, disagreed with his colleagues on the use of speed humps as a traffic-calming measure on Autoville Drive, citing his experience with patients in ambulances and the potential for speed bumps to cause pain. Rigg said, 'When you have a long bone fracture, especially like a hip or a pelvis, which happen more often than you think, speed bumps can cause pain.' His expertise in emergency healthcare has influenced his decisions as a city official, including offering advice during the COVID-19 pandemic.

key_people: Michelle Johnson, Executive Director of the Housing Authority of the City of College Park
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: Housing Authority of the City of College Park
U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)
49087.ai_summary: The lack of affordable housing in College Park has led some residents, like Nausicaä Sinclair, to move away due to soaring rent prices. As Michelle Johnson, Executive Director of the Housing Authority of the City of College Park, stated, 'Affordable housing is a human right' and 'To me, affordable housing is being able to go to work, come home, pay your bills, pay your rent, without stress.' College Park City Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters noted that diversity in the community should include access to affordable housing, saying 'No matter how much money you make, you should be able to come in this community and find housing, housing that is suitable and housing that is quality.'

key_people: Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Eric Olson, Prince George's County Councilmember
Tom Dernoga, Prince George's County Councilmember
Robert Thurston, Lakeland Civic Association President
key_issues: shutdown of the Beltsville Agricultural Research Center (BARC)
49021.ai_summary: County Executive Aisha Braveboy met with local officials in College Park as part of her 'Elevate Prince George's' tour, where she heard concerns about the shutdown of the Beltsville Agricultural Research Center (BARC) and issues with land use authority. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'There are 2,600 people who [work at BARC], ... their livelihoods are at stake, but also, more importantly, many of them, when they work there, they also attend the businesses in College Park.' Braveboy stated, 'I'm learning a lot about our county... My goal is to increase our commercial tax base. That's very important, but we want to do that responsibly.'

key_people: Daniel Cruz Jr., Senior Vice President of Cruz Development
Arelis Pérez, Chair of the College Park Housing Authority board
Sandy Lora, Branchville Volunteer Fire Co. Ladies Auxiliary
48941.ai_summary: A Boston-based development company, Cruz Development Corp., has proposed an 87-unit affordable senior housing project at Branchville Road and University Boulevard. The project has raised concerns among College Park residents and officials about affordability, noise, and parking, with Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell stating 'It seems like the rate of rent is still high for seniors on subsidized incomes' and Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters asking what the developers plan to do about noise. The development qualified for a 4% federal tax credit for affordable housing, and tenants will pay based on their incomes, with prices reflecting 60% of the area median income for Prince George's County.

key_people: Amira Abujuma, deputy student liaison to the College Park City Council
48774.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is urging legislation against rent price-setting after a request by Councilmember John Rigg to send a letter to state legislators supporting action against RealPage and other software companies. Amira Abujuma, deputy student liaison to the College Park City Council, stated that 'Housing in College Park is not a luxury. It’s a necessity' and emphasized that the use of RealPage is 'devastating … for the thousands of students who call College Park home.' The council agreed to send the letter and discuss potential further action at a future meeting, with Abujuma noting that the city has the authority to ban the software, citing other municipalities such as San Francisco, Seattle, and Philadelphia that have passed ordinances to prohibit landlords from using algorithmic rent-setting software like RealPage.

48748.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering stricter parking enforcement measures, including reducing the number of free permits per household and charging residents to park on their streets. Councilmembers discussed the issue, with Susan Whitney saying 'We want people who need their cars to be able to park them, and we want people who can get by with one car, we want to encourage them to do that.' Maria Mackie noted that 'Nobody likes permit parking,' but emphasized that 'We need to realize this is a quality-of-life issue. It’s a safety issue. If we don’t do something about that, it’s bad on us.' The council plans to further discuss the issue in a future worksession.

key_people: Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
48745.ai_summary: Three College Park City Council members, Llatetra Brown Esters, Susan Whitney, and John Rigg, have announced they will not seek re-election in November, citing increasing family and work obligations. As Rigg stated, 'I want to place a much higher priority on … family events, see my friends and neighbors more often and spend time on personal pursuits. I have determined that running for council every other autumn is a trade-off that I am no longer willing to accept.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir noted that their departure will be a loss for the council, but also an opportunity for new residents to run, as 'If the same people keep running, those who are interested, they don’t get a chance. It’s very hard to run against incumbents.'

key_people: Ryna Quiñones, Communications and Events Manager
August Kenner, University of Maryland Police Captain
Rex Powell, College Park Woods Neighborhood Watch Coordinator
Alexis Howlett, Catholic University Graduate Student
48698.ai_summary: The city of College Park held a National Night Out event on August 5, where residents met with law enforcement officers and city officials to focus on public safety and community building. According to College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, 'The whole goal of National Night Out is to get the community connected with the law enforcement who try to keep us safe.' Residents, including Ginger Whitaker, appreciated the chance to build stronger bonds with their community, stating 'It just makes you feel safer to know who’s actually working down there at the fire station, or who’s sitting in the police car around the corner.'

key_people: Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
48651.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmembers Llatetra Brown Esters and Susan Whitney, both representing District 2, announced they will not run for re-election in November due to increasing demands on their time from their full-time jobs and families. Esters stated, 'My need to be present for family has become a priority,' and Whitney wrote, 'My work and family responsibilities have increased substantially since the last election, and I no longer have the time and energy to provide the level of service District 2 residents deserve.' This announcement, along with Councilmember John Rigg's similar decision, affects the upcoming elections for all eight city council seats and the position of mayor.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Stuart Adams, former College Park City Councilmember
Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland student
48611.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember John Rigg announced he will not seek re-election in November, citing a desire to prioritize family events and personal pursuits. Rigg, who has served for eight years, stated 'I want to place a much higher priority on … family events, see my friends and neighbors more often and spend time on personal pursuits. I have determined that running for council every other autumn is a trade-off that I am no longer willing to accept.' He endorsed his colleague Ray Ranker for re-election and urged the newly elected council to consider switching to four-year terms for elected officials, a change that Rigg believes would 'continue a common-sense approach to alleviating the substantial personal burden imposed by our current system of elections every other year.'

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Armando Camacho, Vice Chair of the Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners
Daniel Oates, Calvert Hills Citizens Association President
organizations: Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners
locations: Calvert Hills Playground and Athletic Field
University of Maryland Child Development Center
key_issues: Location and Proximity to Schools and Parks
48606.ai_summary: The Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners voted 4-0 to deny a liquor store's application to reopen next to ZIPS Cleaners on Baltimore Avenue, citing its proximity to the University of Maryland Child Development Center. College Park City Councilmember John Rigg stated, “This is unequivocally the wrong location, and the city council stands in unified opposition to it.” Mayor Fazlul Kabir testified that the community has voiced strong opposition to the introduction of a full-service liquor store so close to the Calvert Hills playground and the University of Maryland Child Development Center, saying “This is a wonderful community, a historic community that’s very well established, very family-oriented.”

48293.ai_summary: Former City Councilmember Bob Catlin remains civically involved in College Park, attending council meetings and sharing historical reflections. Catlin, who served from 1997 to 2013, said 'I've always been interested in history and policies and why things work the way they do. I think you need to understand that in order to make your decisions in the future.' He currently serves on several committees and is co-president of the Berwyn District Civic Association, and his continued involvement is appreciated by Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Julie Johnson, UMD Assistant Director for Intramural Sports and the Reckord Armory
Ryna Quiñones, City Communications and Events Manager
Cat Peretti, University Park Resident and Volleyball Team Captain
48292.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has partnered with the University of Maryland's RecWell program to offer social sports, including volleyball and pickleball, to city residents at a discounted rate. 'Anything that gets people out and moving their bodies is a good thing,' said College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, who plays on the program's volleyball team, adding that team sports are a great way to build community. The city allocated $30,000 to offer 50% discounts to residents, and the program aims to encourage social interaction and community building among participants, with 'It's not about the competition as much as it's just about getting out there and having fun,' said Cat Peretti, a University Park resident and volleyball team captain.

key_people: Donald Hay, Co-chair of the College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee
organizations: College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee
48291.ai_summary: The College Park City Council will hold a public hearing on August 6 to discuss a proposal to outlaw stopping in bike lanes, which would prevent delivery and supply trucks from unloading in bike lanes on city-owned streets. According to College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, the rule would be a 'little inconvenient' for delivery drivers, but Councilmember Susan Whitney says 'If we can't do this here, why can't we do this here? I see no reason why we can't accomplish that in College Park.' The proposed law would not affect Route 1, which is owned and maintained by the state, but would impact roads like Rhode Island Avenue.

48229.ai_summary: The U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) identified College Park and Hyattsville as 'sanctuary jurisdictions' that are 'obstructing the enforcement of U.S. immigration laws.' College Park officials have stated that 'the news of this designation … is surprising' and that the city 'has and will continue to be a welcoming and inclusive community.' The city has not received any formal notification from DHS about its inclusion on the list and 'remains steadfast in our commitment to the safety and well-being of all our residents.'

key_people: Nicole Freeh, Highway Engineer and Yarrow Resident
47367.ai_summary: Residents of the Yarrow neighborhood are asking the city to plant noise-absorbing trees between Edmonston Road and Kenilworth Avenue to alleviate noise pollution. As Maria Cordone, a resident, stated 'I can’t hear my neighbor talking to me' and 'I can’t even sit out in the yard because of the noise.' The residents plan to meet with the city arborist to discuss the tree planting and also seek a sound study from the state to explore other solutions, such as a barrier wall or speed enforcement.

key_people: Kathryn Clay, PNC Senior Vice President
Qin Chih, Jumbo Jumbo Cafe Manager
47353.ai_summary: The city of College Park awarded $75,000 in grants to 15 local businesses through the Retail Reinforcement Mini-Grant Program, funded by PNC Bank, to help offset losses from the ongoing Purple Line construction. As PNC Senior Vice President Kathryn Clay stated, 'Supporting small businesses is a priority focus for PNC, so this opportunity was a great fit.' The grants helped businesses cover rent, equipment, payroll, and lease payments, with recipients like Jumbo Jumbo Cafe and Laser Essential experiencing significant losses due to reduced foot and vehicle traffic.

key_people: Ethan Pham, Founder of CP Flea
Gabrielle Brinkhoff, Store Manager of Shop Made in Maryland
47336.ai_summary: City officials shut down a flea market on College Park's City Hall plaza in March after it had operated for nearly a year without permission. 'From the city's point of view, it's a pretty simple issue for us: They were operating on the City Hall plaza without permission,' said City Manager Kenny Young. The shutdown has prompted the market's founder, Ethan Pham, to plead his case at a College Park City Council meeting and collect 320 signatures from backers on a petition, saying 'It just felt completely out of left field, like, I had no idea this was coming.'

key_people: Maxine Gross, chair of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project
Mary Anne Hakes, organizer of Explorations on Aging
47289.ai_summary: University of Maryland students have been working on senior capstone projects to preserve the history of College Park's Lakeland community and help city-based organizations. As Maxine Gross, chair of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project, said, 'It is helpful for us to see what [the students are] thinking about, and … it’s helpful for them to see how the things that they learned about in a classroom actually work with real-life projects.' The projects benefit both the students and the community organizations they support, with students creating digital archives, websites, and environmental plans for organizations like the Lakeland Community Heritage Project and Explorations on Aging.

47278.ai_summary: The College Park City Council will hold a public hearing on May 6 to discuss the city's proposed $29.6 million fiscal year 2026 budget, which would increase the budget by 0.9% over the previous year. The proposal would add three full-time staff members to City Hall but would not raise residential property taxes. The council must adopt a budget resolution later this month.

key_people: Dhruvak Mirani, University of Maryland Student Government Association President and student liaison to the College Park City Council
Dhruvak Mirani, student member of the University System of Maryland’s Board of Regents
organizations: University System of Maryland’s Board of Regents
47268.ai_summary: Dhruvak Mirani, the 2023-24 University of Maryland student liaison to the College Park City Council, has been elected as the president of the school’s Student Government Association. Mirani, a government and politics major, helped create a pilot project for the city to give subsidies to student renters and also serves as a student member of the University System of Maryland’s Board of Regents. The election drew 6,000 undergraduate voters, more than any year since 2020, according to The Diamondback, the school’s student newspaper.

key_people: Ryna Quiñones, City Communications and Events Manager
47186.ai_summary: The 27th annual Maryland Day was held at the University of Maryland, with city officials and staff participating by handing out information about College Park. As Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'It's building community, bringing people together in one place and showcasing how strong we are as a community.' The event featured over 400 free activities and aimed to build connections between the university and the surrounding community, with attendees like Molly Rubinstein, a 1975 UMD graduate, appreciating the opportunity to see what the university has to offer.

key_people: Daniel Gruner, Professor of Entomology at the University of Maryland
Karin Burghardt, Associate Professor of Entomology and Extension Specialist
Laura Rost, National Coordinator of Bee City USA
46919.ai_summary: The City of College Park has waived the usual 12-inch limit for grass height in April to support the No Mow April initiative, allowing native pollinators like bees and butterflies to feed and find habitats. As Daniel Gruner, a professor of entomology at the University of Maryland, said, 'Pollinators need floral resources in order to get the energy they need to build their broods up, especially early in the season.' Karin Burghardt, an associate professor of entomology, endorsed the move, stating that 'No Mow April is a great communication tool to connect in folks’ minds that their management strategies in their yards have consequences for insects.'

organizations: Meals on Wheels of College Park
46928.ai_summary: County Councilmembers Thomas Dernoga and Eric Olson warned that the coming budget cycle will be “extremely difficult” due to uncertainty surrounding federal grants and budget cuts. As Dernoga noted, “It’s not going to be a fun year, we are going to be trying to prioritize,” and funding for food banks like Meals on Wheels of College Park could take a hit. The city's draft budget proposal totals $29.6 million, and Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated that the city is not proposing a property tax increase and will add four full-time staff members, including a public safety manager.

key_people: Ryna Quiñones, City Communications and Events Manager
Robert Thurston, Lakeland Civic Association President
Daniel Oates, Calvert Hills Citizens Association President
46925.ai_summary: The City of College Park has started a text alert system to keep residents informed about events, news, and emergencies. According to Ryna Quiñones, the city's communications and events manager, 'We're always looking for new ways to reach our residents,' and using texts will help the city communicate more effectively. The system, provided by Civic Plus Mass Communications, allows residents to sign up for news and events by texting COLLEGEPARK to 38276, and for emergency alerts by texting CPALERTS to the same number, with the potential to reach everyone in the city, as noted by Daniel Oates, president of the Calvert Hills Citizens Association.

46869.ai_summary: City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez has been helping Spanish-speaking business owners in North College Park, including Fidel Alfaro, owner of Alfaro Barbershop, by providing them with information about the city in Spanish. Hernandez, who has served on the council since 2023, guided Alfaro through the application process for College Park's economic development grants, resulting in $14,000 for renovations to the shop. As Hernandez stated, his experience of helping his parents navigate society as non-native English speakers has influenced his work on the city council, where he advocates for sharing city information in Spanish, saying 'For me, one of the difficult things that I, like many others … experienced is walking your parents through every single step, whether that is interpreting at a parent-teacher’s conference or having a conversation with your parents about important documents that they need to sign.'

key_people: Jatinder Khokhar, Director of Public Services
46866.ai_summary: College Park’s city manager has proposed a $29.6 million budget for fiscal year 2026, which would not raise property taxes and would add three full-time staff members to city hall. The proposal includes an 8.5% increase over last year for employee salaries and benefits. As Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'Increasing property taxes has never been popular,' and 'this year, thankfully, it’s not happening.' The budget proposal will be reviewed by the City Council and a public hearing will be held on May 6.

46822.ai_summary: City officials in College Park are preparing for the economic fallout from recent federal layoffs, which could reduce customer traffic at local businesses and impact federal grants for city projects. 'We are not really prepared for it,' said Mayor Fazlul Kabir, adding 'We’re really worried about their well being' of the affected federal employees. The city is still assessing the potential impact and considering options to help displaced federal employees, with Councilmember John Rigg stating 'It’s going to be a really tough time over the next couple of years in College Park'.

46529.ai_summary: Ray Ranker, the new District 3 councilmember, took the oath of office on March 18 after winning the special election with 344 out of 512 votes. Ranker stated, 'There are just a lot of amazing people who are, in so many different ways, making our community stronger, more connected, better. I’m excited to build on that.' He plans to dive into city budget planning and reach out to his opponents, Michael Meadow and Gannon Sprinkle, to 'help move College Park forward together.'

46499.ai_summary: College Park officials and community members gathered at Duvall Field for a groundbreaking ceremony to kickstart the renovation of the field into a seven-acre park for sports, entertainment and community events. The project, which includes over a dozen amenities such as an amphitheater and sports courts, was proposed in 2005 but delayed due to inadequate funds and other issues, with Councilmember Alan Hew stating 'It is expensive to make these things happen' and 'When we thought we were near the end point, stormwater management issues came up that we needed to address, and that set us back both in cost and in time.' The ceremony signaled the second phase of the renovation, which officials said will finish by 2026.

key_people: Michael Harris, University of Maryland student
46411.ai_summary: The University Club at College Park, a 60-year-old student housing complex, will undergo a major renovation starting this summer after being purchased by Cape Advisers for $10.5 million. The renovation will include upgrades to the building's water and electrical systems, as well as the addition of outdoor amenities, but may result in higher rents for tenants, a concern for City Councilmember Susan Whitney, who notes that 'There is a problem with a lack of affordable housing in general in the city, but also specifically for students.' The buyer, Matt Hurson, says the repairs will be extensive and require the building to be empty, with tenants facing potential rent increases upon their return.

key_people: Ray Ranker, UMD Chaplain and District 3 Councilmember
Michael Meadow, Lifelong Old Town Resident
46379.ai_summary: Ray Ranker, a University of Maryland chaplain, won the special election for the District 3 College Park City Council seat with 338 votes. Ranker stated, 'I’m grateful that people entrusted me with this responsibility' and plans to dive into city budget planning during his term, saying 'I can really advocate for the kinds of changes that folks in [District 3] want to see reflected in the annual budget that’s coming up.' He will take the oath of office on March 18 before the regular city council meeting, filling the seat left vacant by former Councilmember Stuart Adams.

key_people: Gannon Sprinkle, co-founder of the College Park Tenants Union and former deputy student liaison to the city council
Dhruvak Mirani, co-founder of the tenants union and the 2023-24 student liaison to the city council
organizations: White House Council of Economic Advisers
46407.ai_summary: The College Park Tenants Union is urging the city council to investigate price-fixing allegations involving six large apartment complexes and the use of RealPage, a software company that offers price-setting features based on algorithms. According to Gannon Sprinkle, a co-founder of the union, 'Local involvement is necessary to protect tenants from predatory pricing, and we would urge the city council to investigate the nature of RealPage.' Maryland Delegate Julie Palakovich Carr expressed concern that renters were paying more in rent to fuel their landlords' profits, saying 'It was pretty troubling to me that renters were paying more in rent, really for no reason other than to fuel their landlords' profits.' Proposed legislation in the Maryland House of Delegates (House Bill 817) and the state Senate (Senate Bill 0609) would prevent the use of algorithmic pricing by landlords in the state.

key_people: Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
46382.ai_summary: Li Chun Café, owned by Hua Wang, has opened on the first floor of College Park City Hall, with a ribbon-cutting ceremony attended by College Park City Councilmember Maria Mackie and other community members. Mackie said of the café, 'It’s really a … wonderful addition and a really unique place,' and expressed her excitement. The café features meals from northeastern China and has been popular with customers, who 'like the vibes, and they like being here to enjoy healthy food and teas in between work and studies,' according to Xue Ling, the café's brand manager.

key_people: Denise Mitchell, Mayor Pro Tem and College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Anna Owens, Former Mayor of College Park
P.J. Brennan, Former College Park City Councilmember
46437.ai_summary: Denise Mitchell, Mayor Pro Tem and College Park City Councilmember, reflected on her political journey, stating 'I never would have thought I would be an elected official' and 'This is still a man’s world in regard to politics.' Mitchell, along with fellow councilmembers Maria Mackie, Susan Whitney, and Llatetra Brown Esters, agree that there is room for more female leaders in government. Mackie noted that 'women are really coming up to the plate' and Whitney said 'some people might be more skeptical of your abilities, perhaps because you’re a woman.'

46342.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has proposed an amendment to the city code that would require residents to store tables used for drinking games, such as beer pong, in areas not visible from the street. Councilmember John Rigg stated, 'One thing we’ve always struggled with, one very specific piece of furniture, and that is a beer pong table... That has been very problematic.' Councilmember Jacob Hernandez questioned the proposal, stating 'It is a targeted policy to curb [the] behavior of a particular group of people.'

key_people: Mary Anne Hakes, Resident and City Explorations on Aging Project Worker
46348.ai_summary: The College Park Woods Clubhouse opened to the public with a ribbon-cutting ceremony on March 6, after about a year of construction. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'This is a really, really wonderful time for us to celebrate. This is your second home. Please enjoy it.' The new community space, which will be used as a multi-purpose gathering place, fulfills part of the city’s strategic plan to bring College Park together, according to Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell, who said 'Having this is important, because engagement means inclusivity. And that means people can feel like they belong together.'

key_people: Sherry Felix, English Teacher and Title I Coordinator
46388.ai_summary: The College Park City Council granted $2,750 to High Point High School's Flying from the HP Nest program, which provides care packages for college-bound students, including blankets, bed linens, and Chromebooks. According to Sherry Felix, an English teacher and the school's Title I coordinator, 'The bulk of our work now is creating these college-bound care packages' and the program aims to help the school's 3,100 students graduate, with Felix saying 'we know that there's an economic need, there's a socioeconomic need for our students'. The grant also pays for registration fees for 42 students to join the nonprofit First Generation College Bound, which aims to increase the number of low-income students who graduate from high school and move on to college.

key_people: Erica Otte, College Park City Council Student Liaison
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Chris Hatcher, Attorney for Landmark Properties
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember (District 1)
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember (District 2)
46331.ai_summary: The College Park City Council expressed concerns about a plan to demolish the 63-year-old College Park Towers and replace it with two luxury high-rise apartment buildings, citing the lack of affordable housing in the area. Councilmembers, including Erica Otte, who said 'Luxury apartments are replacing affordable housing,' and Alan Hew, who stated 'One thing I will always ask is that there be a percentage of affordable housing built into this,' argued for the inclusion of affordable units in the new development. The council's push for affordable housing is driven by concerns that students, who are the primary residents of College Park Towers, are being priced out of the area, with Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell saying 'We need to be more collective in letting students find housing that is affordable and that they can afford, not what you’re trying to sell.'

46336.ai_summary: A special election will be held on March 11 to temporarily fill the vacant District 3 College Park City Council seat. The city council has approved the purchase of three drop boxes for voting at a cost of $9,481.25. Early, in-person voting will take place on Sunday, Nov. 1, at Davis Hall, and voters may also mail their ballots or place them in drop boxes at various locations.

key_people: Ellen Tappin, member of Fight Colorectal Cancer
Simran Mulchandani, University of Maryland student
McKayla Guthrie, University of Maryland student
46287.ai_summary: The City of College Park hosted an event on March 9 to raise awareness about colon cancer, in partnership with Fight Colorectal Cancer. Councilmember Alan Hew said, 'Something about health and personal issues like this aren’t very well versed in minority communities, so we’re trying to build opportunities for them to learn about it, speak about it, without social pressures and things like that.' The event aimed to educate residents on the importance of getting screened, with Ellen Tappin, a colon cancer survivor, stating, 'I’m hoping that people coming into the event today will become aware that colorectal cancer is the No. 2 cause of cancer deaths in the nation.'

46182.ai_summary: The three candidates for the vacant District 3 City Council seat, Michael Meadow, Ray Ranker, and Gannon Sprinkle, suggested that the city could offer financial assistance, job search resources, and referrals to mental health agencies to help federal workers who have lost their jobs. The candidates also agreed to oppose any effort to dismantle the city's diversity, equity and inclusion programs, with Meadow stating 'Absolutely do not scrap' DEI programs, and Ranker saying he would 'like to have it both ways: continue doing [DEI] while still retaining federal dollars and support.' The candidates discussed various new projects they would like the city to take on, including tax credits for homeowners and eco-friendly bike and walking paths.

46022.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now will host a forum for the three candidates for the vacant District 3 City Council seat on Wednesday, Feb. 26. The forum will feature Mike Meadow, Ray Ranker, and Gannon Sprinkle, and will be moderated by Shawn Anderson of WTOP radio. The event will take place at City Hall and is open to the public, with the option to attend in person or join via Zoom.

key_people: Ben Barnes, Maryland Delegate (District 21) and House Appropriations Committee Chair
45972.ai_summary: Maryland Gov. Wes Moore's proposed $67.3 billion budget for fiscal year 2026 includes funding to start planning for improvements to Route 1 from Greenbelt Road to the Beltway. As state Sen. Jim Rosapepe noted, 'Route 1 is the front door to the University of Maryland; it’s our flagship university and we want people, when they come, to feel good about the community.' College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir agreed, stating 'Route 1 is our Main Street, plain and simple,' and the upcoming construction will prioritize community input, with potential upgrades including bicycle lanes, raised medians, and improved sidewalks, aiming to make the highway safer for drivers, cyclists, and pedestrians.

key_people: Michael Jacobs, Former College Park Mayor
Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember
Andrew Fellows, Former College Park Mayor
45962.ai_summary: Former College Park Mayor Michael Jacobs died on Jan. 13 at the age of 81. He served as an elected official in the city for two dozen years and played a significant role in establishing relations with the University of Maryland. As mayor, Jacobs was open to talking with residents one-on-one, as recalled by Kathy Bryant, who said, 'He was never too busy to talk to me, or I’m sure, to anybody else who wanted to talk to him.'

key_people: Stefan Leggin, former Brentwood Vice Mayor
organizations: Coalition to Support Prince George’s County Fire and EMS
45951.ai_summary: The Coalition to Support Prince George’s County Fire and EMS has asked candidates running for Prince George’s County executive to support hiring 150 new firefighters and emergency medical service staff in fiscal year 2026. As Stefan Leggin, former Brentwood Vice Mayor, stated, 'We can use this election as leverage to elevate the need for meaningful change, to elevate the conversation' and 'We take it to the top. We want them to know that staffing our fire departments needs to be a top priority.' College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir has expressed concern over increased response times since career firefighters were reallocated in June, which has prompted the coalition to push for reforms in the county’s fire and EMS department.

organizations: Food and Drug Administration’s Center for Food Safety and Applied Nutrition
45903.ai_summary: College Park residents, including federal employees and documented migrants, are feeling the impact of President Donald Trump's executive orders, with one resident stating they are 'shaking like a leaf every day'. Mayor Fazlul Kabir said 'The morale of the community is very low' and acknowledged that 'there are so many jobs of federal employees at stake'. The city has published links to resources and issued a proclamation called 'One College Park' to support the community and reject 'attitudes of fear, pessimism or cynicism'.

45978.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has postponed voting on a proposed charter amendment to lower the voting age for city elections to 16 until after the vacant District 3 council seat is filled on March 11. A couple of residents spoke against the change, while representatives of Vote16 spoke in favor of it. The council also postponed a vote on a second proposed charter amendment that would allow the mayor and council to appoint a replacement council member if a seat is vacated less than a year before the next election.

45975.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is forming two citizen task forces, one for childcare and one for affordable housing. The Childcare Task Force will monitor state legislation and scout out federal, state, county and non-profit programs, while the Affordable Housing Task Force will research and share data about the city’s housing needs. Members of both committees will be appointed by the mayor and council.

key_people: Janeen Miller, Program Lead for Age Friendly College Park
Chyna Diaz, Director of Home Preservation for Habitat for Humanity Metro Maryland
45424.ai_summary: The College Park Aging in Place program, part of a nationwide Habitat for Humanity effort, provides free wheelchair ramps and grab bars to senior citizens' homes, as well as repairs to roofs, drywall, and porches. According to Janeen Miller, Program Lead for Age Friendly College Park, 'It's the home repair and improvements that we can help seniors with that will allow them to stay in their homes as they age.' The program aims to work on at least 10 homes a year and is currently funded by American Rescue Plan Act funding, with the hope that the College Park City Council will take over funding once ARPA funds are depleted.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Robert Day, Former College Park City Councilmember
45574.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams has resigned from his position, citing the demands of his job and family responsibilities, saying 'I'd rather worry about Facetiming my kids.' Adams represented District 3 and led conversations on vacant and blighted properties, as well as a pilot program to subsidize University of Maryland students' rents. The city will hold a special election on March 11 to fill Adams' seat, with two candidates already announced to run, as Mayor Fazlul Kabir noted 'It's a loss for us,' and Councilmember John Rigg stated 'Stuart brought a lot of creative and extremely helpful ideas to council.'

45420.ai_summary: Two candidates, Michael Meadow and Ray Ranker, have announced they will run for the District 3 College Park City Council seat vacated by the resignation of Councilmember Stuart Adams. Meadow, a lifelong resident of College Park, said he would focus on helping the city 'grow and prosper, but at the same time I want to take care of the small business owners.' Ranker, who is UMD's Lutheran chaplain, stated he wants to 'make sure that those whose primary language is not English, that they're not left behind.'

key_people: Alan Bradford, Retired Heavy Equipment Operator and Volunteer
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Joseph Perry, Son of Jack Perry
Martin Klapac, College Park Resident and Longtime Friend of Alan Bradford
organizations: College Park Boys and Girls Club
45413.ai_summary: The College Park City Council presented Alan Bradford with the 2024 Jack Perry Award, recognizing his 42 years of volunteer service to the community. As Mayor Fazlul Kabir wrote, 'Alan’s countless acts of service … have fostered a spirit of community and improved the quality of life for residents, embodying the legacy of Jack Perry himself.' Bradford's volunteer work has included coaching softball, managing the College Park Boys and Girls Club, and serving on the College Park Recreation Board, making him a beloved figure in College Park.

organizations: Department of Housing and Urban Development
45409.ai_summary: Attick Towers, a 108-unit apartment building for tenants ages 60 and up, is starting its third year of a renovation project that could last up to five more years. The repairs, which have included replacing heating and air conditioning units and modernizing elevators and piping, are expected to cost up to $16 million. As Councilmember Susan Whitney noted, 'It’s a building that’s, you know, about 50 years old, and there’s a lot of deferred maintenance,' and Michelle Johnson, the Housing Authority’s director, is continuing to apply for grants to keep the project running smoothly.

45598.ai_summary: The College Park City Council will hold a public hearing on a proposal to change the minimum legal voting age in College Park from 18 to 16. Councilmembers who have supported the amendment to the city charter have said that teenage voters tend to grow up to be civically engaged adults. If the amendment passes, College Park residents who register to vote in Prince George’s County and will be at least 16 on Election Day will automatically become registered to vote in the city.

45174.ai_summary: City Councilmember Stuart Adams announced his resignation effective January 8 due to family commitments, stating 'As a young father, my availability as a city councilmember has become more constrained.' Adams asked the mayor, councilmembers, and residents to 'continue to push' projects he has championed, including the Calvert Hills stormwater drainage project and affordable childcare. A special election will be held within 65 days to fill Adams' seat, which will be held until the next general election in November.

key_people: David Mitchell, University of Maryland Police Chief
Lt. Rosanne Hoaas, University of Maryland Police Department
Stephen Boehm, Deputy Chief of the Metro Transit Police
Yasmin Brown, Maryland-National Capital Park Police Assistant Chief
45050.ai_summary: The University of Maryland Police Department has been stopping electric scooters for traffic violations, including failure to stop at stop signs and riding on sidewalks. As Maj. James Keleti of Hyattsville Division 1 noted, 'We do stop a lot of scooters,' and University of Maryland Police Chief David Mitchell added, 'We have charged people for intoxication on a scooter. We will continue to do that.' The police departments are facing challenges in pulling over scooter riders, with Lt. Rosanne Hoaas stating, 'I have to weigh out, you know, is it worth me going into opposing traffic to try to stop that e-scooter, who’s probably now already half a mile down the road?'

key_people: Lisa Eally, Meals on Wheels of College Park Chair
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: Meals on Wheels of College Park
45028.ai_summary: Meals on Wheels of College Park is not scheduled to return to the city anytime soon due to the lack of a suitable location, despite the city council's efforts to support the organization. As Lisa Eally, the local chapter's chair, stated, 'We're doing whatever we can to find a location back in College Park.' The city council has allocated $400,000 out of its general funds for Meals on Wheels to use if it moves during fiscal year 2025, and Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that the council has 'put emphasis [and] substantial dollars' into supporting nonprofit food services.

45025.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously voted to spend $7,354,000 to renovate Duvall Field, a project that has been delayed for 19 years due to funding issues. As Councilmember Alan Hew stated, 'Making greatness in our city costs, and the longer we wait, the more it’s going to cost.' Councilmembers agreed that delaying the project further would only increase costs, with Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters saying, 'The longer we wait, it is going to cost that much more money, and what I would hate to see is for us to vote against this, and then it not happen.'

key_people: Michael Williams, College Park Director of Economic Development
44853.ai_summary: A Ross Dress For Less discount store has opened in North College Park, bringing 70 to 110 new jobs to the area. College Park Councilmember Jacob Hernandez said the store will be a major economic driver, providing high-quality clothing and merchandise at affordable prices. As College Park resident Mona Lynch noted, having a Ross in the city was 'in order', and the new store will help to revitalize the shopping plaza.

key_people: Keith Hooks, local representative of the National Association of Letter Carriers
Nancy Harris, resident of College Park Woods
44850.ai_summary: Some North College Park residents are complaining about mail not being delivered every day, with issues including letters, bills, and checks not being received. Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell said 'There’s maybe two or three days that a resident will not get mail at all,' and 'There’s some days where the mail will come, but it will be for another address.' Keith Hooks, a local representative of the National Association of Letter Carriers, stated that the post office has had an 'onslaught' of new, inexperienced employees, and that union leaders want to shift the perspective of mail carriers back to 'that our communities come first, that our customer base comes first.'

key_people: Yasmin Brown, Assistant Chief of the Maryland-National Capital Park Police
Brian Wiesemann, Commander of the Maryland State Police College Park Barracks
Jim Keleti, Commander of the Prince George’s County Police Hyattsville Division 1
Stephen Boehm, Deputy Chief of the Metro Transit Police
44814.ai_summary: Theft is the most common crime in College Park, with thefts from automobiles and thefts of automobiles being most prevalent, according to law enforcement officials. As Yasmin Brown, assistant chief of the Maryland-National Capital Park Police, noted, 'Most of our incidents are theft-related... A lot of that is a crime of opportunity: people leaving something in their car or leaving their car unlocked.' Overall, crime in College Park is down by 11%, with property crime like theft from autos declining by 15%, but violent crime is up by 6%.

key_people: Stephanie Stullich, former College Park city councilmember
organizations: City of College Park animal control
44810.ai_summary: Stephanie Stullich, a former College Park city councilmember, has been fostering kittens and finding them forever homes for four years, with the help of the city's animal welfare officer and the nonprofit Beltsville Community Cats. Stullich is one of about half a dozen volunteers who take in homeless kittens until they can be adopted. As Patricia Coil, another foster volunteer, said, 'You bring them in, and you never know what you're going to get, they each develop their own personality. It's just cute to watch them … become cats over the course of, like, two or three months.'

key_people: William Pines, Maryland State Highway Administrator
Charlie Gischlar, State Highway Administration Deputy Director
44790.ai_summary: Maryland Senator Jim Rosapepe called for swift planning and completion of the U.S. Route 1 Improvement Project during a ribbon-cutting ceremony for the end of Phase 1 in College Park, saying 'We're hoping that we can get the planning [for the rest of the project] in this coming year's budget.' The first phase, which cost $56.9 million, added bicycle lanes, raised medians, expanded lanes, and upgraded drainage between College Avenue and University Boulevard. The project's completion is a significant step towards improving road safety in College Park, with future phases set to extend to I-495.

key_people: Joel Biermann, Former University Park Mayor
Patrick Wojahn, Former College Park Mayor
Debi Sandlin, University Park Town Administrator
Aisha Braveboy, Prince George’s County State’s Attorney
44763.ai_summary: Former University Park Mayor Joel Biermann was arrested on 28 counts relating to child sexual abuse materials, with investigators from the FBI finding over 45,000 files of suspected child sexual abuse materials. He was denied bond and is currently in custody. This arrest comes about 20 months after former College Park Mayor Patrick Wojahn was arrested on 56 counts of child pornography, and serves as a reminder of the importance of law enforcement investigation in ensuring public safety, with current College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir declining to comment on Biermann's charges.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Hua Wang, Owner of Northwest Chinese Food
Xue Ling, Brand Manager of Northwest Chinese Food
Ashfiha Rahman, University of Maryland Student
Flavyne Tsongwain, University of Maryland Student
44760.ai_summary: Northwest Chinese Food on Baltimore Avenue was proclaimed College Park's 2024 Business of the Year by Mayor Fazlul Kabir, citing its unique cuisine and commitment to engaging with and investing in the community. As Kabir said, 'Their efforts have made College Park a more vibrant and diverse place to live.' The restaurant will eventually close its current location to make room for new student apartments and will move to the city hall building, where it will reopen as Li Chun Cafe in February.

key_people: Josanne Francis, UMD's Lead Community Engagement Program Coordinator
Trevor Muñoz, Director of UMD's Maryland Institute for Technology in the Humanities
Xiomara Bradden, UMD Junior Government and Politics Major
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC)
44704.ai_summary: The 13th annual Good Neighbor Day in College Park drew hundreds of volunteers who worked on various projects, including raking leaves, picking up trash, and painting a sewer drain.
