As volunteer Kaelan Wilfred said, 'We're here helping out, grabbing some weeds, playing in some trees, getting real, in touch with nature.' The event, sponsored by the City of College Park, UMD, and M-NCPPC, aimed to enhance community space and cater to the needs of the city and its environment, with projects designed to be educational and doable within 3.5 hours, as noted by Josanne Francis, UMD's lead community engagement program coordinator.

44571.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering installing stop sign cameras around the city after a three-week pilot program showed that up to 92% of drivers did not come to a full stop at equipped signs. As Councilmember Susan Whitney said, 'I just wanted to make sure there was an opportunity for public comment and really frame this in a clear and public way.' The goal of the program is safety, not profit, with Councilmember Maria Mackie stating, 'Our motivation is safety,' and the city planning to spend revenue from the program on traffic safety efforts.

key_people: Raymond Rodriguez, University of Maryland’s School of Public Policy Moderator
organizations: University of Maryland’s School of Public Policy
44540.ai_summary: College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir emphasized the importance of resident engagement in earning the trust of the community, stating 'The key to earning the trust of the community, is engaging residents' and 'You are doing it to serve the people. You are no one but the public servant.' Kabir shared his approach to transparency and community connection at a 'Living Democracy' symposium on the University of Maryland campus, alongside Galena Mayor John Carroll. The mayors discussed the need for local government officials to prioritize community issues over party affiliations, with Carroll noting 'You’re not a D, you’re not an R, you’re not an anything. You’re a citizen and a resident in your community.'

key_people: Andreé Sahakian, senior development manager at LV Collective
organizations: College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
44458.ai_summary: A Texas-based development company, LV Collective, is planning to build a mixed-use housing complex, referred to as Project Turtle, at the site of the former Campus Village Shoppes along Route 1 in College Park. The development will add 299 units of privately-owned student housing and include retail space, a community space for the Lakeland neighborhood, and a parking garage. As Andreé Sahakian, senior development manager at LV Collective, stated, 'With each development, we look for creative ways to collaborate with the community, embracing its history and honoring its character and culture,' and the project aims to bring value and vibrancy to the neighborhood, while also addressing community concerns about the closure of the former strip mall, with City Councilmember Susan Whitney noting that the city is 'really, really trying to keep a lot of those beloved businesses here.'

key_people: Sydney Nicole, University of Maryland student
Michael Williams, Director of Economic Development
44465.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved a $130,000 grant for Rally House, a sports apparel store, to move into the former Target storefront on Baltimore Avenue. 'I think it'll be nice,' said Sydney Nicole, a University of Maryland student, 'A lot of people around this area, they wear athleisure, Lululemon, Nike, Adidas; all that's very popular around College Park, especially with college students. So I think it'll be really good for business.' The store is expected to hire up to 70 employees and will be the chain's first Maryland location, chosen because College Park is home to a Big 10 sports program.

key_people: Carlos Johnson, Hollywood Elementary School’s principal
Lindsay Sullivan, Amplify’s associate vice president
44455.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved $40,000 for a tutoring program for Hollywood Elementary School’s Spanish-speaking students. The program, provided by Amplify Tutoring, offers reading tutoring to 24 students in third to fifth grades and aims to equip them with the literacy skills they need for middle school. As Councilmember Jacob Hernandez said, 'If I could, I would try to carve [the] entirety of our budget to try and help the schools that we have here in College Park,' and Councilmember Maria Mackie noted she was 'very impressed with how [Amplify does] their tutoring and their success that they had [at Hollywood Elementary] in just four short months of last year, that they brought the students’ reading ability up.'

44445.ai_summary: Kit Slack, Executive Director of College Park Here & Now, thanked over 200 readers who took the first audience survey for the publication and its sister publications. The survey revealed that readers rely on the publication for city government coverage, local events, and hyperlocal housing and development news. Slack noted that the publication will continue to deliver valued news and information, with a priority on civic engagement, and plans to deepen and broaden conversations with readers and community leaders in the coming year.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
44437.ai_summary: College Park has installed cameras on nine stop signs around the city to collect data on traffic and parking. Councilmember Stuart Adams said the city located the cameras at heavily trafficked intersections, and the data will be discussed at the Oct. 8 meeting. State Delegate Anne Healey sponsored legislation giving cities in Prince George’s County the authority to install stop sign monitors, saying "There have been a lot of people that have talked to me about people just ignoring the stop signs, just running right through them, sometimes very fast and not slowing down at all, and near misses and accidents and people getting hurt, So I expect that once the cameras are in place and people start getting tickets, they will change their behavior and actually stop at the stop signs where the cameras are."

key_people: Mike Wells, Senior Director of Franchise Operations for The Greene Turtle
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
44147.ai_summary: The Greene Turtle, a sports bar and grill, is set to open in College Park in late September, bringing a new casual dining option to the city. 'We're happy and we're really excited to be in College Park,' said Mike Wells, Senior Director of Franchise Operations for The Greene Turtle, noting that the company has long partnered with University of Maryland Athletics. The business's opening has been welcomed by city officials, including Councilmembers Susan Whitney and Stuart Adams, with Whitney expressing some concern about the restaurant staying within its allowed limit of alcohol sales, saying 'They definitely have the experience to be successful' but also noting that alcohol sales make up a significant portion of the business.

key_people: Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Gail Lovelace, co-chair of the Age-Friendly College Park initiative
key_issues: quality of life for older adults
44115.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is working towards achieving an age-friendly designation from the AARP, which indicates the city offers a good quality of life for older adults. According to Jen Holz, membership in the AARP Network of Age-Friendly States and Communities 'provides cities, towns, counties and states with the resources to become more age-friendly by tapping into national and global research, planning models and best practices.' College Park City Councilmember Maria Mackie said, 'I think that we need, as a city, to make College Park a place that people want to retire to and people want to continue living here,' and Mary Anne Hakes, SAC’s co-chair, stated the committee’s goal is 'to make College Park an age-friendly community for all ages … to allow people to age in place and a secret desire to make College Park a retirement destination.'

key_people: Aaron Goldbeck, University of Maryland (UMD) bicycle coordinator
44031.ai_summary: The city of College Park has seen a surge in e-bikes and e-scooters in recent years, but the effectiveness of these alternatives to traditional transportation methods is being questioned. As Aaron Goldbeck, the University of Maryland (UMD) bicycle coordinator at the time, said, 'We are very excited about the growth and success of the system.' The Union of Concerned Scientists has concluded that ride-hailing trips result in an estimated 69 percent more climate pollution on average than the trips they displace, highlighting the need for College Park to carefully consider its transportation options.

key_people: Susan Slingluff Hartmann, Executive Director of the College Park City-University Partnership
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
43934.ai_summary: The College Park City-University Partnership plans to spend $3 million to build an off-campus bike lane along Campus Drive that will connect to campus and to the Trolley Trail. Mayor Fazlul Kabir hopes future improvements will extend the trail north to Beltsville, stating 'Maybe in the future we could extend even beyond College Park, because bicycle transportation is not that great once you go past [here]... Beltsville is very dangerous, actually, to bike, so that’s something we’ll be advocating for.' The Trolley Trail was recently extended in Hyattsville, and the partnership is working to install art projects and improve safety features along the trail.

43946.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering a proposal to lower the voting age to 16, with Councilmember John Rigg saying 'this is something that we’ve been discussing for the better part of 10 years.' The council previously discussed lengthening their terms from two years to four, but declined to continue the effort. Rigg supports registering teenagers to vote, noting 'the younger they start participating in elections, the more likely they will regularly vote as adults.'

key_people: Brian Galbraith, Cherokee Lane Elementary Principal
Kayla Newsome, Cherokee Lane Elementary Assistant Principal
Bernitta Johnson, former coordinator of the signature programs and adviser of the business program
43931.ai_summary: The City of College Park has awarded a total of $36,800 in grants to eight local schools to fund educational projects, including an outdoor classroom, experiential learning opportunities, and a pitch competition for aspiring student entrepreneurs. As Cherokee Lane Elementary Principal Brian Galbraith stated, 'We are grateful for cities like College Park... They give schools these opportunities to think outside the box.' The grants will support various initiatives, such as Cherokee Lane's outdoor classroom, where Assistant Principal Kayla Newsome noted, 'The outdoor classroom will allow our children to explore and discover the world around them in ways that a traditional classroom simply cannot.'

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Varaa Kukreti, President of the UMD’s Graduate Student Government
Edward Maginnis Jr., UMD’s assistant vice president for real estate
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
43919.ai_summary: The University of Maryland is developing a new graduate student housing complex, Leonardtown apartments, which will provide 465 units near downtown College Park. College Park City Councilmembers John Rigg and Stuart Adams are enthusiastic about the project, with Rigg stating it will be a 'vast improvement over the status quo' and provide 'much needed housing at below market for some of our residents who have the most financial needs.' Varaa Kukreti, the new president of the UMD’s Graduate Student Government, said the project is a 'step in the right direction' for graduate student housing, although the new apartments will not be as affordable as students would like.

43838.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has recommended approval for a four-story, eight-unit apartment building on Autoville Drive, which will replace a single-family home. Councilmember Alan Hew praised the property owner for being cooperative and receptive to the city's suggestions, saying 'It's rare that we have an applicant that's so cooperative to the conditions put on them.' However, Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell voted against the project, citing concerns that it will 'change the character of the community, the quality of life of the residents.'

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Julia Bauer, Research and Data Analysis Program Manager for the National League of Cities
Michael Williams, Economic Development Department Director
43835.ai_summary: The City of College Park has spent $19 million of the $22 million in federal pandemic funding it received through the American Rescue Plan Act (ARPA) of 2021, with the remaining $3 million to be used by December 31, 2026. The city used the funds to help residents pay bills, subsidize local businesses, and invest in affordable housing, with small businesses receiving $1.7 million and nonprofits receiving $62,350. As College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams said, 'Without question, that was something unprecedented in the City of College Park,' and the city council focused on using ARPA funds to help residents and restart the local economy.

key_people: Janeen Miller, College Park City Clerk
43564.ai_summary: College Park City Clerk Janeen Miller will retire on Aug. 1 after 18 years in the position, and will be succeeded by Assistant City Clerk Yvette Allen. Miller said she is ready for the freedom that comes with retirement, stating 'More free time to myself to do the things that I put on the back burner and enjoy life without the responsibility of waking up to an alarm clock.' City Manager Kenny Young and College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir praised Miller's dedication and service to the city, with Kabir noting 'She reminds us, she keeps us on track. She’s very punctual. She gets also very honest.'

key_people: Tiffany Green, Prince George’s County Fire Chief
organizations: Branchville Volunteer Fire Co. & Rescue Squad
Berwyn Heights Volunteer Fire Department & Rescue Squad
43553.ai_summary: The Prince George’s County Circuit Court denied a request by College Park, Greenbelt, and Berwyn Heights to stop a plan to move paid firefighters from the Greenbelt and Berwyn Heights fire stations to elsewhere in the county. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated, 'The damage is already done,' and noted that the moves will 'definitely impacting College Park residents.' The relocation of firefighters may delay responses to fire and medical emergencies in College Park, with Kabir expressing concern that relying on volunteers, especially during the summer, puts residents at risk.

key_people: Michael Bodaken, Member of Prince George’s County Council’s Rent Stabilization Working Group
organizations: College Park Office of Economic Development
43581.ai_summary: A 3% rent cap proposed by the Prince George’s County Council may have a low impact on College Park, as up to 65% of apartment buildings could be exempt due to being constructed after 2000. As Michael Williams, the city’s economic development director, stated, 'My takeaway personally, and at the first swipe of it, is that because of all the new construction that really boomed since 2012, that we’re going to have a very small or very little effect on the 3% cap.' The proposed law aims to protect tenants from 'outrageous gouging' while ensuring it does not inhibit investment in the county, according to County Councilmember Eric Olson.

key_people: Tiffany Green, Prince George’s County Fire Chief
43462.ai_summary: The cities of Greenbelt, Berwyn Heights, and College Park filed a joint motion for a Temporary Restraining Order (TRO) against Prince George’s County to prevent the removal of career firefighters from local stations, citing concerns about the ability to provide timely and effective emergency responses. The municipalities argued that the county's plan to rely on volunteers would result in 'drastic shortages' of adequate personnel and impose an 'untenable workload' on volunteer staff. Judge William Snoddy rejected the motion, primarily due to the plaintiffs' failure to demonstrate irreparable harm, as the county claimed the summer staffing plan is for the department's health and safety and in the public interest.

key_people: Mike Galvin, Director of SavATree’s consultant group
Richard Jones, University of Maryland Campus Arborist
organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
key_issues: Tree canopy loss in residential areas
Tree canopy gain in non-residential areas
43393.ai_summary: A new assessment found that tree cover on residential properties in College Park decreased between 2009 and 2020, despite overall growth throughout the rest of the city. According to Mike Galvin, director of SavATree’s consultant group, the city's efforts to limit tree removal and encourage planting are 'great proactive steps' toward slowing or stopping the loss of tree canopy in neighborhoods. As University of Maryland Campus Arborist Richard Jones noted, 'Having forest is so critical, not only for the environment, but for our ability to go about our day-to-day lives in a city.'

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Ryna Quinones, City Communications and Events Manager
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
43119.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is questioning the future of the annual spring parade, with Councilmember John Rigg suggesting that the city 'strongly consider getting out of the parade business' due to the high cost, which amounts to approximately $47 per attendee. The city spent $34,000 on this year's parade and invested around 1,000 staff hours worth another $34,000, according to Ryna Quinones, the city's communications and events manager. Councilmembers are exploring alternative events, such as a music festival or a summer concert series, to achieve the objective of community-building and engagement.

key_people: Ken Ulman, President of Terrapin Development Co.
Sahil Patel, UMD Junior Computer Science Major
organizations: University of Maryland College Park Foundation
43121.ai_summary: Shake Shack, Chopt, and Duck Donuts will open in College Park this fall, located on the first floor of the new student housing building, Union on Knox. Ken Ulman, President of Terrapin Development Co., stated that 'Shake Shack, you know, frankly, they have been looking for a location in College Park for a number of years' and is excited to welcome them. City Councilmember John Rigg expressed enthusiasm for the new additions, saying 'I have a passion for small businesses, but I know these will be great additions to College Park that members of the community will enjoy' and 'Bad businesses don’t last long in College Park, our residents tend to know good stuff when they see it and go back again and again'.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
43109.ai_summary: The city of College Park offers multiple programs to help homeowners save on property taxes, including the Homestead Property Tax Credit and the Homeowners' Property Tax Credit. According to College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams, 'I would say without hesitation that College Park has some of the most generous property tax credits of any municipality in Prince George’s County and some of the strongest in the entire state.' These credits can help reduce the financial burden of property taxes, especially after the recent 11% increase in the residential tax rate in College Park.

key_people: Gannon Sprinkle, Former Deputy Student Liaison
organizations: Harford County Parks and Recreation Department
Maryland Office of the Public Defender
43126.ai_summary: Two new University of Maryland students, Erica Otte and John Fahmy, will serve as liaison and deputy liaison to the College Park City Council. Otte, who was selected by a panel and approved by the SGA, aims to 'make it easier for students to connect with the broader community' and said 'I'm very passionate about advocacy' and wants to 'make it easier to raise a family in College Park, especially after graduating.' Councilmember Maria Mackie praised Otte's 'real desire to build bridges between students and long-term residents' and said 'She doesn't want to do the 'us and them' thing.'

key_people: Teresa Way-Pezzuti, College Park Director of Human Resources
Rick Campbell, Technical Director of Paypoint HR
43045.ai_summary: The City of College Park will increase salaries for 74 employees after a study by Paypoint HR showed they earn less than their peers in other cities in the region. 'I think it's vitally important for us to bring our employees to a fair compensation and wage, especially if we want to maintain them,' said Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters. The city aims to reach the 50th percentile of the salaries of 25 comparable cities, with a potential cost of $240,784, and may aim to meet the 60th percentile in the future.

43114.ai_summary: The Washington Post featured the North College Park neighborhoods of Hollywood, Branchville, and Daniels Park in an article about the advocacy efforts of their residents. The community benefits from engaged neighbors who know how to work together, as noted by reporter Iris Vukmanovic, who highlighted their efforts to save the Hollywood Farmers Market through an email-writing campaign. The article showcases the community's passion for walking and biking and its sense of community due to its tremendous diversity.

key_people: Gary Fields, Director of the College Park Department of Finance
Bonny Davis, long-time College Park resident
42752.ai_summary: The College Park City Council adopted a $27.4 million operating budget for fiscal year 2025, including an 11% residential property tax rate increase. As Gary Fields, Director of the College Park Department of Finance, stated, 'We do not take proposing a property tax increase lightly... We're looking to the future to make sure we stay financially sound.' The tax increase was met with opposition from some residents, including Brian Roan, who warned that it could lead to a 'massive drain of families and long-time residents' from the city.

key_people: Glenn Ivey, U.S. Congress representative for Maryland District 4
key_issues: Summer student exodus impact on local businesses
42023.ai_summary: The city of College Park has entered the contractor selection phase for a flood abatement project in the Calvert Hills neighborhood, which has been in the works for over 12 years. Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that the project will bring stormwater systems up to current standards, but that current codes are not up-to-date with climate change, saying 'We need to acknowledge that this is going to happen a lot more frequently than people want to believe.' The city is also facing challenges from the summer student exodus, which affects local businesses, and recently held primary elections where Angela Alsobrooks won the state Democratic primary for U.S. Senate and Glenn Ivey won the primary for his seat in the U.S. Congress.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Chair of the Restorative Justice Commission
Maxine Gross, Chairwoman of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project
Ruth Murphy, Lakeland Civic Association Vice President
Robert Thurston, President of the Lakeland Civic Association
41839.ai_summary: Maxine Gross, a fifth-generation Lakelander and Chair of the Restorative Justice Commission, is working to preserve the history of Lakeland and share the stories of the community. As Gross said, the city's urban renewal effort destroyed 104 single-family homes, displacing families, and she is committed to documenting and preserving the community's history through the Lakeland Community Heritage Project. According to Ruth Murphy, 'Maxine [is] such an incredible gift... she takes the ego out of the work, and she is genuinely committed to the work itself,' and her efforts have led to the inclusion of a new community center in a student housing development, which will also store a physical, historic archive of Lakeland.

key_people: Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Carson Peters, doctoral student at the UMD School of Public Health
Mark Williams, director of the jazz studies program
41833.ai_summary: The City of College Park participated in Maryland Day, an annual event that took place on April 27, with City Manager Kenny Young stating, 'I think it’s an important event just to showcase what the University of Maryland offers to the community.' The city set up a tent to provide information about local resources and engage with attendees. The event featured various activities, including lawn games, live music, and educational exhibits, with Carson Peters, a doctoral student, noting, 'I think Maryland Day is a really good testament in terms of … academics, partnerships in the community [and] community engagement.'

key_people: Rachel Romeo, UMD hearing and speech sciences professor
Susan Slingluff Hartmann, Executive Director of the College Park City-University Partnership
organizations: Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development
41822.ai_summary: The College Park City-University Partnership has ended an eight-year homeownership program that awarded $15,000 to 83 new homebuyers, and has launched a new housing assistance program called Live + Work College Park. The new program offers a forgivable loan of $20,000 to support full-time employees working for any employer in the city, with the goal of supporting homeownership and neighborhood stabilization. As Rachel Romeo, a UMD professor, said, 'it did make it possible for us to buy a house we might not otherwise have been able to,' and Susan Slingluff Hartmann noted that the programs 'are able to offer a variety of housing solutions that I think benefit the College Park community and benefit our goals of supporting homeownership and neighborhood stabilization in our communities.'

key_people: Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Stuart Adams, structural engineer and College Park City Councilmember
Daniel Oates, president of the Calvert Hills Citizens Association
41817.ai_summary: The Calvert Hills stormwater drainage project is moving forward after over a decade, with contractor selection underway and work expected to begin by mid-summer. As Prince George’s County Councilmember Eric Olson said, 'It’s unfortunate that this project took this long, but I’m glad that we’re finally at the stage where it [went] to bid.' The project aims to divert stormwater from Calvert Road into an underground concrete vault at Calvert Park, preventing pooling on streets and lawns, but as Stuart Adams noted, 'We need to acknowledge that this is going to happen a lot more frequently than people want to believe... it’s going to bring us up to the current standard, but not up to current climate.'

key_people: Ryna Quiñones, Communications and Events Manager
Betty Colonomos, College Park Senior Advisory Committee member
41846.ai_summary: The city's third annual Star Wars-themed parade on May 4 attracted hundreds of residents despite rain, with many wearing costumes and marching in the event. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'It's wonderful, I've met quite a few neighbors, and I'm very pleased that they came with their families.' According to Ryna Quiñones, the city's communications and events manager, the parade is an example of the city's community building events, stating 'There's something for everybody, for every age, and [our offerings are] very diverse.'

key_people: Nora Urbieta Eidelman, Church Administrator & Outreach Director
organizations: Maryland Cannabis Public Health Advisory Council
Community Legal Services of Prince George’s County, Inc.
Human Rights Commission for Prince Georges’ County
42280.ai_summary: The Board of Directors consists of various professionals, including Marta McLellan Ross, President of the Board, Melanie Dzwonchyk, Secretary, and Joe Murchison, Treasurer. Nora Urbieta Eidelman serves on the Ethics Commission for the City of College Park, Maryland and the Maryland Cannabis Public Health Advisory Council. The board members have diverse backgrounds and experiences, with some having worked in journalism, law, and community service, which may influence their roles in College Park governance.

key_people: Kelly Jordan, Co-President of Berwyn District Civic Association
Bob Catlin, Co-President of Berwyn District Civic Association
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
41659.ai_summary: The Berwyn community in College Park has been publishing a regular newsletter for 50 years, and two years ago, the Berwyn District Civic Association started translating it into Spanish to reach the neighborhood's Spanish-speaking population. As Kelly Jordan, Co-President of the association, said, 'You have to understand your audience and who makes up your audience,' and with the help of a grant from the city, they were able to hire a translator, Edward Najjar. The city's proposed operating budget for fiscal year 2025 includes $7,500 for neighborhood associations to pay for translators and $65,000 to add a full-time bilingual communications coordinator, which Councilmember Jacob Hernandez supports, saying 'I'm all about getting folks who speak Spanish to be a part of the larger community.'

41342.ai_summary: The Hollywood Farmers Market in College Park is set to open for the season on April 13, despite concerns from city staff about its cost, with the city considering increasing its budget to $30,000 next year. Assistant City Manager Bill Gardiner noted that 'In the big scheme of our budget, it's not very much money,' but added that 'At the same time, in terms of comparable farmers markets, it's still unusual.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated that 'Some of them are very passionate, had very strong comments and strong support for the market,' highlighting the market's value to the Hollywood neighborhood for community engagement.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
organizations: Meals on Wheels of College Park
41366.ai_summary: In his State of the City address, Mayor Fazlul Kabir highlighted the 'exponential growth' College Park experienced in 2023, with 10 new businesses opening and an estimated 250 new jobs created. The city is expecting nearly 20 new businesses and restaurants to open in the coming year, and will add student and affordable housing complexes through grants and partnerships. Kabir also noted the city's efforts to improve sustainability, diversity, and safety, and made donations to community organizations, including $300,000 to fire companies and $500,000 to local food banks.

41323.ai_summary: The College Park city council is considering a 13% increase in residential property taxes, which would be the first increase in 10 years. According to Gary Fields, the city's finance director, 'It's a reset... We're doing it for the future... We feel like that we will not have to be looking at [another] rate increase in the near future.' The proposed tax increase is part of a $27.4 million budget proposal for fiscal year 2025, which aims to fund the city's expenses.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Dhruvak Mirani, Student Liaison to College Park City Council
Gannon Sprinkle, Deputy Student Liaison to College Park City Council
41358.ai_summary: The application for student liaison to the College Park City Council is open until April 12, with the student liaison and deputy liaison serving one-year terms and participating in weekly council meetings. According to Mayor Fazlul Kabir, 'The ideal candidate would attend the meetings, work with [city council] in a collaborative fashion and who has a genuine concern about College Park on both sides of Route 1.' Current student liaison Dhruvak Mirani noted that 'our city ought to be responsive to student interests, but sometimes it’s really hard to believe that, see[ing] it play out in reality,' and expressed his desire for student liaisons to have voting status on the city council.

41348.ai_summary: The Maryland General Assembly passed two bills to increase penalties for street racing and allow Prince George’s and Montgomery counties to install noise-sensing traffic cameras. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'It is not unusual for groups of motorcycle and ATV drivers to briefly take over sections of Baltimore Avenue on Route 1... These actions harm our communities, and these actions waste … resources, especially the police resources.' Delegate Mary Lehman stated, 'The fact is, exhibition driving will continue to disrupt our communities, endanger law enforcement and risk serious bodily harm, or worse, to participants and passersby.'

key_people: Gregory Slater, Former Maryland Transportation Secretary
Kayla Scott, manager at Looney’s Pub
41191.ai_summary: The first phase of the Route 1 improvement project is set to finish this spring after a year-long delay due to staffing and supply shortages during the pandemic. The project, which began in 2020, aims to widen the street to make room for bike and turn lanes, and includes adding pedestrian lighting, resurfacing the road, and landscaping the medians. As College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'The changes, once it’s happened, will make Route 1 very nice... It’s not about making the street pretty, … It’s for our safety.'

key_people: Danny Copeland, RST Development Vice President
Kate Kennedy, District 1 resident and former city councilmember
organizations: Meals on Wheels of College Park
41187.ai_summary: Developers of a 317-unit affordable apartment building on Baltimore Avenue, called Flats at College Park, expect to start construction as soon as April. The new property will offer two- and three-bedroom apartments with monthly rents based on income, and will revitalize the community, according to RST Development Vice President Danny Copeland, who said 'It’s a redevelopment of some motels that weren’t necessarily a shining light along the Route 1 corridor.' However, District 1 City Councilmember Alan Hew expressed concern that the project, along with other nearby developments, will create heavy traffic on Cherokee Street, a concern shared by District 1 resident and former city councilmember Kate Kennedy, who said 'The idea is that we want as much traffic on Route 1 as possible and as little traffic in the neighborhoods.'

key_people: Griffin Limerick, Life & Times managing editor
41131.ai_summary: The city of College Park voted to explore imposing a tax on vacant and/or blighted properties, as it can be more profitable for owners and developers to keep a property vacant. The city is hoping to identify the right tax structure and language to make that math work more towards ending vacancies. Griffin Limerick, Life & Times managing editor, is calling for a return of in-person council meetings in Hyattsville, noting that other cities like Takoma Park, Greenbelt, College Park, and Laurel have already done so.

key_people: Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
40635.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to form a subcommittee to explore imposing a tax on vacant and blighted properties in the city. According to College Park City Manager Kenny Young, 'We're in a position now that [we] kind of have to force the issue and get people to take more of an interest in making sure properties are maintained in College Park.' Councilmember Stuart Adams has advocated for this tax since 2019, stating 'The intent of the vacant and blighted property tax discussion, substantially in the commercial space but also in the residential space, is to mitigate some of the tax incentives that can occur for property owners to keep things vacant.'

key_people: Maxine Gross, chairwoman of the Restorative Justice Commission
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Joanne Braxton, CEO and president of the Braxton Institute
40745.ai_summary: The College Park Restorative Justice Commission has partnered with the Braxton Institute to host a dozen events in Lakeland, focusing on the community's history and restorative justice. As Maxine Gross, chairwoman of the Restorative Justice Commission, said, 'It’s important to not just recognize that thing happened, but also to take positive action on assisting that community and heal it.' The events, which include lectures, workshops, and book club conversations, aim to raise awareness about urban renewal in Lakeland and its impact on the community, as noted by College Park City Councilmember Susan Whitney, who stated 'I think a lot of people by now have heard the story, But it’s different to hear the facts of what happened and to really go into the impact of them and how other communities have felt about that and how it has impacted people psychologically.'

key_people: Bonnie McClellan, Vice Chair of the College Park Woods Neighborhood Watch
Robert Marsili, College Park Director of Public Works
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
40680.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved a $1.3 million contract with Rainbow Construction Co. to build a new community center at 3545 Marlborough Way, which is expected to be completed by the end of the year. According to Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember, 'Many of our activities have been underneath the gazebo in the pouring rain, so it’ll be a nice thing to have for the District 4 community, but also for the city.' The new community center will provide indoor space for various gatherings and events, and Bonnie McClellan, Vice Chair of the College Park Woods Neighborhood Watch, says 'This is a dream come true... it will make a very big difference for our community–and the entire community–because it’s going to be a very usable space.'

organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
40750.ai_summary: The College Park Restorative Justice Commission proposed changes to park names to honor Lakeland history, including renaming Lake Artemesia Natural Area and the College Park Community Center. The commission also called for the establishment of a scholarship fund for Lakeland residents, with Kaya Carter stating that council support would be an acknowledgment of the 'enduring spirit and resilience of Lakelanders.' Councilmember Jacob Hernandez thanked the commission for their efforts, saying 'I just want to thank you all for the very thoughtful steps to try to address some of the harder parts of our history here in College Park, but also trying to pay homage to the brighter future that we have.'

40533.ai_summary: The Washington Commanders' headquarters has moved to the University of Maryland's Discovery District on River Road, with the city and university hoping to attract other prominent companies. The move may impact traffic on River Road, particularly during College Park Academy pickup times. Meanwhile, a recent PGCPS board meeting focused on bus problems, with board member Pamela Boozer-Strother saying, 'Let's be honest, we've been talking about the bell times in many task forces on transportation for years. This district has been unwilling to change them because every school community has a reason why they think their bell time shouldn't change.'

key_people: Dhruvak Mirani, University of Maryland liaison to the College Park City Council
Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland deputy liaison to the College Park City Council
William Edwards, senior studio art major
40245.ai_summary: University of Maryland student leaders Dhruvak Mirani and Gannon Sprinkle proposed establishing a tenants union for students who rent in College Park, with goals to lower the cost of rent, improve living conditions, and address maintenance issues. As Mirani said, 'We can’t really address housing prices in a vacuum without talking about all of the other issues that go along with it,' and Sprinkle added, 'We need something to rally behind, a force to fight for better living conditions and lower rent prices.' The median monthly rent in College Park between 2018 and 2022 was $1,838, according to the U.S. Census Bureau, highlighting the need for affordable housing solutions.

key_people: Denise Mitchell, Mayor Pro Tem and District 4 Councilmember
39395.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has proposed a housing subsidy of $1,500 for 150 low-income University of Maryland undergraduates to help them rent apartments in high-rise Route 1 buildings. The proposal has received pushback from residents, with some calling it a 'theft of taxpayer revenue' and questioning why the city and not the university is offering the subsidy, as resident Brian McAllister asked, 'Why does the City Council feel it is their responsibility to pay these subsidies when the university and the business developers aren’t willing to do it for themselves?' Mayor Fazlul Kabir predicted the proposal will change considerably before the council votes on it.

organizations: College Park Committee for a Better Environment (CBE)
39385.ai_summary: The College Park Committee for a Better Environment (CBE) has been leading sustainability efforts in the city for over 50 years. The committee has partnered with local artists and organizations to raise awareness about environmental issues, such as storm drain management, and has implemented initiatives like the tree identification program and Green Awards. According to CBE Chair Todd Larson, the projects like the storm drain murals 'give local artists a chance to show their work in a place where everyone can see it, while also providing an educational message to help residents understand why it’s important not to put anything down the storm drain except water.'

key_people: Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember
39177.ai_summary: New laws are taking effect in Maryland as of Jan. 1, 2024, including a plastic bag ban, a higher minimum wage of $15 per hour, and the Stop the Spam Calls Act. According to Prince George’s County Councilmember Eric Olson, the plastic bag ban “will remove over 300 million plastic bags a year from the waste stream in Prince George’s County alone.” Maryland Del. Joseline Pena-Melnyk said the higher minimum wage is still too low, but it’s a step in the right direction, stating “People are barely getting by. Even $15 an hour is not enough; we should have a living wage. But it’s a balancing act between the needs of people and what the businesses can handle right now.”

key_people: Jacob T. Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
39012.ai_summary: Jacob T. Hernandez has joined the College Park City Council as a District 1 representative, becoming the city's only Hispanic American councilmember. Hernandez aims to enhance communication, improve education, and help residents navigate bureaucratic hurdles, saying 'It's only through our united organized voices that we are going to be able to facilitate the greater projects and programs that I have in store.' He hopes to strengthen relationships between the city and local public education leaders to improve conditions in public schools and address overcrowding.

key_people: Michael Williams, College Park’s economic development director
39023.ai_summary: The City of College Park is addressing the displacement of small businesses due to new apartment building developments, with a $75,000 business retention fund in the fiscal 2024 budget. As Michael Williams, College Park’s economic development director, stated, 'We wanted to retain some of the businesses who make up the fabric and the character of College Park.' The city has allocated $23,000 to Laser Essential for relocation assistance, and Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that around half of the funds raised by a commercial property tax increase could be used to support local businesses.

key_people: H.C. "Curley" Byrd, University of Maryland President
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC)
39017.ai_summary: The article discusses the history of Lakeland, a neighborhood in College Park, and its transformation over the years. The author, Maxine Gross, shares her personal journey of researching the community's past and its struggles, including the displacement of residents due to urban renewal in the 1960s and 1970s. The prosperity of neighborhoods like Lakeland, Berwyn, and Hollywood is crucial to the overall thriving of the city, and understanding this history is essential for College Park governance.

39032.ai_summary: The Winter Wonderland event was held at City Hall Plaza on Dec. 2, featuring holiday music, vendors, and photos with Santa. Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell and Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters lit up a 20-foot tall holiday tree made of white globes. Old Town resident Kathy Bryant commented that the new city hall was a wonderful venue for the event, saying 'We couldn’t have had something like this before, there wasn’t any place to have it,' while Berwyn resident Emily Friend said her 5-month-old son Miles had a good time, noting 'He was scared of Santa but loved the Grinch, it’s cool that there was something fun for every group and every age.'

39010.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now is seeking donations to support local news, with a goal of raising $13,000 by the end of the year. As Kit Slack, Executive Director, notes, the publication relies on donations to deliver news to every home in the city, every month, for free. If 90 of their 30,000 readers in College Park chip in $100, they will meet their goal, and Newsmatch has pledged to match donations received before Dec. 31 dollar for dollar.

38926.ai_summary: City Councilmember Emily Strab won the first-ever Maryland Catholic Woman of the Year award, presented by the Maryland Catholic Women’s Conference. According to the conference, “Emily Strab’s selection as the 2023 Maryland Catholic Woman of the Year exemplifies the values of faith, hope, and love in action.” This award recognizes Strab's contributions to the community, including her role as a music educator and administrator at St. Jerome Institute.

key_people: Sara Rían, author and grief poet
38992.ai_summary: The author, Jaclyn Bruner, is advocating for crossing guards at elementary schools in College Park, after two Riverdale Elementary School students were hit by a car. As she notes, “don’t you grow up / we say to babies. / don’t you get old / we say to adults. / but please do. / grow up. get old. / what a privilege. / what a gift,” quoting author Sara Rían. The College Park City Council listened to her concerns in January, but as of December, there are still 83 open crossing guard positions in Prince George’s County, and the issue remains unresolved.

key_people: Robert "RC" Carter, head of the ElectionGuard program
Benjamin Hovland, member of the College Park Board of Elections Supervisors
38704.ai_summary: The City of College Park has implemented new verification technology in its elections, allowing voters to confirm that their votes have been counted. As Robert "RC" Carter, head of the ElectionGuard program, said, "Think of it as like FedEx saying your package has arrived". The technology, developed by Microsoft, provides voters with a paper confirmation that they can use to verify their vote was counted, and also creates an encrypted public record of the vote that can be inspected and verified by interested parties.

key_issues: new city ordinance on grass and weeds
38718.ai_summary: Miss Floribunda, an advice columnist, weighed in on the new city ordinance on grass and weeds, which permits alternative gardens and sanctions the use of native plants. A reader, Mowed and Manicured on Madison Street, expressed aesthetic reservations about the ordinance, stating 'it seems to me there’s no way such a garden can look anything but messy and unkempt.' Miss Floribunda argued that using native plants and grasses can create beautiful and harmonious gardens, citing examples from France and Japanese gardens.

key_people: Jacob T. Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Kamthorn G. Clay, College Park City Council candidate
Bryan Haddad, College Park City Council candidate
Brian J. Roan, College Park City Council candidate
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Stuart M. Adams, College Park City Councilmember
John B. Rigg III, College Park City Councilmember
A. Perez Abbott, College Park City Council candidate
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan L. Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Maria E. Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Denise C. Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
38626.ai_summary: Jacob T. Hernandez was elected to the College Park City Council for District 1 with 266 votes, running on a platform focused on family and community involvement. Incumbent Mayor Fazlul Kabir was reelected with 1,217 votes, and other councilmembers, including Alan Hew, Stuart M. Adams, and John B. Rigg III, also secured their seats. Voters also cast their ballots on a nonbinding question about term lengths for councilmembers, with 710 supporting a change to staggered 4-year terms.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Perez Abbott, College Park City Council Candidate
organizations: The U.S. Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority
Prince George’s County Economic Development Corporation
38536.ai_summary: College Park District 3 city council candidates and Mayor Fazlul Kabir discussed economic development, public safety, and property taxes at a forum sponsored by the Yarrow Civic Association. The candidates, including Stuart Adams, John Rigg, and Perez Abbott, shared their plans to address the city's challenges, with Mayor Kabir stating 'We have been working to take care of the businesses because they are our assets.' The forum also touched on the displacement of 15 businesses at Campus Village Shops and the need for an independent police force in College Park.

38531.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now sponsored a candidate forum for District 1 in the council chambers at City Hall on October 18, 2023. The forum covered various topics, including government teamwork, small business development, construction, connectivity, climate change, and road dangers. Voting for the College Park election is scheduled to take place on November 5 at the College Park Community Center.

38455.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now has published a guide for the 2023 city election, featuring candidate information and voting details. Mayor Fazlul Kabir, who is running for re-election, has outlined his platform, stating that he plans to 'continue leading College Park by uniting the community, enhancing transparency and fostering trust' with a focus on 'improving the quality of life with strong public safety measures, enhanced code enforcement, smart economic development, support for new small businesses, sustainable and green programs, solutions for aging-in-place seniors'. The election will take place on November 5, with early voting on October 25 and November 2.

key_people: Daniel Cunningham, Executive Director of the Community Preservation Trust
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Susan Hartman, Executive Director of the College Park City-University Partnership
organizations: Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development
38473.ai_summary: The Community Preservation Trust, an offshoot of the College Park City-University Partnership, celebrated its first home purchase and sale with a ribbon cutting ceremony. The program aims to preserve the character of the city's neighborhoods and increase home ownership by making affordable homes available. As homeowner William Whichard said, 'Gratitude characterizes what I feel today,' and Mayor Fazlul Kabir praised the program, saying 'This is a fantastic event … and this is a program that will give our residents an opportunity to own a home, build generation wealth and help stabilize the neighborhood.'

key_people: Miriam Bader, Director of Planning and Community Development
38373.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved the street name Ancestor’s Lane, which was chosen by the Restorative Justice Commission to honor the local Black community. As Kayla Carter, Racial Equity Officer, explained, 'The Lakeland community, in particular, was historically Black,' and the commission wanted to give the street a name that the entire community could connect with. The commission aims to create committees to work toward reparations and build community engagement, with Carter stating 'It’s a very large project, and things are still new, but we’re doing the best we can.'

38371.ai_summary: The College Park election will take place on November 5. Voting information and candidate forums are available for District 1 and District 3. The article provides updates and resources for voters, including a Voters Guide and summaries of candidate forums.

38290.ai_summary: The College Park city council is preparing for a November 5 election where voters will decide on a non-binding advisory question about switching from two-year to four-year terms for elected officials. Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that 'If the people vote yes, the city won’t actually change things until the council votes on it. The ballot question’s purpose is to gather information.' Resident Aubrey Batten expressed her opposition, stating 'I take exception to the assertion by certain councilmembers at last week’s discussion that residents need to be educated about this issue so that we change our minds. Our voice was made clear by the 2019 vote that we prioritize accountability over making it easy to campaign.'

key_people: Jeremy Rose, FairVote worker and North College Park resident
Alexa Bely, University of Maryland teacher/researcher and Calvert Hills resident
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
38056.ai_summary: The current voting system in College Park has a flaw where voter preferences among non-winning candidates are ignored. Jeremy Rose and Alexa Bely suggest adopting ranked choice voting, which considers a voter's top preference and ranked choices, to increase voter satisfaction and reduce polarization. As the next mayoral election approaches, College Park should consider ranked choice voting for the 2025 city election, as it can provide a better approach to elections, with benefits including increased overall voter satisfaction and a larger and more diverse pool of candidates.

key_people: Matthew Kirschebaum, English professor at the University of Maryland
38050.ai_summary: Two new franchise restaurants, The Great Greek Mediterranean Grill and The Little Blue Menu, are coming to College Park, sparking community debate. The Little Blue Menu, a digital-based restaurant, will be located on the former site of the Applebee's on Baltimore Avenue and will offer competitive-wage employment and donate $25,000 to Feeding America on behalf of the new restaurant. As a local resident, Elizabeth Maring expressed 'sadness and frustration', while Matthew Kirschebaum, an English professor at the University of Maryland, said '[Chick-fil-A] simply has no interest in the community and isn’t going to even try to pretend otherwise'.

key_people: John Kish, Chairman of the Election Board
38039.ai_summary: The College Park election board has enforced a temporary ban on campaigning by candidates for city office, causing confusion among three announced mayoral candidates. Keith Sydnor said, 'I don’t agree with it, but I’m abiding by it,' after suspending his campaign. Martin Mitchell contested the no-campaign rule in a letter, stating, 'I see no violation of City election law by campaigning for an office prior to being certified by the Board,' and argued it raises First Amendment issues.

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Marcus Afzali, former College Park Councilmember
Joel Carlson, friend and email author
organizations: College Park Committee for a Better Environment
37990.ai_summary: Alan Hew, a long-time community advocate, was sworn in as the new District 1 council member in College Park. Hew previously served on the city council from 2013 to 2015 and has been involved in various community initiatives, including the College Park Animal Welfare Committee and the Bee City USA Committee. As Hew recalled, former councilmember Marcus Afzali encouraged him to run for the seat, saying 'No, but your heart's there. You understand how the city works, through working through that development and working on your committees. That's what it takes to serve, and you've done that.'

37973.ai_summary: The City of College Park held its first in-person town hall meeting, where City Manager Kenny Young presented the city's budget for fiscal year 2024, totaling nearly $41 million. Residents asked questions about tax breaks for developers, with Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell explaining that the city offers a tax credit over five years for developers to build in the city, and also prioritizes affordable student housing, saying 'Seniors and students are our most vulnerable residents, so affordable student housing is a priority.' Councilmembers and city officials responded to concerns about student housing, local businesses, and city communications, with Economic Development Manager Michael Williams noting that tax credits help businesses operate year-round and Councilmember Alan Hew suggesting an off-campus orientation guide for students.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
37724.ai_summary: Mayor Fazlul Kabir, who was inaugurated on May 16, is a strong advocate for community engagement and public service. As he stated, 'I'm a very hyper-local person. The thing I enjoy most is talking to a neighbor on his or her porch and talking about some problems or some issues and see[ing] how to solve them or get things done.' Kabir's experience with the North College Park Civic Association and his time on the city council have prepared him for his role as mayor, where he aims to rebuild community trust in city leaders.

37734.ai_summary: Biohabitats Inc. has completed an initial survey of the Sentinel Swamp Sanctuary, identifying two nontidal wetlands and recommending a raised boardwalk and viewing platform. According to Erik Valentine, Community Development Planner for the City of College Park, 'We're still in the very early stages of this project,' and 'there are still 13 invasive species that still have to be removed.' The city has allocated $200,000 for the project, with funding expected to be ongoing through Program Open Space.

key_people: Violetta Sharps Jones, Vice President of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project
Julie Schablitsky, Chief of Cultural Resources with the Maryland Department of Transportation
Rev. Latishia Cokley, Pastor of Embry African Methodist Episcopal Church
37619.ai_summary: The Lakeland community gathered to unveil a historic marker, led by Denise Mitchell, Mayor Pro Tem, and featuring a ceremonial libation by Violetta Sharps Jones, who said 'We pour this libation to give honor, reverence, respect, and recognition to our ancestors whose shoulders we stand on and whose shadows we walk in.' The event was attended by state and local officials, and highlighted the importance of legacy and community recognition. As Ed Williams noted, 'It's been a long, a long time coming, but I know a change gon' come,' reflecting the community's ongoing efforts to preserve their history and cultural heritage.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: College Park Tree and Landscaping Board
37521.ai_summary: The City of College Park has begun a sidewalk installation project on Edmonston Road, which involves cutting down a large portion of trees to build a sidewalk for mobility to bus stops and the Calvert Road Community Park. According to College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, 'The Complete Streets project is part of our broader initiatives that the city has been involved in. But it’s been about enhancing connectivity and safety for pedestrians and cyclists in our city.' However, the project has created controversy among residents who are concerned about cutting down trees, with Greenbelt resident Kennedy Walker saying 'the money used to construct the sidewalk could definitely go towards other aspects of the community like more flowers and less trash in the area.'

key_people: Maria James, former managing editor of The Hyattsville Life & Times
37504.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now hosted an election forum featuring candidates running in the May 2023 Mayoral Special Election. Maria James posed a series of questions to the four candidates, covering topics such as development, traffic, and affordable housing. The forum was held on April 15, and election day is May 6th.

key_people: Terry Schum, Director of the College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
Miriam Bader, Director of Planning and Development
Dhiru Thadani, Artist and Terry Schum's Husband
organizations: College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
37434.ai_summary: Terry Schum is retiring after 37 years of service as the director of the College Park Department of Planning & Community Development. As she said, 'How am I going to disengage from these things?' Schum will be succeeded by Miriam Bader, and she regrets not being in the city to see projects like the renovation of Duvall Field and the Route 1 revitalization effort finalized, and plans to pursue her lifelong interest in drawing and painting, saying 'Maybe I will carry around a sketchbook'.

37417.ai_summary: The City of College Park has purchased a small parcel of undeveloped land in the Berwyn neighborhood through the state-funded Program Open Space. Community Development Planner Erik Valentine hopes to use the parcel as open space where people can gather, and the city is seeking community input on how to move forward with the property. Valentine said that by preserving the sanctuary, the city “hope[s] to incorporate educational elements because of the site’s unique natural habitat.”

37426.ai_summary: The City of College Park is holding a mayoral election on May 6, with early voting on May 2. Bryan Haddad, a small business owner, is running as a single-issue candidate to increase penalties against illegally modified vehicles, stating 'A vote for me is a vote for a more peaceful community.' A candidate forum will be held on April 15, moderated by Maria James, to discuss issues with the candidates.

key_people: Patrick Wojahn, Mayor of College Park
organizations: National League of Cities University Communities Council
The National Center for Missing and Exploited Children
key_issues: Arrest and resignation of Mayor Patrick Wojahn
37262.ai_summary: College Park Mayor Patrick Wojahn was arrested on charges related to the possession and distribution of child pornography and resigned from office. Wojahn stated, 'While this investigation does not involve any official city business of any kind, it is in the best interests of our community that I step aside and not serve as a distraction.' Mayor Pro Tem Mitchell will serve as pro tem mayor until a special election is held within 65 days.

key_people: Robert Thurston, president of the Lakeland Civic Association
Terry Schum, director of the College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
Todd Alexander, representative of Renewal Housing Associates
organizations: College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
37038.ai_summary: LV Collective, a real estate developer, has purchased a property in Lakeland for $36 million and plans to build a student housing complex with retail on the ground floor. Robert Thurston, president of the Lakeland Civic Association, emphasized the need for multigenerational housing and community involvement in the development process, stating 'It’s critical that Lakelanders and the Lakeland diaspora are involved in this redevelopment.' The development review process is underway, with Terry Schum, director of the College Park Department of Planning & Community Development, encouraging collaboration between property owners and the community.

key_people: Martha Ainsworth, Prince George’s Sierra Club
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember (District 1)
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Robert Marsili, College Park Department of Public Works Director
Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director
Todd Larsen, Committee for a Better Environment (CBE)
Alexa Bely, Committee for a Better Environment (CBE)
organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
37031.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed a proposed ordinance to regulate the use of shopping bags by retail businesses, including restaurants, which would ban plastic carryout bags and require a minimum $0.10 fee for paper or new reusable bags. The proposal aims to promote the city's sustainability goals by reducing shoppers' reliance on single-use bags, with support from the Prince George’s Sierra Club and the Maryland Retailers Association. Councilmember John Rigg expressed support for the bill and requested that the city's implementation include distribution of reusable bags in targeted locations to ensure support for people who may struggle to afford bag fees.

key_people: Rebecca Bailey, College Park Animal Control Officer
Bob Ryan, College Park Department of Public Services Director
organizations: College Park Department of Public Services
37024.ai_summary: College Park Animal Control Officer Rebecca Bailey loves her job because it gives her the opportunity to help animals, saying 'They don’t have a way to advocate for themselves.' Bailey's responsibilities include picking up stray pets, responding to complaints, and educating the public about animal care. The city has a small animal shelter where it temporarily houses rescued pets, and Bailey spends most of her time responding to calls for service, with the goal of reuniting pets with their owners or finding them new homes, as seen in the adoption stories of Aron Bender and Bryan Draper.

key_people: Anna Latta Owens, College Park Mayor
William E. "Brit" Kirwan, University of Maryland Leader
36789.ai_summary: Anna Latta Owens, College Park's first and only female mayor, served from 1987 to 1993 and led a fight against a proposed garbage incinerator. She established strong ties with the University of Maryland and supported the city's independent living facility for seniors and persons with disabilities, Attick Towers. Anna Latta Owens will be remembered fondly by those who knew her, as she touched many lives and improved them with her measures.

36796.ai_summary: The City of College Park has introduced a new composting program, making it easier for residents to recycle food waste through inexpensive compost containers and free weekly collections. As stated, 'composting is one of the easiest and most productive things individuals can do for the environment' and can help combat climate change by reducing methane production in landfills. The program allows residents to compost a wide range of materials, including food scraps, paper towels, and pizza boxes, and provides resources for participants to obtain compostable bags and containers.

key_people: Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
36528.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved a new district map, the October Plan 1 map, during their meeting on November 15. Councilmembers Susan Whitney, Maria Mackie, Denise Mitchell, and Kate Kennedy supported the map, with Whitney noting that the alternative Plan 3A map would have unevenly distributed the city's civic associations, saying 'The fact that so many civic associations would be concentrated in District 2 really shows an imbalance.' Mackie also expressed that 'Although I'll be very sad to lose those two neighborhoods in my district, this isn't just about me — this is about all of College Park.' The new map will be in effect prior to the November 2023 city election.

key_people: Tyrone Wells, Operations Manager of the Prince George’s County Office of Emergency Management
organizations: Prince George’s County Office of Emergency Management
key_issues: support for senior citizens and homebound individuals
36357.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed emergency management with Tyrone Wells, the operations manager of the Prince George’s County Office of Emergency Management, who presented the recently updated County Emergency Operations Plan. Councilmembers expressed concerns about the county's efforts, with Councilmember John Rigg stating 'Citizens in my community are wondering why they don’t hear from the county more often' and Councilmember Maria Mackie noting 'A lot of our senior citizens don’t even own a phone'. The councilmembers suggested the county could improve its communication with residents, particularly during emergencies, and proposed ideas such as maintaining a registry of city residents who may need special assistance.

key_people: Gale Mamatova, Vice President of the North College Park Community Association
36359.ai_summary: The City of College Park is discussing redistricting, which has been a heated topic among residents. According to Gale Mamatova, the city needs to improve its approach to community engagement and access to information, and invest in hiring multilingual staff to address inclusivity. Mamatova emphasizes that city leaders must 'listen with intent' and not just hear residents' concerns in order to effectively lead the city through this change.

key_people: Kathy Bryant, President of the Old Town Civic Association
Jordan Dewar, Vice-Chair of the Redistricting Commission
Robert Day, Chair of the Redistricting Commission
36067.ai_summary: The College Park City Council's redistricting debate continued, with residents and councilmembers questioning the Redistricting Commission about their proposals, which would separate the four core District 3 neighborhoods. Kathy Bryant said, '[The commission] kept saying ‘we follow the numbers,’ but it’s disregarding long term neighborhood connections that I feel are very important.' Councilmember Fazlul Kabir emphasized the importance of keeping districts intact, stating 'People don’t really differentiate between the boundaries of these neighborhoods; they all think as one cohesive community.'

36180.ai_summary: College Park Day, the city's signature celebration, is returning on Oct. 15 after a 2-year hiatus, with about 150 organizations participating in the event. Councilmember Denise Mitchell recalled that College Park Day grew out of discussions among Councilmember Fazlul Kabir, Mayor Patrick Wojahn, and a small group of residents. The event will feature local food vendors, live entertainment, including Gymkana, a gymnastic and acrobatics troupe at the University of Maryland, and the Wild Anacostias, who will 'bring our boogy to College Park Day,' said band leader Shawn Sharifi.

key_people: Lisbeth M Melendez Rivera, Ward 2 Candidate
35987.ai_summary: Emily Strab has won a tight three-way race to fill the position of city councilmember for Ward 2, receiving 280 votes. The Ward 2 seat was vacant after former Ward 2 councilmember Robert Croslin was elected mayor in a June special mayoral election following former mayor Kevin Ward’s death in January. Strab will take the oath of office in King Park on Thursday, Oct 20, at 6 p.m.

key_people: Lee Sommer, College Park Airport Manager
David Dorsch, Chair of the College Park Airport Authority
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
key_issues: New commuter program by Tailwind Air
Noise concerns from new air traffic
35792.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed a new commuter program run by Tailwind Air, which plans to launch four daily flights between College Park Airport and New York City’s Skyport Marina. As David Dorsch, chair of the College Park Airport Authority, noted, 'The noise is generally the helicopters and the ones that are the worst are the military, because they’re the biggest and heaviest,' and the planes in Tailwind’s fleet are quieter than those aircraft. The airport would need to change its operating regulations to allow Tailwind to maintain their proposed flight schedules, and the council was due to revisit this issue during their Sept. 13 worksession.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Shawn Joseph, Interim Superintendent of Schools
Judith White, PGCPS Chief Academic Officer
Alexandra Tyukavina, Paint Branch PTA Secretary
organizations: Prince George’s County Board of Education
51753.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to send a letter to the Prince George’s County Board of Education in support of continued funding for the Chinese immersion program at Paint Branch Elementary School. The letter asks the board to fund the full program for all Paint Branch students, stating 'The program has become an important part of the broader educational ecosystem serving College Park residents.' Some Paint Branch parents have objected to the proposed changes, with PTA Secretary Alexandra Tyukavina calling the proposals 'inequitable' and proposing an alternative solution to address overstaffing concerns.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Sami Saeed, public policy major at the University of Maryland
David Dorsch, College Park resident and landlord
Nick DiSpirito, UMD's student liaison to the city council
51534.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved an ordinance that prevents landlords from asking tenants to renew a lease sooner than 180 days from the end of the current lease. The ordinance, which will fine landlords $500 for the first offense and $2,500 for subsequent offenses, was introduced in September and drew comments from over 20 tenants. According to Sami Saeed, a public policy major at the University of Maryland, 'The stress that that caused me … trying to find anyone to live with for next year. I had to call 50 people. … That is the situation renters are faced with. That is the situation I was faced with. It happened to me.'

key_people: Donald Hays, chairman of the BPAC subcommittee
organizations: College Park’s Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee (BPAC)
51397.ai_summary: A citizen-led committee, the College Park’s Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee (BPAC), resubmitted a year-old report on school bus stop safety to the College Park City Council in January, recommending upgrades to protect elementary school children. The report graded 75 bus stops and identified seven that scored low on safety, with recommendations including adding bike lanes and installing a sidewalk. As committee chairman Donald Hays said, 'I personally feel that is an outrage and there has been very little movement and very little progress,' particularly at the bus stop at St. Andrews Place and Duke Street, which he believes 'continues to be the most dangerous place for elementary school children to wait for their bus that could be fixed tomorrow.'

key_people: Maxine Gross, member of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project and fifth-generation Lakeland resident
51400.ai_summary: The Lakeland neighborhood in College Park is planning to add 10 murals to sidewalks, storm drains, fences, retaining walls, and paths throughout the community to highlight its history. As Maxine Gross, a member of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project, stated, 'It's just a way for folks to be able to see the community that we have been talking about for a number of years.' The project has a total budget of $28,382, with $12,650 from a 2022 grant from the Maryland Heritage Areas Authority and $15,732 of city funds, and is a joint effort among city officials, community members, and University of Maryland partners.

organizations: UMD Partnership for Action Learning in Sustainability Program
51390.ai_summary: Three UMD students, Frances Chen, Nicholas Crane, and Ashna Pandya, presented to the College Park City Council on ways to ease neighborhood tensions near the university. They suggested that the city and university could do more to help undergraduate tenants become better neighbors, citing the issue of 'studentification' of established neighborhoods, which can degrade a community's quality of life, with Chen stating 'There has … been an issue with neighborhood degradation, with many students and party houses creating a lot of trash as well as general noise complaints within neighborhoods.' Councilmember Alan Hew emphasized that the university should take more responsibility in teaching its students how to be good neighbors.

51376.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering expanding permit parking in residential neighborhoods, with a study to determine whether to restrict parking on additional streets. Parking Enforcement Officer Jim Miller proposed the study, suggesting that permit-only parking could help address overcrowding in homes by limiting permits to homeowners and tenants whose names appear on a lease, saying 'Where are they going to park their car now, because they are not on a lease agreement, they are not listed as [the] owner-occupied property owner.' The council discussed the proposal, with former Councilmember Susan Whitney expressing concerns that paid street parking could lead to less green space and increased stormwater runoff, and suggesting the city offer incentives to encourage residents to own fewer cars, saying 'We want people who need their cars to be able to park them, and we want people who can get by with one car to do that and to be encouraged to do that.'

51441.ai_summary: The College Park City Council agreed to recommend that Prince George’s County amend a parking ordinance to allow a developer to build a four-story, 87-unit apartment building for older residents on Branchville Road. The project, called Branchville Crossing, will be a senior affordable housing complex with elevators, social spaces, and amenities that appeal to senior citizens, said Matthew Tedesco, a lawyer representing Cruz Development Corp. “There is a real need for senior housing in College Park,” said Councilmember Kelly Jordan, adding that the developer has listened to the residents to see what would fit in the area and what’s needed.

key_people: Tom Dernoga, Prince George’s County Councilmember
51373.ai_summary: Maryland Governor Wes Moore has proposed a bill to change the state's zoning law, allowing for more dense housing construction, which has sparked division among state officials representing College Park. House Speaker Joseline Peña-Melnyk supports the proposal, stating 'Maryland has a housing shortage of roughly 100,000 homes... When people can’t afford to live here, we lose workers. We lose families. And we lose the economic growth that allows us to fund schools, transportation and public safety.' In contrast, State Senator Jim Rosapepe and Prince George’s County Councilmember Tom Dernoga have expressed concerns about the proposal, with Dernoga calling it a 'sprawl multiplier' that will worsen congestion and strain public safety resources.

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Erica Johnson, Sgt. and Coordinator of the Prince George’s County Police Department’s Citizens’ Police Academy
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
51366.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember Alan Hew has graduated from three different citizen police academies, where he learned how officers do their jobs and create relationships with members of the community.
