Hew said, 'There’s a lot of speculation of how they operate and I wanted to be able to speak confidently in support of the departments that operate within our city.' According to Hew, the training has helped him contribute to council discussions about safety and has given him a chance to network with police officers, allowing him to pass information along to his constituents about issues such as a recent surge in graffiti around the city.

51316.ai_summary: A winter storm named Fern brought snow, sleet, and freezing rain to the Route 1 corridor, prompting local jurisdictions to mount snow removal and emergency response efforts. Prince George’s County is operating in 'Full Snow Operation Mode' and urging residents to avoid driving until roadways have been cleared, with County Executive Aisha Braveboy stating 'The county’s snow response operation is fully active. Crews are working around the clock to clear and treat the roadways and public safety remains are top priority.' College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir reminded residents to report icy sidewalks and unsafe conditions to the city and to check when their streets were last plowed through an interactive map.

key_people: Tina Nguyen, Queens Chapel Barbershop Owner
Joshua Solomon, College Park City Councilmember (Ward 5)
Kelson Nisbett, College Park City Councilmember (Ward 5)
Laura Lanham, Hyattsville Deputy Police Chief
Merrill Hartson, Customer and Retired AP News Editor
51173.ai_summary: The Queens Chapel Barbershop celebrated its 85th anniversary with a party attended by customers, city council members Joshua Solomon and Kelson Nisbett, and Hyattsville Police Chief Jarod Towers. Merrill Hartson, a customer and retired editor of AP News, gave poetical remarks, saying 'For 85 years, the Queens Chapel Barbershop has persevered, never short of sheers, never at a loss for cheers' and noting that 'everyone knows your name' at the barbershop, similar to the TV show Cheers. The event highlighted the barbershop's loyal customer base and its significance in the community.

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Alex Posorske, Veo's policy and partnerships manager for the East Coast
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
51475.ai_summary: Veo, the company that owns e-scooters and e-bikes in College Park, is cracking down on parking issues by charging riders extra for parking outside designated areas. As Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember, said, 'This is just almost malicious,' regarding a rider who left a Veo e-scooter in the middle of a sidewalk. Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated, 'We are very happy that we have this micromobility system in College Park because this is in line with our strategic sustainability goal,' and noted that 'Every time someone rides a Veo scooter, that person is not using the car, right? So we’re taking out the cars from the street.'

51408.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has become younger with the election of three newcomers, including Daniel Oates, Kelly Jordan, and Holly Simmons, who are all under 50. As Oates stated, 'To me what’s interesting is less [about] the relative ages than the new people coming on the council and bringing different experiences and different perspectives from that vein.' The newcomers believe their fresh perspectives will help the council think about the future of the city, and issues like rising utility bills, school quality, and safety will be a focus for them, with Jordan noting 'we’ll all have lots of questions and … when you’re used to something, comfortable with something, new questions can bring new ideas for how you’re used to looking at it.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
organizations: U.S. Bureau of City Labor Statistics
key_issues: Job losses due to federal government shutdown and policy changes
Impact on local economy and residents
51404.ai_summary: A year into President Donald Trump's second term, College Park is dealing with the consequences of executive orders, employee layoffs, and funding cuts, affecting residents and the local economy. Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated, 'We do not know how things will actually go in the future,' and 'Our policies will stay as used to be, and we will be supporting the community in any way we can.' The city has increased its financial assistance program budget to $100,000 to help residents affected by job losses, with Kabir noting that 'Neighbors have had to take jobs that they are not trained for, and with much lower pay.'

key_people: Joseline Peña-Melnyk, Speaker of the Maryland House of Delegates
Adrienne A. Jones, Former Speaker of the Maryland House of Delegates
organizations: U.S. Attorney’s Office for the District of Columbia
House Health and Government Operations Committee
50899.ai_summary: Joseline Peña-Melnyk, a College Park resident and former College Park City Councilmember, was sworn in as the 109th Speaker of the Maryland House of Delegates, becoming the second woman speaker and the first Afro-Latina to hold that position. Peña-Melnyk said, 'My journey did not begin in these chambers. It began in the Dominican Republic in a small wooden house with a tin roof, one with holes where the rain will come through. When it rained, my family would place pots and pans around the house to catch the falling drops.' She also stated, 'This moment belongs not just to me, but to every child catching rain in a pot.' As speaker, Peña-Melnyk aims to collaborate with both Democrats and Republicans, calling herself an 'inclusive' leader.

key_people: Edouard Haba, Association President and Hyattsville City Council member
Monica Casañas, PGCMA Vice President and Mayor of Colmar Manor
50826.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has paused its membership in the Prince George’s County Municipal Association (PGCMA) after a former treasurer allegedly embezzled over $30,000. Councilmembers will reconsider membership once the association creates a security plan to prevent future thefts. As Councilmember Jacob Hernandez said, 'It’s our taxpayer money... Why would we give money to an organization that is clearly lacking integrity?'

key_people: Carly Zapernick, Managing Director of Construction and Development for Article Student Living
Julie Bringman, Pastor of Hope Lutheran Church
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park Councilmember
50763.ai_summary: College Park officials pressed Article Student Living, a Chicago-based student housing developer, to include affordable units in a proposed 192-unit complex on Guilford Drive. As Pastor Julie Bringman said, the new space will be larger than the old church and 'fully accessible', and Mayor Fazlul Kabir encouraged the developer to use the city's tax incentive program, noting 'the catch is that you must have affordable housing in it'. The development has raised concerns among councilmembers, including Denise Mitchell, who stated 'we have a vision moving forward, and we're not going to negate that vision'.

key_people: Nisha Kumar, University of Maryland student
Scott Tebbe, University of Maryland student
Lisa Miller, president of the Prince George’s Property Owners Association
Nick DiSpirito, UMD student liaison to the council
Amira Abujuma, UMD student liaison to the council
50602.ai_summary: Nearly 30 people, mostly University of Maryland students, asked the College Park City Council to prevent landlords from pressuring tenants to renew leases before they have lived in their units for six months. Students like Nisha Kumar and Scott Tebbe shared their experiences of being asked to re-sign leases early, with Kumar saying 'We’ve just moved in for the semester and we already have to figure out next year... Who’s going to take my spot? … Will I have to pay over $1,000 a month for a place I’m not even living in?' Landlords, including Richard Biffle, opposed the proposed ordinance, with some arguing that limiting early lease renewals would harm their business and others saying that students benefit from being able to secure housing early.

key_people: Samantha McLoughlin, Parent Teacher Organization (PTO) President
Carl Franceski, general manager of sales and operations for Coca-Cola Consolidated
50509.ai_summary: The local bottler for Coca-Cola gave each of Hollywood Elementary School’s 301 students new sneakers, socks and school supplies on Nov. 12 through Coca-Cola Consolidated’s Back-to-School with Purpose initiative. As Principal Carlos Johnson said, 'When you can provide for a parent and the things that they need at home, school and home become one.' City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez praised Johnson's efforts, saying 'He’s gone above and beyond to make sure he’s providing meaningful outreach.'

key_people: Charlotte Davis, Staff Member at Attick Towers
Glenn Reniere, Engineer at Vitro Corp.
50310.ai_summary: Helen Reniere, a resident of Attick Towers, celebrated her 100th birthday and received an official proclamation from the College Park mayor and City Council, congratulating her on reaching the milestone. Reniere said, 'I get up in the morning, I take my medicine and … I guess you got to put Jesus first.' She also values her friendship with Charlotte Davis, a staff member at the apartment complex, who she says is 'the best thing that ever happened to me'.

organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC)
50276.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has endorsed the construction of a bridge connecting Old Calvert Road Park to the Northeast Branch Trail, with Councilmember Ray Ranker stating 'We don’t want to spend another month waiting, We want to get [construction] started … tomorrow.' The proposed bridge is estimated to cost around $2 million and would provide a safe route for residents to access the trail. The council considered an alternative option to modify the Campus Drive bridge, but Ranker noted that this option is not preferred by residents and would still pose safety risks for cyclists.

key_people: Gavin Neubauer, University of Maryland public policy student and task force member
Christina Martin Kenny, College Park Estates resident and task force member
organizations: College Park Child Care Task Force
50298.ai_summary: The College Park Child Care Task Force proposed ways to lower child care costs, including offering grants to home-based providers and property tax credits to established centers. According to task force member Gavin Neubauer, 'If you’re able to even increase the amount of businesses here that can survive and care for children, we’re going to be a real standout city in this entire metropolitan area.' Task force member Christina Martin Kenny noted that 'In my own community, the options for child care in the city of College Park are neither affordable nor accessible,' highlighting the need for the city to address this issue.

key_people: Jatinder Khokhar, Director of Public Services
50304.ai_summary: The College Park City Council introduced an ordinance to increase fines for city code violations, including construction, fire safety, housing, noise, and unruly social gatherings. The proposed changes aim to align with a Maryland law that allows municipalities to fine up to $5,000 for municipal infractions. As City Attorney Stephanie Anderson said, 'We are not the highest but we want to make sure that the types of violations that are occurring are taken seriously,' and Councilmember John Rigg noted that 'Noise should be bad, but unruly social gatherings almost always include noise and it includes a lot more.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
50292.ai_summary: The November 4 election in College Park marked the first time 16- and 17-year-olds were allowed to cast ballots in city elections, with six teenagers voting. Mayor Fazlul Kabir won 1,480 votes, and the top vote-getters for council seats were District 1 incumbents Jacob Hernandez and Alan Hew. A total of 1,598 people voted, accounting for 9.9% of registered voters in College Park.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Marc Lore, Entrepreneur and Founder of Wonder
Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director
50876.ai_summary: The food hall chain Wonder has opened its first Maryland location in College Park on Baltimore Avenue, featuring takeout and delivery from over 15 restaurants. According to College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, 'The city has been working hard, and our economic development staff in particular have been working hard to try to really bring an exciting concept here,' and 'We want them to be successful, and we want them to be here for a long time.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'It’s a milestone and College Park is very lucky to have them,' as the city aims to bring new businesses and revitalize its downtown area.

key_people: Holly Simmons, College Park City Councilmember
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Chris Gill, College Park City Council candidate
Daniel Oates, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
50191.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has three new members after voters in Districts 2 and 3 elected newcomers to the dais on Nov. 4. Holly Simmons and Kelly Jordan won a three-way election for two District 2 seats, while Daniel Oates won the seat in District 3. As Jordan stated, 'It's a good feeling, I'm happy to be able to serve the residents and excited about the next two years,' and Simmons said her first action will be to 'listen and learn.'

key_people: Brencis D. Smith, former council member
Holly Simmons, College Park City Council candidate
Christopher Gill, College Park City Council candidate
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Council candidate
50185.ai_summary: The results of the local elections in College Park are uncertain, with some races being very close. In College Park District 2, Holly Simmons seems likely to have secured one of two seats on the council, while Christopher Gill and Kelly Jordan are only one vote apart for the second seat. The final results will be certified after mail-in and provisional ballots are counted.

key_people: Kelly Jordan, District 2 council candidate and co-president of the Berwyn District Civic Association (BDCA)
Holly Simmons, District 2 council candidate and deputy zoning manager for the city of Rockville
key_issues: restorative justice for the Lakeland community
50017.ai_summary: The three candidates for two vacant College Park City Council seats in District 2, Chris Gill, Kelly Jordan, and Holly Simmons, said restorative justice for the Lakeland community and affordable housing will be their priorities if they are elected. As Jordan stated, 'I'm just really pleased to be able to serve the community,' and Simmons emphasized the importance of reparations for the Lakeland community, recalling a gentleman's statement, 'I want my 40 acres and a mule.' The candidates are running to fill the seats of former councilmembers Llatetra Brown Esters and Susan Whitney, with incumbent Mayor Fazlul Kabir running unopposed.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
49848.ai_summary: College Park voters will elect a mayor and eight City Councilmembers in November. Fazlul Kabir, the current mayor, is running for re-election and has led efforts to 'reimagine College Park by strengthening the city’s finances, opening new community spaces and welcoming businesses that created jobs.' Kabir has also worked to make neighborhoods 'safer, more walkable and better connected, while also embracing and celebrating the city’s diversity.'

key_people: Ray Ranker, District 3 City Council Candidate
Dan Oates, District 3 City Council Candidate
49703.ai_summary: District 3 City Council candidates Ray Ranker and Dan Oates discussed their plans to improve public safety and enhance resident engagement at a recent forum. Ranker emphasized the importance of community, saying 'I just love our people... The people are what make College Park great.' Both candidates oppose creating an independent police force, instead suggesting alternative measures such as increasing security cameras or blue light phones, with Oates stating 'I think jumping to the answer of 'we need a police force' is premature.'

key_people: Christopher Gill, IT Project Manager and City Council Candidate
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
49536.ai_summary: Three candidates, Christopher Gill, Kelly Jordan, and Holly Simmons, are running for two District 2 seats on the College Park City Council. Gill, an IT project manager, aims to address housing affordability and improve walkability and trail connections, stating 'I don't want people to be pushed out of their community.' Jordan's campaign focuses on representation, ensuring that diverse interests are heard and incorporated into decision-making. The election will take place on November 4.

key_people: Kelly Jordan, District 2 city council candidate
organizations: Prince George's County Board of Elections
49533.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted 5-3 to lower the voting age for city elections from 18 to 16, making College Park the ninth Maryland city to allow teens to vote. As 16-year-old Lucas Rigg said, 'I think it lets the people who didn't choose to live here have a say.' Lovisa Jordan, 16, noted that preparing to vote could make teenagers more aware of what's going on in the city and the candidates who want to run it, saying 'I think anyone can be uninformed and anyone can be informed, especially 16 and up.'

key_people: Danny Copeland, Vice President for RST Development
Orlando Velez, Director of Operations and Housing Initiatives
David Varnado, Senior Program and Operations Manager
organizations: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)
locations: College Park–University of Maryland Metro Station
49529.ai_summary: The city of College Park offers affordable housing options, including the Flats at College Park, a 317-unit apartment complex with income-restricted units, and the College Park Community Preservation Trust, which buys and sells single-family homes to low-income buyers. According to Danny Copeland, Vice President for RST Development, the Flats at College Park was financed in part through the federal Low-Income Housing Tax Credit program. The city also offers programs like Live+Work College Park, which provides zero-interest loans to full-time employees who purchase a home in the city, as noted by David Varnado, Senior Program and Operations Manager.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Chair of the Restorative Justice Commission
50287.ai_summary: A city-appointed committee, the Restorative Justice Commission, has urged the College Park City Council to address the harm done to the Lakeland neighborhood during urban renewal in the 1970s and 80s by offering financial compensation, housing priority, and free college tuition. As Maxine Gross, the chair of the commission, said, 'When you talk to people who experienced Lakeland before urban renewal, they speak of a vibrant community, a place for caring people who work together to meet everyone’s needs.' The commission's recommendations aim to return former residents to Lakeland and provide them with benefits such as a right of first refusal policy for real estate and a Lakeland Legacy Scholars Program for free tuition.

51542.ai_summary: College Park officials are considering annexing multiple properties, including the soon-to-be-vacant Beltsville Agricultural Research Center, to expand the city's boundaries. As Councilmember Jacob Hernandez stated, 'We're kind of just barely in the initial stages of talking about it... We're trying to take a common-sense approach to every single one of these areas.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir emphasized that the city would consider annexing land to protect it, for economic development, or to boost population, and said 'It is a residents-driven process' that requires input from residents, property owners, and government officials.

key_people: Ryna Quiñones, Communications and Events Manager
Connor Johnson, Former College Park Resident
Jhonny Ayquipa, Trumpet Player for La Rumbera Mayor
49215.ai_summary: College Park hosted its second annual Bailamos event to celebrate Hispanic Heritage Month, with nearly 200 residents and visitors attending the celebration at the College Park Woods Clubhouse. Councilmember Jacob Hernandez said, 'I think, when you break bread, when you sit down with one another, and you have that opportunity to, no kidding, just engage, it’s a whole other world.' Attendees like Danielle Delgado noted the importance of having such events, stating 'now is the moment where we should be proud, and whether or not the Latino community feels safe enough to come out, it’s great to see that people of other communities and cultures are here to support us.'

key_people: Teresa Way-Pezzuti, Director of Human Resources
49170.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering a proposal to double the part-time salaries of councilmembers from $10,000 to $20,000 a year, as well as increase the mayor's salary from $15,000 to $25,000 and the mayor pro tem's from $12,500 to $22,500. 'Everyone on this dais works incredibly hard for the residents that we care for,' said Councilmember Jacob Hernandez, and the proposed salary increase 'might not be extraordinarily significant, but it does help cover the costs.' 'We do it for the love and passion of being part of the community,' added Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell, noting that the council has raised its compensation only twice during her eight terms.

key_people: Miriam Bader, Director of Planning and Community Development
49106.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to require owners of vacant homes and commercial properties to register with the city, with a $25 registration fee and fines for non-compliance. According to College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, 'Vacant properties can negatively impact our community... they can become safety hazards, attract vandalism and contribute to blight.' City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez agreed, stating 'It supports improving public safety... it protects property values.' The ordinance aims to track vacant properties and ensure compliance with maintenance standards.

key_people: Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
49102.ai_summary: A city-commissioned noise analysis by RK&K found that traffic noise on Edmonston Road in College Park's Yarrow neighborhood is 'clearly audible' and warrants further study and potential mitigation by the State Highway Administration. Resident Maria Cordone said, 'I'm not surprised; I've been living it... The annoyance of the noise is significant and … it does disturb our quality of life.' College Park City Councilmember Ray Ranker stated, 'We're not waiting for the results of' further study, and the city is preparing to plant 80-plus trees and shrubs between Edmonston Road and Kenilworth Avenue to help with sound and beautification.

Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember

49098.ai_summary: Five first-time candidates and five incumbents have filed to run for seats on the College Park City Council in the Nov. 4 election. Mayor Fazlul Kabir will run unopposed for re-election. The election will fill seats in Districts 1, 2, 3, and 4, with some incumbents running without opposition, while District 2 has four candidates competing for two council seats.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
49093.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, who is also a volunteer paramedic, disagreed with his colleagues on the use of speed humps as a traffic-calming measure on Autoville Drive, citing his experience with patients in ambulances and the pain caused by speed bumps. Rigg, who has volunteered for 16 years with Calvert Advanced Life Support (CALS), said 'I just closely identify with being a paramedic' and that his expertise in emergency health care has influenced many of his decisions as a city official. As a paramedic, Rigg knows 'from direct experience how painful it is for patients to be in an ambulance and bumping over speed bumps.'

key_people: Michelle Johnson, Executive Director of the Housing Authority of the City of College Park
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: Housing Authority of the City of College Park
U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)
49087.ai_summary: The lack of affordable housing in College Park is causing some residents, like Nausicaä Sinclair, to move away due to soaring rent prices. As Michelle Johnson, executive director of the Housing Authority of the City of College Park, stated, 'affordable housing is a human right' and 'to me, affordable housing is being able to go to work, come home, pay your bills, pay your rent, without stress.' College Park City Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters emphasized the importance of diversity and affordable housing, saying 'no matter how much money you make, you should be able to come in this community and find housing, housing that is suitable and housing that is quality.'

key_people: Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
49076.ai_summary: Three College Park City Councilmembers, Llatetra Brown Esters, Susan Whitney, and John Rigg, have decided not to run for re-election due to the demands of the part-time job interfering with their family and personal time. As Rigg stated, 'My falls are so busy,' and Esters noted that being a councilmember can be 'taxing' to 'be on' all the time. The issue of burnout has been acknowledged by other councilmembers, including Ray Ranker, who advises being 'realistic about how much time and energy you have,' and Mayor Fazlul Kabir, who emphasizes the importance of setting priorities to avoid burnout.

key_people: Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember
Tom Dernoga, Prince George’s County Councilmember
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Robert Thurston, President of the Lakeland Civic Association
key_issues: shutdown of the Beltsville Agricultural Research Center (BARC)
49021.ai_summary: County Executive Aisha Braveboy met with local officials in College Park as part of her 'Elevate Prince George’s' tour, where she heard concerns about the shutdown of the Beltsville Agricultural Research Center (BARC) and issues related to land use, code enforcement, and affordable housing. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'There are 2,600 people who [work at BARC], many of them live in College Park and the surrounding communities... and their livelihoods are at stake, but also, more importantly, many of them, when they work there, they also attend the businesses in College Park.' Braveboy stated, 'I’m learning a lot about our county... My goal is to increase our commercial tax base. That’s very important, but we want to do that responsibly.'

key_people: Daniel Cruz Jr., Senior Vice President of Cruz Development
Arelis Pérez, Chair of the College Park Housing Authority board
Sandy Lora, Branchville Volunteer Fire Co. Ladies Auxiliary
48941.ai_summary: A Boston-based development company, Cruz Development Corp., has proposed an 87-unit affordable senior housing project at Branchville Road and University Boulevard, sparking concerns from College Park residents and officials about affordability, noise, and parking. Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell said, 'It seems like the rate of rent is still high for seniors on subsidized incomes,' and Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters asked what the developers plan to do about noise from University Boulevard. The project qualified for a 4% federal tax credit for affordable housing, and tenants will pay based on their incomes, with prices reflecting 60% of the area median income for Prince George's County.

key_people: Amira Abujuma, Deputy Student Liaison to the College Park City Council
48774.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has been urged to adopt an ordinance banning software that allows apartment developers to collude on rent prices, with Amira Abujuma stating 'Housing in College Park is not a luxury. It’s a necessity.' The council agreed to send a letter to state legislators supporting possible legislative action against RealPage and other software companies. The issue was brought to the council's attention by residents and a former UMD Student Government Association officer, with Councilmember John Rigg noting that a few large landlords own multiple properties in the city, increasing the 'odd[s] of collusion and upward price pressure.'

key_people: Charkera Ervin, Legal and Policy Director for the African American Redress Network
Keith Webster, Commission Member and Descendant of one of Lakeland's original property owners
organizations: Braxton Institute for Sustainability, Resilience and Joy
48751.ai_summary: Restorative justice advocates gathered in College Park's Lakeland neighborhood to present a report urging the city to help the community improve education, transportation, and housing. The report, compiled by the African American Redress Network and the Braxton Institute for Sustainability, Resilience and Joy, details the history and impact of urban renewal on Lakeland, and recommends free college tuition, public transportation passes, and compensation for homes lost during urban renewal in the 1960s and '70s. As Keith Webster, a commission member and descendant of one of Lakeland's original property owners, said, 'Let's resurrect the spirit of Lakeland, the Lakeland that we once knew.'

48748.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering stricter parking enforcement measures, including hiring more enforcement officers, reducing the number of free permits per household, and charging residents to park on their streets. Councilmembers, such as Susan Whitney, suggested extending the hours of enforcement and offering incentives to one-car households, saying 'We want people who need their cars to be able to park them, and we want people who can get by with one car, we want to encourage them to do that.' The council is weighing the idea of charging taxpayers to park in front of their homes, with Maria Mackie noting 'Nobody likes permit parking,' but 'We need to realize this is a quality-of-life issue. It’s a safety issue.'

key_people: Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
48745.ai_summary: Three College Park City Councilmembers, Llatetra Brown Esters, Susan Whitney, and John Rigg, have announced they will not seek re-election in November. They cited increasing family and work obligations as reasons for their decision, with Rigg stating he wants to 'place a much higher priority on … family events, see my friends and neighbors more often and spend time on personal pursuits.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir said their departure will be a loss for the council, but also an opportunity for new residents to run, saying 'if the same people keep running, those who are interested, they don’t get a chance. It’s very hard to run against incumbents.'

key_people: Ryna Quiñones, College Park Communications and Events Manager
August Kenner, University of Maryland Police Captain
Rex Powell, College Park Woods Neighborhood Watch Coordinator
Alexis Howlett, Catholic University Graduate Student
48698.ai_summary: The city of College Park held a National Night Out event on August 5, where residents met with police officers and city officials to focus on public safety and build a safer community. As College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'The whole goal of National Night Out is to get the community connected with the law enforcement who try to keep us safe.' The event, which featured snacks, music, and a bounce house for kids, attracted a diverse crowd of dozens, including residents who appreciated the chance to build stronger bonds with their community, with one resident noting 'It just makes you feel safer to know who’s actually working down there at the fire station, or who’s sitting in the police car around the corner.'

key_people: Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
48651.ai_summary: Both District 2 College Park City Councilmembers, Llatetra Brown Esters and Susan Whitney, announced they will not run for re-election in November, citing increasing demands on their time from their full-time jobs and families. Esters stated, 'My need to be present for family has become a priority,' and Whitney wrote, 'My work and family responsibilities have increased substantially since the last election, and I no longer have the time and energy to provide the level of service District 2 residents deserve.' This announcement, along with Councilmember John Rigg's similar decision, will impact the upcoming city council elections in November.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Stuart Adams, former College Park City Councilmember
Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland student
48611.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember John Rigg announced he will not seek re-election in November, citing a desire to prioritize family events and personal pursuits. Rigg, who has served for eight years, noted that running a campaign every other year is 'tremendously expensive, mentally taxing and time-consuming,' and urged the newly elected council to push for a change to four-year terms for elected officials, which he believes would 'continue a common-sense approach to alleviating the substantial personal burden imposed by our current system of elections every other year.' Rigg endorsed his colleague Ray Ranker for re-election in November.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Armando Camacho, Vice Chair of Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners
Daniel Oates, Calvert Hills Citizens Association President
organizations: Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners
locations: Calvert Hills Playground and Athletic Field
University of Maryland Child Development Center
key_issues: Location Near Day Care Center and Playground
48606.ai_summary: The Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners voted 4-0 to deny a liquor store's application to reopen next to ZIPS Cleaners on Baltimore Avenue, citing its proximity to the University of Maryland Child Development Center. College Park City Councilmember John Rigg stated, 'This is unequivocally the wrong location, and the city council stands in unified opposition to it,' and Mayor Fazlul Kabir testified that the community has 'voiced very strong opposition and concerns' about the introduction of a full-service liquor store near the Calvert Hills playground and the day care center. The decision was made after the city council voted unanimously to send a letter of opposition to the county commissioners regarding the license transfer application.

48293.ai_summary: Former City Councilmember Bob Catlin remains civically involved in College Park, attending council meetings to share historical reflections and serving on various committees. Catlin, who represented District 2 from 1997 to 2013, said, 'I've always been interested in history and policies and why things work the way they do,' and believes understanding the past is necessary for making informed decisions. As a volunteer, Catlin continues to contribute to the city, including as co-president of the Berwyn District Civic Association, and Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters notes that 'he has been very involved for a long time and in different ways in the city, and I think that speaks to his appreciation for living here and wanting to be engaged.'

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Julie Johnson, UMD’s assistant director for intramural sports and the Reckord Armory
Ryna Quiñones, City Communications and Events Manager
Cat Peretti, University Park resident and volleyball team captain
48292.ai_summary: The city of College Park has partnered with the University of Maryland's RecWell program to offer social sports, including volleyball and pickleball, to its residents at a discounted rate. 'Anything that gets people out and moving their bodies is a good thing,' said College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, who plays on the program's volleyball team. The program aims to build community and encourage residents to engage in fun and active activities, with City Communications and Events Manager Ryna Quiñones stating, 'We’re happy to help reduce the barriers, to encourage our residents to not only meet their neighbors, but kind of engage in these fun and active activities.'

key_people: Donald Hay, co-chair of the College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee
organizations: College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee
48291.ai_summary: The College Park City Council will hold a public hearing on a proposal to make it a violation for cars to park or stand in bike lanes, as city officials have heard complaints from bicyclists about cars and delivery trucks blocking bike lanes. According to College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, the rule would be a “little inconvenient” for delivery drivers, while Councilmember Susan Whitney notes that the city can start changing the culture around bike lanes, saying “If we can’t do this here, why can’t we do this here? I see no reason why we can’t accomplish that in College Park.” The new law would not affect Route 1, which is owned and maintained by the state.

48237.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted 7-1 to purchase a 4.5-acre parking lot, known as the lower parcel, next to Driskell Park for $6.5 million. The land has been a point of contention since 2017, with Werrlein Properties attempting to develop it without required permits. As Ward 1 resident Sean Carlson stated, 'I find it galling that we would consider bailing out the developers who have not shown that they are capable of being good stewards of that land,' highlighting concerns about the city rewarding developers for misuse of the property.

48229.ai_summary: The U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) removed a list from its website that included College Park and Hyattsville as 'sanctuary jurisdictions' that are 'obstructing the enforcement of U.S. immigration laws.' According to the city, 'College Park has and will continue to be a welcoming and inclusive community... We remain steadfast in our commitment to the safety and well-being of all our residents.' The city stated that it complies with all laws and treats all residents justly and equally, and has not received any formal notification from DHS about its inclusion on the list.

key_people: Nicole Freeh, Highway Engineer and Yarrow Resident
47367.ai_summary: Residents of the Yarrow neighborhood, including Maria Cordone, are asking the city to plant noise-absorbing trees between Edmonston Road and Kenilworth Avenue to alleviate loud noise from the highway. Cordone stated, 'I can’t hear my neighbor talking to me,' and 'I can’t even sit out in the yard because of the noise.' The residents plan to meet with the city arborist to request the planting of large, native trees and may also ask the state to conduct a sound study to determine the best solution to the noise problem.

key_people: Kathryn Clay, PNC Senior Vice President
Qin Chih, Jumbo Jumbo Cafe Manager
47353.ai_summary: The City of College Park awarded $75,000 in grants to 15 local businesses through the Retail Reinforcement Mini-Grant Program, funded by PNC Bank, to help offset losses from the ongoing Purple Line construction. As Michael Williams, the city's economic development director, said, 'The money flew out the door' once the city announced the grant, and 'We knew people were hurting. We knew our merchants were really upset with the traffic diversions.' Businesses like Jumbo Jumbo Cafe and Laser Essential received grants to cover rent, equipment, and payroll costs, with Qin Chih, Jumbo Jumbo Cafe manager, stating 'It's very harmful' and 'It's really hard for us,' and Keith Bouchelion, Laser Essential co-owner, saying the grant is 'very well appreciated, especially during these turbulent times and uncertainty.'

key_people: Ethan Pham, CP Flea market founder
Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Gabrielle Brinkhoff, Shop Made in Maryland store manager
47336.ai_summary: City officials shut down a flea market on College Park’s City Hall plaza in March after it had operated for nearly a year without permission. College Park City Manager Kenny Young cited the lack of permission as the main reason for shutting down the market, stating 'From the city’s point of view, it’s a pretty simple issue for us: They were operating on the City Hall plaza without permission.' The market's founder, Ethan Pham, had been working with Shop Made in Maryland to host the market and was unaware of any issues before the shutdown, saying 'It just felt completely out of left field, like, I had no idea this was coming.'

key_people: Maxine Gross, Chair of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project
Mary Anne Hakes, Organizer of Explorations on Aging
47289.ai_summary: University of Maryland seniors are working with the Lakeland Community Heritage Project and Explorations on Aging to preserve the history of the Lakeland community and support local organizations. According to Maxine Gross, Chair of the heritage project, 'It is helpful for us to see what [the students are] thinking about, and … it’s helpful for them to see how the things that they learned about in a classroom actually work with real-life projects.' The students are creating a website for Explorations on Aging and designing plans for a '15-minute city' to improve accessibility in College Park.

47278.ai_summary: The College Park City Council will hold a public hearing on May 6 to discuss the proposed $29.6 million fiscal year 2026 budget, which would increase the budget by 0.9% over the previous year. The proposal adds three full-time staff members to City Hall but does not raise residential property taxes. The council must adopt a budget resolution later this month.

47284.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted 5-3 to lower the voting age for city elections from 18 to 16, starting with the November election. Councilmember John Rigg championed the measure, stating it is designed to boost the number of residents who vote in local elections and to instill a sense of civic duty in teenagers. Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell and Councilmembers Maria Mackie and Alan Hew opposed the change, with Mackie suggesting a youth advisory council instead and Hew proposing a resident vote on the issue.

key_people: Dhruvak Mirani, University of Maryland Student Government Association President and student liaison to the College Park City Council
Dhruvak Mirani, student member of the University System of Maryland’s Board of Regents
organizations: University System of Maryland’s Board of Regents
47268.ai_summary: Dhruvak Mirani, the 2023-24 University of Maryland student liaison to the College Park City Council, has been elected as the president of the school’s Student Government Association. Mirani, a government and politics major, helped create a pilot project for the city to give subsidies to student renters and also serves as a student member of the University System of Maryland’s Board of Regents. Mirani won a landslide victory in the April election, which drew 6,000 undergraduate voters, the highest since 2020.

key_people: Debbie Herbert, President of the College Park Moose Family Center Lodge #453
Robert Lucian, Junior Past President of the College Park Moose Family Center Lodge #453
organizations: College Park Moose Family Center Lodge #453
Iglesia de Dios Pentecostés, Misión el Buen Samaritano
Mekane Hiwot Medhane Alem Tigray Orthodox Tewahedo Church
47339.ai_summary: The College Park Moose Family Center Lodge #453 is preparing to relocate after 75 years due to financial difficulties and is struggling to find a new location. According to Debbie Herbert, the president of the lodge, 'I hate it because we wanted to get back our building' and 'We are saddened that we have to find another place, and it’s a shame that we can’t find anything in College Park.' Robert Lucian, the junior past president of the lodge, has appealed to the College Park City Council for help in finding a new location, stating 'I’m hoping that sometime very soon, we will find a place to go to and reorganize and keep our members having a place to go to.'

key_people: Ryna Quiñones, City Communications and Events Manager
47186.ai_summary: The 27th annual Maryland Day drew thousands to the University of Maryland, where College Park city officials and staff participated by handing out information about the city. 'It's building community, bringing people together in one place and showcasing how strong we are as a community,' said College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir. The event featured over 400 free, family-friendly activities and served as an opportunity for the city to connect with current and prospective students, as well as their families, to promote a sense of community and welcome in College Park.

key_people: Daniel Gruner, professor of entomology at the University of Maryland
Karin Burghardt, associate professor of entomology and extension specialist
Laura Rost, national coordinator of Bee City USA
46919.ai_summary: The city of College Park has waived the 12-inch limit for lawn height in April to support pollinators like bees and butterflies. As Daniel Gruner, a professor of entomology at the University of Maryland, said, 'Pollinators need floral resources in order to get the energy they need to build their broods up, especially early in the season.' By participating in No Mow April, residents can help provide these resources, and experts like Karin Burghardt and Laura Rost encourage adopting this approach as a long-term strategy for supporting pollinators.

organizations: Meals on Wheels of College Park
46928.ai_summary: County Councilmembers Thomas Dernoga and Eric Olson warned of an 'extremely difficult' budget cycle due to federal grant uncertainty and budget cuts, which could affect the county's $267 million in federal grant money. As Olson noted, 'The federal government is doing so much damage,' and 'Our president is trying to eliminate the Department of Education, where a lot of those grants originate from.' The city of College Park is not proposing a property tax increase in its upcoming budget, with a draft budget proposal totaling $29.6 million, and will add four full-time staff members, including a public safety manager.

key_people: Ryna Quiñones, City Communications and Events Manager
Robert Thurston, Lakeland Civic Association President
Daniel Oates, Calvert Hills Citizens Association President
46925.ai_summary: The City of College Park has started a text alert system to keep residents informed about events, news, and emergencies. According to Ryna Quiñones, the city's communications and events manager, 'We're always looking for new ways to reach our residents,' and the text alert system will help achieve this goal. As Daniel Oates, president of the Calvert Hills Citizens Association, noted, 'There's [previously] not a way that I'm aware of for the city to do the same kind of communication by text. So I welcome creating more awareness of what's going on in the community.'

key_people: Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Xue Ling, Brand Manager and Owner's Daughter
46916.ai_summary: The grand opening of Li Chun Café, owned by Hua Wang, was attended by dozens of community members, University of Maryland students, and local politicians, including College Park City Councilmember Maria Mackie. Mackie said of the café, 'It’s really a … wonderful addition and a really unique place,' and expressed her excitement. The café, located on the first floor of College Park City Hall, features a menu with meals from northeastern China and has been popular with customers, offering a unique dining experience for the community.

46869.ai_summary: City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez has been helping Spanish-speaking business owners in North College Park, including Fidel Alfaro, owner of Alfaro Barbershop, by providing them with information about the city in Spanish. Hernandez, who learned English as a second language, has made it a priority to engage both English and Spanish communities through a bilingual approach, and supported the hiring of a bilingual staff member to translate city documents into Spanish. As Alfaro said, 'He always had time to get here,' and with Hernandez's guidance, Alfaro's shop received $14,000 in economic development grants to spend on renovations.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Jatinder Khokhar, Director of Public Services
46866.ai_summary: College Park's city manager has proposed a $29.6 million budget for fiscal year 2026 that would not raise property taxes. The proposal includes an 8.5% increase over last year for employee salaries and benefits and adds three full-time staff members to city hall. As Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated, 'Increasing property taxes has never been popular,' and 'this year, thankfully, it’s not happening.'

46822.ai_summary: City officials in College Park are preparing for the economic fallout from recent federal employee layoffs, with Mayor Fazlul Kabir stating 'We are not really prepared for it' and 'We're really worried about their well being.' Economic Development Director Michael Williams notes that 'We're going to just see a slowdown in some of the retail activities' due to rising unemployment. The city is assessing the potential impact on residents and the city's fiscal year 2026 budget, with Councilmember John Rigg saying 'It's going to be a really tough time over the next couple of years in College Park'.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Michael Meadow, Lifelong Old Town Resident
46529.ai_summary: Ray Ranker, the new District 3 councilmember, took the oath of office on March 18 after winning the special election with 344 votes. Ranker said, 'There are just a lot of amazing people who are, in so many different ways, making our community stronger, more connected, better. I’m excited to build on that.' He plans to dive into city budget planning and reach out to his opponents, Michael Meadow and Gannon Sprinkle, to help move College Park forward together. Meadow stated, 'If we have a successful city councilmember, that benefits us all. If he succeeds, we all succeed in College Park.'

46499.ai_summary: College Park officials and community members gathered at Duvall Field for a groundbreaking ceremony, marking the start of the renovation project, which will turn the field into a seven-acre park for sports, entertainment, and community events. The project, which includes more than a dozen amenities, such as an amphitheater, pickleball and basketball courts, and a rugby pitch, is expected to be completed by 2026 and will cost $7.3 million. As Councilmember Jacob Hernandez said, 'I think of the future of Duvall Field … [and] I picture a place where seniors can gracefully age in place and just enjoy the largest park in the city and the fresh air that it’ll bring,' and Mayor Fazlul Kabir added, 'I’m really excited that the project is coming to a completion, I can’t wait to see that the construction is completed.'

key_people: Michael Harris, Tenant and Sophomore Business Finance Major at University of Maryland
46411.ai_summary: The 60-year-old University Club at College Park on Berwyn House Road will undergo a major renovation starting this summer, with upgrades to water and electrical systems, higher-end furnishings, and outdoor amenities. Matt Hurson of Cape Advisers said, 'This property was of interest to us because of its distress pricing, but the fact that it is a property that needs a lot of work,' and noted that the building will be empty as the leases of the 135-unit student housing complex’s tenants will expire in July. City Councilmember Susan Whitney expressed concern that the potential rent increase will exacerbate the problem of a lack of affordable housing in the city, stating, 'There is a problem with a lack of affordable housing in general in the city, but also specifically for students.'

key_people: Ray Ranker, UMD Chaplain and District 3 Councilmember
Michael Meadow, Lifelong Old Town Resident and Candidate
Gannon Sprinkle, UMD Student and Candidate
46379.ai_summary: Ray Ranker, a University of Maryland chaplain, has won the District 3 council seat in a special election, defeating lifelong Old Town resident Michael Meadow and UMD student Gannon Sprinkle. Ranker stated, 'I'm grateful that people entrusted me with this responsibility,' and plans to dive into city budget planning during his term, which will end in November. He aims to 'help reflect our city's values and hopes through how we spend our money, [and] to be good stewards of that process,' and will take the oath of office on March 18.

key_people: Gannon Sprinkle, Co-founder of the College Park Tenants Union
Dhruvak Mirani, Co-founder of the College Park Tenants Union and 2023-24 Student Liaison to the City Council
organizations: White House Council of Economic Advisers
46407.ai_summary: The College Park Tenants Union has urged the city council to investigate accusations of price-fixing by landlords of six large apartment complexes, citing the use of RealPage's algorithmic pricing features. According to Gannon Sprinkle, a co-founder of the union, 'Local involvement is necessary to protect tenants from predatory pricing, and we would urge the city council to investigate the nature of RealPage.' Maryland Delegate Julie Palakovich Carr noted that 'it was pretty troubling to me that renters were paying more in rent, really for no reason other than to fuel their landlords' profits,' and proposed legislation aims to prevent the use of algorithmic pricing by landlords in the state.

key_people: Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Xue Ling, Brand Manager and Owner's Daughter
46382.ai_summary: The Li Chun Café, owned by Hua Wang, held a grand opening ceremony on March 8, attended by College Park City Councilmember Maria Mackie and other community members. Mackie stated, 'It’s really a … wonderful addition and a really unique place,' and expressed excitement about the new café. The café features a menu with dishes from northeastern China, and has become popular among customers, with its chun bing wraps being a top item, according to Brand Manager Xue Ling, who said 'She really wants to pick a food item from her hometown to … bring its delicious flavors to College Park.'

key_people: Denise Mitchell, Mayor Pro Tem and City Councilmember
Anna Owens, Former Mayor of College Park
key_issues: female representation on the city council
46437.ai_summary: Female College Park city councilmembers, including Denise Mitchell, Maria Mackie, Susan Whitney, and Llatetra Brown Esters, reflect on their journey into politics, noting the challenges they faced as women in a male-dominated field. As Mitchell said, 'This is still a man's world in regard to politics,' and Whitney noted, 'Some people might be more skeptical of your abilities, perhaps because you're a woman.' The women agree that there is room for more female leaders in government and are working to increase representation.

key_issues: Storage of trash and recycling carts
46342.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has scheduled a public hearing for April 1 to discuss a proposed amendment to the city code that would forbid residents from keeping tables used for drinking games in their front and side yards. Councilmember John Rigg said, 'One thing we’ve always struggled with, one very specific piece of furniture, and that is a beer pong table... People really don’t appreciate drinking games next door … on their street when they are trying to encourage their children to have healthier approaches to alcohol than is exhibited by such drinking games.' Councilmember Jacob Hernandez questioned how much control the city should have over what residents keep in their yards, saying 'It is a targeted policy to curb [the] behavior of a particular group of people.'

key_people: Mary Anne Hakes, Resident and City Explorations on Aging Project Worker
46348.ai_summary: The College Park Woods Clubhouse opened to the public with a ribbon-cutting ceremony on March 6 after about a year of construction. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'This is a really, really wonderful time for us to celebrate. This is your second home. Please enjoy it.' Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell noted that the new community space fulfills part of the city’s strategic plan to bring College Park together, saying 'Having this is important, because engagement means inclusivity. And that means people can feel like they belong together.'

key_people: Sherry Felix, English teacher and Title I coordinator
46388.ai_summary: The College Park City Council granted $2,750 to High Point High School's Flying from the HP Nest program, which supplies care packages for students headed to college after graduation. The grant paid for care packages for 14 students and registration fees for 42 students to join the nonprofit First Generation College Bound. As Sherry Felix, an English teacher and the school's Title I coordinator, said, 'this is just a small treat,' and she hopes the program will expand to support more students in transitioning into their post-high school education, noting that 'We know that there's an economic need, there's a socioeconomic need for our students.'

key_people: Erica Otte, College Park City Council non-voting student liaison
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Anna-Kaye McDonald, College Park City Council deputy student liaison
Denise Mitchell, College Park Mayor Pro Tem
Chris Hatcher, attorney for Landmark Properties
46331.ai_summary: The College Park City Council expressed concerns about a plan to demolish College Park Towers and replace it with two luxury high-rise apartment buildings, citing the need for affordable housing in the area. Councilmembers, including Erica Otte, John Rigg, and Alan Hew, argued that the new development should include a percentage of affordable housing units, with Otte stating that 'Luxury apartments are replacing affordable housing' and Rigg saying 'What we have on site right now is an example of pretty good, market-rate affordable housing that would be difficult to replicate and if it is gone, then it is gone.' Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell also emphasized the importance of affordable housing, saying 'We need to be more collective in letting students find housing that is affordable and that they can afford, not what you’re trying to sell.'

46336.ai_summary: A special election will be held on March 11 to fill the vacant District 3 College Park City Council seat temporarily. A regular city election will take place on November 4 to select a full council, including a mayor and all eight councilmembers. Early, in-person voting will be available on November 1 at Davis Hall, and voters can also mail their ballots or use drop boxes at five locations.

key_people: Ellen Tappin, member of Fight Colorectal Cancer
46287.ai_summary: The City of College Park teamed with the Fight Colorectal Cancer organization to host an event on March 9, educating residents on the dangers of colorectal cancer and the importance of getting screened. As Councilmember Alan Hew said, 'Something about health and personal issues like this aren’t very well versed in minority communities, so we’re trying to build opportunities for them to learn about it, speak about it, without social pressures and things like that.' Ellen Tappin, a colon cancer survivor, hopes that 'people coming into the event today will become aware that colorectal cancer is the No. 2 cause of cancer deaths in the nation' and encourages people to get screened.

key_people: Gannon Sprinkle, City Council Candidate and University of Maryland junior
46182.ai_summary: The three candidates for the vacant District 3 City Council seat, Michael Meadow, Ray Ranker, and Gannon Sprinkle, suggested the city should help federal workers who have lost their jobs by offering financial assistance, job search resources, and referrals to mental health agencies. The candidates agreed they would not vote to raise residential property taxes and instead proposed new projects such as tax credits for homeowners, 'third spaces' for residents, and eco-friendly bike and walking paths. As Ranker said, 'I'm passionate about building community' and 'connecting people', while Sprinkle noted the need for a 'strong safety net' to help those affected by federal job losses.

46022.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now will host a forum on Wednesday, Feb. 26, for the three candidates for the vacant District 3 City Council seat. The forum will feature Mike Meadow, Ray Ranker, and Gannon Sprinkle, and will be moderated by Shawn Anderson of WTOP radio. The event will take place at City Hall and can also be joined via Zoom, allowing observers to attend in person or virtually.

45972.ai_summary: Maryland Gov. Wes Moore's proposed $67.3 billion budget for fiscal year 2026 includes funding to start planning for improvements to Route 1 from Greenbelt Road to the Beltway. As state Sen. Jim Rosapepe said, 'Route 1 is the front door to the University of Maryland; it's our flagship university and we want people, when they come, to feel good about the community,' and College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir agreed, 'Route 1 is our Main Street, plain and simple.' The project will make the highway safer for drivers, cyclists, and pedestrians, with improvements such as dedicated left-turn lanes, bright green bike lanes, and landscaped medians.

key_people: Gannon Sprinkle, UMD Student and City Council Candidate
Michael Meadow, Engineer and City Council Candidate
Ray Ranker, UMD's Lutheran Chaplain and City Council Candidate
45947.ai_summary: Gannon Sprinkle, a University of Maryland junior, is running for the District 3 College Park City Council seat, aiming to 'bridge the gap' between student residents and long-time residents. Sprinkle, who is executive vice president of UMD's Student Government Association, said, 'I do believe that students deserve a voting member of the city council' and intends to meet every long-time resident. If elected, he would focus on issues like affordable housing, food equity, and bicycle and pedestrian safety, stating 'I wish to be here long term' and 'I'm very dedicated to spending the next years of my life to what the College Park residents would like to see out of their city council'.

key_people: Michael Jacobs, Former College Park Mayor
Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember
Andrew Fellows, Former College Park Mayor
45962.ai_summary: Former College Park Mayor Michael Jacobs, who served as an elected official for 24 years, has died at the age of 81. Jacobs played a significant role in establishing relations with the University of Maryland and was a leader of the Quality of Life Committee, focused on updating city codes. As mayor, Jacobs was known for being accessible to residents, with Kathy Bryant recalling, 'He was never too busy to talk to me, or I’m sure, to anybody else who wanted to talk to him.'

key_people: Stefan Leggin, former Brentwood Vice Mayor
organizations: Coalition to Support Prince George’s County Fire and EMS
45951.ai_summary: The College Park City Council agreed to sign a letter to Prince George’s County executive candidates asking for funding to hire 150 new firefighters and emergency medical service staff. The Coalition to Support Prince George’s County Fire and EMS drafted the letter, which also asks for support in reducing barriers to onboarding volunteer firefighters and increasing transparency between the county Fire/EMS Department and local officials. As former Brentwood Vice Mayor Stefan Leggin stated, 'We can use this election as leverage to elevate the need for meaningful change, to elevate the conversation,' and the coalition's efforts aim to prioritize staffing for the county's fire departments.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
organizations: Food and Drug Administration’s Center for Food Safety and Applied Nutrition
45903.ai_summary: College Park residents are feeling the impact of President Donald Trump's executive orders, with federal employees fearing job loss and immigrants carrying their passports in case of questioning. Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'The morale of the community is very low,' and acknowledged that 'there are so many jobs of federal employees at stake.' The city has published links to resources and issued a proclamation called 'One College Park' to support the community and promote diversity, equity, and inclusion.

45978.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has postponed voting on a proposed charter amendment to lower the voting age for city elections to 16 until after the vacant District 3 council seat is filled on March 11 in a special election. A couple of residents spoke against the change, while multiple representatives of Vote16 spoke in favor of it. The council also postponed a vote on a second proposed charter amendment that would allow the mayor and council to appoint a replacement council member if a seat is vacated less than a year before the next election.

45975.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is forming two citizen task forces, one for childcare and one for affordable housing. The Childcare Task Force will track state legislation, scout out federal, state, county and non-profit programs, and interview childcare providers to report to the council. The Affordable Housing Task Force will research and share data about the city’s housing needs, define “affordable housing”, and propose a program to offer affordable housing to those who qualify.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Founding Chair of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project
45563.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has approved a contract with Columbia University to develop a plan for reparations to the Lakeland community, which was harmed by urban renewal efforts in the 1970s and 1980s. According to Councilmember Susan Whitney, 'It is important to have a well-researched, well-documented, academically sound record of what happened in Lakeland and what parties were involved in that occurring, either by actions or inactions.' The research team will determine appropriate reparations regarding economic stability, history of access to education and health care, and other effects of losing the neighborhood and social community.

key_people: Janeen Miller, Age Friendly College Park program lead
Chyna Diaz, director of home preservation for Habitat for Humanity Metro Maryland
45424.ai_summary: Habitat for Humanity's College Park Aging in Place program has upgraded half a dozen homes in the city to make it easier for older residents with limited incomes to continue living at home as they age. The program, which offers repairs and improvements such as wheelchair ramps and grab bars, is part of a nationwide effort and is funded by American Rescue Plan Act (ARPA) funding. As Janeen Miller, Age Friendly College Park program lead, said, 'We would like to be a destination for seniors that are retiring and they're looking for a place to age,' and 'It's the home repair and improvements that we can help seniors with that will allow them to stay in their homes as they age.'

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Robert Day, Former College Park City Councilmember
key_issues: Subsidy for University of Maryland students' rent
45574.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams has resigned from his position, citing the demands of his job and family life. Adams, who represented District 3, said 'I just knew that my ability to dedicate the time that the council involved … was constrained' and 'I'd rather worry about Facetiming my kids.' The city will hold a special election to fill his seat, and Mayor Fazlul Kabir said 'It's a loss for us' as Adams 'has done quite a bit of work, even though it's been only three years now.'

45420.ai_summary: Two candidates, Michael Meadow and Ray Ranker, have announced they will run for the District 3 College Park City Council seat vacated by the resignation of Councilmember Stuart Adams. Meadow, a lifelong resident of College Park, said he would focus on helping the city 'grow and prosper, but at the same time I want to take care of the small business owners.' Ranker, UMD's Lutheran chaplain, stated he wants to 'make sure that those whose primary language is not English, that they're not left behind' and aims to create a city that is 'livable, walkable, bikeable, a place that can offer support to students, to families, to seniors.'

key_people: Alan Bradford, Volunteer and Recipient of the 2024 Jack Perry Award
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Joseph Perry, Son of Jack Perry
organizations: College Park Boys and Girls Club
45413.ai_summary: The College Park City Council presented Alan Bradford with the 2024 Jack Perry Award, recognizing his 42 years of volunteer service to the community. As Mayor Fazlul Kabir wrote, 'Alan's countless acts of service … have fostered a spirit of community and improved the quality of life for residents, embodying the legacy of Jack Perry himself.' Joseph Perry, Jack Perry's son, expressed his family's pride in Bradford, stating 'Once the nominations came out, we as a family were very proud that you were there, and without a question … you were our choice.'

organizations: Department of Housing and Urban Development
key_issues: Use of American Rescue Plan Act funds
45409.ai_summary: The Attick Towers, a 108-unit apartment building for seniors, is starting its third year of renovation, with repairs that could last up to five more years and cost up to $16 million. According to Councilmember Susan Whitney, 'It’s a building that’s, you know, about 50 years old, and there’s a lot of deferred maintenance,' due to insufficient funding from the federal Department of Housing and Urban Development. The College Park City Council has allocated a total of nearly $3 million in American Rescue Plan Act funds toward the renovation, with the Housing Authority seeking additional grants and potentially refinancing the building to cover the remaining costs.

45598.ai_summary: The College Park City Council will hold a public hearing on February 4 to discuss a proposal to change the minimum voting age in College Park from 18 to 16. Councilmembers who support the amendment believe that teenage voters tend to grow up to be civically engaged adults. If the amendment passes, College Park residents who register to vote in Prince George’s County and will be at least 16 on Election Day will automatically become registered to vote in the city.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Robert Day, former College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
key_issues: resignation of a city council member
45174.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams announced his resignation, effective January 8, due to family commitments. As a young father, Adams stated, 'As a young father, my availability as a city councilmember has become more constrained,' and 'I must reflect, and based on weighing my priorities, I have decided to resign.' The city charter requires a special election to fill Adams' seat within 65 days, with his replacement holding the seat until the next general election in November.

key_people: James Keleti, Maj. of Hyattsville Division 1
David Mitchell, University of Maryland Police Chief
Rosanne Hoaas, Lt. of the University of Maryland Police Department
Stephen Boehm, Deputy Chief of the Metro Transit Police
Yasmin Brown, Maryland-National Capital Park Police Assistant Chief
45050.ai_summary: The University of Maryland Police Department is stopping electric scooters for traffic violations, including failure to stop at stop signs and riding on sidewalks. As Police Chief David Mitchell noted, 'We have charged people for intoxication on a scooter. We will continue to do that.' The police department is also targeting scooter drivers who break traffic rules as part of a monthly program. The department's efforts are part of a broader challenge to address scooter-related crimes, including thefts, in the College Park area.

key_people: Lisa Eally, Meals on Wheels of College Park Chair
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: Meals on Wheels of College Park
45028.ai_summary: Meals on Wheels of College Park, which distributes meals to senior citizens, will not be returning to College Park anytime soon due to lack of suitable space. According to Lisa Eally, the organization's chair, 'We’re doing whatever we can to find a location back in College Park,' but they were unable to come to an agreement with the developer of the Flats at College Park. The College Park City Council has allocated $400,000 for Meals on Wheels to use if it moves during fiscal year 2025 and has also divided $455,000 of ARPA money among Meals on Wheels and other local meal delivery services, with Councilmember Stuart Adams stating that 'The support that the city has pushed toward these types of services are magnitudes [more than] what it used to be.'

45025.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted unanimously to spend $7,354,000 to renovate Duvall Field, a seven-acre park in the city. Councilmembers agreed that the longer they waited, the more expensive the project would become, with Councilmember Alan Hew saying “Making greatness in our city costs, and the longer we wait, the more it’s going to cost.” The renovation plan includes the addition of soccer and baseball fields, an event amphitheater, and other amenities, with City Manager Kenny Young warning that value-engineering the project would set the start date back by at least another year.

key_people: Michael Williams, College Park Director of Economic Development
44853.ai_summary: A Ross Dress For Less discount store has opened in North College Park, filling the vacancy left by Best Buy. According to College Park Councilmember Jacob Hernandez, 'If you see across the city, we don’t have any major clothing store options... Ross provides, you know, high quality clothing and merchandise for low cost and affordable prices.' The store is expected to be a major economic driver, hiring 70 to 110 new employees, and is seen as a positive development by residents such as Kahyla Hernandez, who said 'I think it’s good... it’s a refresher, considering that we’re [near] campus and there’s a bunch of college students, so I think they would appreciate having something so close.'

key_people: Keith Hooks, local representative of the National Association of Letter Carriers
44850.ai_summary: Some North College Park residents are complaining about mail not being delivered every day, with issues including missing checks and incorrect deliveries. Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell stated that 'There’s maybe two or three days that a resident will not get mail at all' and that many seniors or retirees are not receiving their pension or Social Security checks in a 'timely fashion'. Keith Hooks, a local representative of the National Association of Letter Carriers, mentioned that the post office has had an 'onslaught' of new, inexperienced employees following a rash of retirements of long-time mail carriers, and that union leaders want to shift the perspective of mail carriers back to 'that our communities come first, that our customer base comes first'.

key_people: Yasmin Brown, Assistant Chief of the Maryland-National Capital Park Police
Lt. Brian Wiesemann, Commander of the Maryland State Police College Park barracks
Maj. Jim Keleti, Commander of the Prince George’s County Police Hyattsville Division 1
Stephen Boehm, Deputy Chief of the Metro Transit Police
44814.ai_summary: Theft is the most common crime in College Park, with thefts from automobiles and thefts of automobiles being most prevalent, according to law enforcement officials. As Yasmin Brown, assistant chief of the Maryland-National Capital Park Police, stated, 'Most of our incidents are theft-related... A lot of that is a crime of opportunity: people leaving something in their car or leaving their car unlocked.' Overall, crime in College Park is down by 11%, despite a rise in certain types of theft, such as scooter theft, which has become a major issue on the University of Maryland campus.

key_people: Stephanie Stullich, Former College Park City Councilmember
Rebecca Bailey, City's Animal Welfare Officer
44810.ai_summary: Stephanie Stullich, a former College Park city councilmember, has been fostering stray cats and finding them homes with the help of the city's animal welfare officer and the nonprofit Beltsville Community Cats. She is one of about half a dozen volunteers in the city who take in homeless kittens until they can be adopted. As Patricia Coil, a volunteer, said, 'You bring them in, and you never know what you're going to get, They each develop their own personality. It's just cute to watch them … become cats over the course of, like, two or three months.'

key_people: William Pines, Maryland State Highway Administrator
Charlie Gischlar, State Highway Administration Deputy Director
44790.ai_summary: Maryland Senator Jim Rosapepe called for swift planning and completion of the U.S. Route 1 Improvement Project during a ribbon-cutting ceremony for Phase 1 in College Park. Rosapepe said, 'We're hoping that we can get the planning [for the rest of the project] in this coming year's budget,' and noted that funding and utility line relocation had delayed the project. The first phase, which cost $56.9 million, added bicycle lanes, raised medians, expanded lanes, and upgraded drainage, and the State Highway Administration remains committed to safety improvements, with Administrator William Pines stating, 'We are going to keep going, we're going to continue to pursue grant funding through the US DOT RAISE program to try to achieve a grant for future costs.'

key_people: Joel Biermann, Former University Park Mayor
Patrick Wojahn, Former College Park Mayor
Debi Sandlin, University Park Town Administrator
Fazlul Kabir, Current College Park Mayor
Aisha Braveboy, Prince George’s County State’s Attorney
44763.ai_summary: Former University Park Mayor Joel Biermann was arrested on October 28 and charged with 28 counts relating to child sexual abuse materials, with investigators finding over 45,000 files of suspected child sexual abuse materials. Biermann served as mayor from June 2022 to June 2024 and is no longer employed by the town. The arrest comes about 20 months after former College Park Mayor Patrick Wojahn was arrested on child pornography charges, and current College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir declined to comment on Biermann's charges.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Hua Wang, Owner of Northwest Chinese Food
