Xue Ling, Brand Manager of Northwest Chinese Food
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Ashfiha Rahman, University of Maryland Student
Flavyne Tsongwain, University of Maryland Student
44760.ai_summary: Northwest Chinese Food was proclaimed College Park's 2024 Business of the Year by Mayor Fazlul Kabir, citing its unique cuisine and commitment to community engagement. As Kabir stated, 'Their efforts have made College Park a more vibrant and diverse place to live.' The restaurant, which has been a staple on Baltimore Avenue since 2015, will eventually close its current location to make way for new student apartments and will relocate to the city hall building, where it will reopen as Li Chun Cafe.

key_people: Josanne Francis, UMD’s lead community engagement program coordinator
Trevor Muñoz, director of UMD’s Maryland Institute for Technology in the Humanities
Xiomara Bradden, junior government and politics major at UMD
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC)
44704.ai_summary: The 13th annual Good Neighbor Day in College Park drew hundreds of volunteers to work on various projects, including a permaculture garden installation, removal of invasive plant species, and a 'transcribe-athon' to preserve the history of the Lakeland neighborhood. As Josanne Francis, UMD’s lead community engagement program coordinator, said, 'With Good Neighbor Day, we are ensuring that we are catering to the needs of the City of College Park and environment.' The event aims to enhance community space and be educational, with projects designed to be completed within 3.5 hours, and as Xiomara Bradden noted, 'preserving the history of African Americans, especially in this community who broke barriers, is extremely important.'

44571.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering installing stop sign cameras around the city after a three-week pilot program showed that up to 92% of drivers did not come to a full stop at the signs. As Councilmember Susan Whitney said, 'I just wanted to make sure there was an opportunity for public comment and really frame this in a clear and public way.' The program’s goal is safety, not profit, with Councilmember Maria Mackie stating 'Our motivation is safety,' and 'The city’s not making big money on this.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
John Carroll, Mayor of Galena, Maryland
Raymond Rodriguez, Moderator and University of Maryland School of Public Policy
organizations: University of Maryland School of Public Policy
44540.ai_summary: College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir emphasized the importance of resident engagement in building trust in government, stating 'The major thing I think we should be doing in … having more transparency to the community is to have more engagement … in a small city like us, even though we are not very small.' Kabir shared his experiences at a 'Living Democracy' symposium on the University of Maryland campus, where he was joined by Galena, Maryland, Mayor John Carroll. Kabir also highlighted the need for transparency and communication with all residents, including those who do not speak English, saying 'I know the pain and some of the communication gap that the immigrant community, especially the Hispanic community … they are facing.'

key_people: Andreé Sahakian, senior development manager at LV Collective
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
44458.ai_summary: A Texas-based development company, LV Collective, is planning to build a mixed-use housing complex at the site of the former Campus Village Shoppes in College Park. The seven-story building will have 299 units and 13,000 square feet of retail space, including a community space for the Lakeland neighborhood, as stated by Andreé Sahakian, senior development manager at LV Collective, 'With each development, we look for creative ways to collaborate with the community, embracing its history and honoring its character and culture.' The project faced community backlash after the developer sent closure notices to the tenants of the former strip mall, but some businesses have been invited to return to the space once the building is finished.

key_people: Sydney Nicole, University of Maryland student
Michael Williams, Director of Economic Development
44465.ai_summary: A sports apparel store, Rally House, is set to move into the former Target storefront on Baltimore Avenue, with the College Park City Council approving a $130,000 grant for the store. According to Michael Williams, Director of Economic Development, the vacancy has been 'economically detrimental to the city' and Rally House's move is expected to bring up to 70 new employees. As Sydney Nicole, a University of Maryland student, said, 'A lot of people around this area, they wear athleisure, Lululemon, Nike, Adidas; all that’s very popular around College Park, especially with college students. So I think it’ll be really good for business.'

key_people: Carlos Johnson, Principal of Hollywood Elementary School
Lindsay Sullivan, Associate Vice President of Amplify
44455.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved $40,000 for a tutoring program for Hollywood Elementary School’s Spanish-speaking students. The program, provided by Amplify Tutoring, offers reading tutoring to 24 students in third to fifth grades. As Councilmember Jacob Hernandez said, 'If I could, I would try to carve [the] entirety of our budget to try and help the schools that we have here in College Park,' and Councilmember Maria Mackie noted she was 'very impressed with how [Amplify does] their tutoring and their success that they had [at Hollywood Elementary] in just four short months of last year, that they brought the students’ reading ability up.'

44445.ai_summary: The executive director of College Park Here & Now, Kit Slack, thanked over 200 readers for taking the first audience survey and shared the results, which indicated that readers rely on the publication for city government coverage, local events, and hyperlocal housing and development news. Readers also requested more depth and detail in city council coverage and urged the publication to represent the diverse communities it serves. As Slack stated, 'We’ll keep trying to thread the needle on a tight budget, and we will be asking for your help to do it.'

key_people: Christina Cross, Director of the UMD Child Development Center
organizations: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services
locations: University of Maryland Child Development Center
44448.ai_summary: College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir sent a letter to state legislators asking for a review of day care rules that require one staff member per three children under 24 months, citing 'significant economic burdens on families in College Park'. Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that Maryland has 'perhaps one of the most restrictive staff/child ratios in the United States for infants and toddlers', leading to high costs, with tuition at the University of Maryland Child Development Center reaching $27,744 a year for infants and toddlers. Adams said that if the ratio is increased, parents could have more opportunities to find suitable, affordable, high-quality child care, and predicted a change in the state's required staff-to-student ratio could take two or three years.

44437.ai_summary: College Park has installed cameras on nine stop signs around the city to collect data on traffic and parking. Councilmember Stuart Adams said the city located the cameras at heavily trafficked intersections, and the data will be discussed at the Oct. 8 meeting. State Delegate Anne Healey, who sponsored legislation allowing the cameras, said 'there have been a lot of people that have talked to me about people just ignoring the stop signs, just running right through them, sometimes very fast and not slowing down at all, and near misses and accidents and people getting hurt,' and expects the cameras to change driver behavior.

key_people: Mike Wells, Senior Director of Franchise Operations for The Greene Turtle
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
44147.ai_summary: The Greene Turtle, a sports bar and grill, is set to open in College Park in late September, bringing a new casual dining option to the city. 'We're happy and we're really excited to be in College Park,' said Mike Wells, Senior Director of Franchise Operations for The Greene Turtle, who noted that the restaurant has long partnered with University of Maryland Athletics. College Park City Councilmembers Susan Whitney and Stuart Adams have expressed support for the new business, with Whitney noting that she has some concern about the restaurant staying within its allowed limit of alcohol sales, saying 'They definitely have the experience to be successful.'

key_people: Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Gail Lovelace, co-chair of the Age-Friendly College Park initiative
Mary Anne Hakes, co-chair of the Senior Advisory Committee
key_issues: quality of life for older adults
44115.ai_summary: College Park is working toward achieving an age-friendly designation from the AARP, which indicates the city offers a good quality of life for older adults. 'I think that we need, as a city, to make College Park a place that people want to retire to and people want to continue living here,' said College Park City Councilmember Maria Mackie. The city's volunteer Senior Advisory Committee began working toward the designation in 2020, and members of the Age-Friendly College Park workgroup hope to earn the designation by next summer.

key_people: Aaron Goldbeck, University of Maryland Bicycle Coordinator
44031.ai_summary: The city of College Park has seen a surge in e-bikes and e-scooters in recent years, with companies like Bird and Lime introducing dockless e-scooters and e-bikes, but the rise of these alternatives has also presented challenges such as charging and durability. As Aaron Goldbeck, University of Maryland bicycle coordinator, said, 'We are very excited about the growth and success of the system.' The Union of Concerned Scientists has concluded that ride-hailing trips result in an estimated 69 percent more climate pollution on average than the trips they displace, making the exploration of micromobility alternatives crucial for College Park governance.

key_people: Susan Slingluff Hartmann, Executive Director of the College Park City-University Partnership
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
43934.ai_summary: The College Park City-University Partnership plans to spend $3 million to build an off-campus bike lane along Campus Drive, connecting to the Trolley Trail. Mayor Fazlul Kabir hopes to extend the trail north to Beltsville, citing safety concerns for cyclists. As Kabir, a cyclist himself, said, 'Beltsville is very dangerous, actually, to bike, so that’s something we’ll be advocating for' and 'The idea is to give residents information that there are ways other than taking your car.'

43946.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering lowering the voting age to 16, with Councilmember John Rigg stating that 'the younger they start participating in elections, the more likely they will regularly vote as adults.' Rigg noted that few 16- and 17-year-olds would be likely to vote, and therefore would be unlikely to sway an election, saying 'As a matter of mathematics and a matter of political economy, there's only about 300 residents ages 16 and 17 in the city.' The council also declined to continue a decade-old effort to lengthen their terms from two years to four years.

key_people: Brian Galbraith, Cherokee Lane Elementary Principal
Kayla Newsome, Cherokee Lane Elementary Assistant Principal
Bernitta Johnson, former coordinator of the signature programs and adviser of the business program
43931.ai_summary: Eight local schools have received a combined $36,800 in grants from the City of College Park to fund educational projects, including an outdoor classroom, experiential learning opportunities, and a pitch competition for aspiring student entrepreneurs. As Cherokee Lane Elementary Principal Brian Galbraith said, 'We are grateful for cities like College Park... They give schools these opportunities to think outside the box.' The grants will support various initiatives, such as Cherokee Lane's outdoor classroom, where Assistant Principal Kayla Newsome noted, 'The outdoor classroom will allow our children to explore and discover the world around them in ways that a traditional classroom simply cannot.'

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Varaa Kukreti, President of the UMD’s Graduate Student Government
Edward Maginnis Jr., UMD’s assistant vice president for real estate
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
43919.ai_summary: The University of Maryland is planning to build a new graduate student housing complex, Leonardtown apartments, near downtown College Park, which is expected to have a positive impact on the local economy. College Park City Councilmember John Rigg said, “It’ll be a vast improvement over the status quo,” and “It’ll provide much needed housing at below market for some of our residents who have the most financial needs.” Varaa Kukreti, the new president of the UMD’s Graduate Student Government, said the project is a “step in the right direction” for graduate student housing, although the new apartments will not be as affordable as students would like.

43838.ai_summary: The College Park City Council recommended approval of a four-story, eight-unit apartment building on Autoville Drive, which will replace a single-family home. Councilmember Alan Hew praised the property owner for being receptive to the city's suggestions, saying 'It’s rare that we have an applicant that’s so cooperative to the conditions put on them.' Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell cast the sole vote against the project, stating 'I will be voting no regarding this project due to the fact that it will change the character of the community, the quality of life of the residents.'

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Julia Bauer, Research and Data Analysis Program Manager for the National League of Cities
Michael Williams, Economic Development Department Director
43835.ai_summary: The City of College Park has spent $19 million of its $22 million in federal pandemic funding from the American Rescue Plan Act (ARPA) to help residents pay bills, subsidize local businesses, and invest in affordable housing. According to Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember, 'Without question, that was something unprecedented in the City of College Park.' The city must use the remaining $3 million by December 31, 2026, and has focused on using ARPA funds to help residents and restart the local economy, with small businesses receiving $1.7 million in assistance.

key_people: Janeen Miller, College Park City Clerk
43564.ai_summary: College Park City Clerk Janeen Miller will retire on Aug. 1 after 18 years in the position, to be succeeded by Assistant City Clerk Yvette Allen. Miller said she is ready for the freedom that comes with retirement, stating 'More free time to myself to do the things that I put on the back burner and enjoy life without the responsibility of waking up to an alarm clock.' According to College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, Miller 'reminds us, she keeps us on track' and will be missed by her colleagues.

key_people: Tiffany Green, Prince George’s County Fire Chief
organizations: Branchville Volunteer Fire Co. & Rescue Squad
Berwyn Heights Volunteer Fire Department & Rescue Squad
43553.ai_summary: The Prince George’s County Circuit Court denied a request by College Park, Greenbelt, and Berwyn Heights to stop a plan to move paid firefighters from the Greenbelt and Berwyn Heights fire stations to elsewhere in the county. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'The damage is already done,' and noted that the moves will 'definitely impacting College Park residents.' The relocation of 55 firefighters, including 24 from Greenbelt and six from Berwyn Heights, may lengthen response times to fire and medical emergencies, according to the jurisdictions.

key_people: Michael Bodaken, Member of the Prince George’s County Council’s Rent Stabilization Working Group and Adjunct Instructor at the University of Maryland’s School of Public Policy
organizations: City of College Park Office of Economic Development
43581.ai_summary: A proposed 3% rent cap in Prince George’s County may have a low impact on College Park, as up to 65% of apartment buildings could be exempt due to being constructed after 2000. According to Michael Williams, the city’s economic development director, 'My takeaway personally, and at the first swipe of it, is that because of all the new construction that really boomed since 2012, that we’re going to have a very small or very little effect on the 3% cap.' The rent cap aims to protect tenants from excessive rent hikes while ensuring it does not inhibit investment in the county, as stated by County Councilmember Eric Olson, who said the council worked 'toward a fair compromise that will achieve all the goals that we want: protecting tenants from, you know, outrageous gouging and that kind of thing, while also making sure that what we do doesn’t inhibit investment in the county.'

key_people: Tiffany Green, Prince George’s County Fire Chief
43462.ai_summary: The cities of Greenbelt, Berwyn Heights, and College Park filed a joint motion for a Temporary Restraining Order against Prince George’s County to prevent the removal of career firefighters from local stations. The county argued that the summer staffing plan is for the department's health and safety and in the public interest, and Judge William Snoddy rejected the motion, primarily on the grounds that the plaintiffs had not demonstrated irreparable harm. The municipalities had warned that the plan's 'reliance on volunteers to maintain service levels raises serious concerns about the ability to provide timely and effective emergency responses' and claimed that 'if the injunction is denied, it would simply be a matter of time until an emergency situation arises, and the public suffers an exacerbated injury due to the defendants’ lack of adequate staffing of the fire stations in question'.

key_people: Mike Galvin, Director of SavATree’s consultant group
Richard Jones, University of Maryland Campus Arborist
organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
43393.ai_summary: A new assessment found that tree cover on residential properties in College Park decreased between 2009 and 2020, despite overall growth throughout the rest of the city. Mike Galvin, director of SavATree’s consultant group, said the city’s efforts to limit tree removal and encourage planting are “great proactive steps” toward slowing or stopping the loss of tree canopy in neighborhoods. According to the report, the city has more than 56,800 trees, and University of Maryland Campus Arborist Richard Jones noted that “having forest is so critical, not only for the environment, but for our ability to go about our day-to-day lives in a city.”

key_people: Ryna Quinones, Communications and Events Manager
43119.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is questioning the future of the annual spring parade, with Councilmember John Rigg suggesting that the city consider getting out of the parade business due to its high cost, approximately $47 per attendee. Ryna Quinones, the city's communications and events manager, noted that the event has been challenging for the city, with difficulty recruiting volunteers and performers, and stagnant attendance over the past three years. Councilmembers are now exploring alternative community-building events, with Rigg suggesting a music festival and Councilmember Susan Whitney proposing a North College Park version of Friday Night LIVE!

key_people: Ken Ulman, President of Terrapin Development Co.
Sahil Patel, UMD Junior Computer Science Major
organizations: University of Maryland College Park Foundation
43121.ai_summary: Shake Shack, Chopt, and Duck Donuts will open their first Prince George’s County eateries in College Park this fall, as part of the new student housing building, Union on Knox. Ken Ulman, President of Terrapin Development Co., said 'Shake Shack, you know, frankly, they have been looking for a location in College Park for a number of years.' City Councilmember John Rigg expressed excitement about the new additions to downtown College Park, saying 'I have a passion for small businesses, but I know these will be great additions to College Park that members of the community will enjoy.'

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
43109.ai_summary: The city of College Park offers several programs to help homeowners save on property taxes, including the Homestead Property Tax Credit, Homeowners' Property Tax Credit, and Elderly Property Tax Credit. According to College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams, 'I would say without hesitation that College Park has some of the most generous property tax credits of any municipality in Prince George’s County and some of the strongest in the entire state.' These programs aim to limit the increase in property taxes for eligible homeowners, with some offering additional discounts based on income or age.

key_people: Gannon Sprinkle, Former Deputy Student Liaison
organizations: Harford County Parks and Recreation Department
Maryland Office of the Public Defender
43126.ai_summary: Two new University of Maryland student liaisons, Erica Otte and John Fahmy, will start their year-long terms with the College Park City Council in June. Otte said, 'I'm very passionate about advocacy,' and aims to 'make it easier for students to connect with the broader community.' Councilmember Maria Mackie stated, 'The biggest thing was her real desire to build bridges between students and long-term residents,' which set Otte apart from the competition.

key_people: Teresa Way-Pezzuti, Director of Human Resources
43045.ai_summary: The City of College Park will increase salaries for 74 employees after a study showed they earn less than their peers in other cities in the region. As Teresa Way-Pezzuti, Director of Human Resources, said, the city decided to be at least at the 50th percentile, and Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters stated, 'I think it’s vitally important for us to bring our employees to a fair compensation and wage, especially if we want to maintain them.' The salary increases could cost the city about $240,784.

43114.ai_summary: The Washington Post featured the North College Park neighborhoods of Hollywood, Branchville, and Daniels Park in an article about the advocacy efforts of their residents. As reporter Iris Vukmanovic wrote, 'The community benefits from engaged neighbors who know how to work together,' highlighting their efforts to save the Hollywood Farmers Market through an email-writing campaign. The article showcases the community's passion for walking and biking, sense of community, and diversity, and notes the residents' successful campaign that flooded College Park City Councilmembers' inboxes with nearly 100 messages of support.

key_people: Gary Fields, Director of the College Park Department of Finance
Bonny Davis, long-time College Park resident
42752.ai_summary: The College Park City Council adopted a $27.4 million operating budget for fiscal year 2025, which includes an 11% residential property tax rate increase. As Gary Fields, Director of the College Park Department of Finance, stated, 'We do not take proposing a property tax increase lightly... We're looking to the future to make sure we stay financially sound.' The tax increase will help fund city services, sustainability efforts, and new parks and facilities, as well as a needs-based student housing subsidy pilot program, which some residents, like Brian Roan and Bonny Davis, opposed due to concerns about rising costs and the burden on homeowners.

key_people: Glenn Ivey, U.S. Congress representative for Maryland District 4
key_issues: Summer student exodus and its impact on local businesses
42023.ai_summary: The city of College Park has entered the contractor selection phase for a flood abatement project in the Calvert Hills neighborhood, with plans to begin mid-summer. Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that current codes are not up-to-date with climate change, stating 'We need to acknowledge that this is going to happen a lot more frequently than people want to believe.' Meanwhile, local businesses are preparing for the summer student exodus from the University of Maryland, getting creative to handle the steep drop in customers during the summer months.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Chair of the Restorative Justice Commission
Ruth Murphy, Lakeland Civic Association Vice President
Robert Thurston, Lakeland Civic Association President
41839.ai_summary: Maxine Gross, a fifth-generation Lakelander and Chair of the Restorative Justice Commission, has committed to documenting and preserving the history of Lakeland, a community that was impacted by the city's urban renewal effort in the 1960s. As Gross said, 'It looked like nirvana... It looked like paradise' when she first saw the city plans, but 'over time, the plans changed, and ultimately something else was built.' Gross, along with Ruth Murphy, played a major role in ensuring that a new Lakeland community center will be included in the student housing development that will replace Campus Village Shoppes, which will also store a physical, historic archive of Lakeland.

key_people: Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Carson Peters, doctoral student at the UMD School of Public Health
Megan Ravert, representative of the Sustainable Terps
Mark Williams, director of the jazz studies program
41833.ai_summary: The City of College Park participated in the University of Maryland's Maryland Day, offering information about local resources to attendees. According to College Park City Manager Kenny Young, 'I think it’s an important event just to showcase what the University of Maryland offers to the community,' and 'It gives people a chance to come in and really have an experience of the University of Maryland and its sense of community.' The city set up a tent in the heart of the mall, where attendees could engage with city staff, play lawn games, and meet the mayor and city councilmembers.

key_people: Rachel Romeo, UMD hearing and speech sciences professor
Susan Slingluff Hartmann, executive director of the College Park City-University Partnership
organizations: Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development
41822.ai_summary: The College Park City-University Partnership has ended an eight-year homeownership program that awarded $15,000 to 83 new homebuyers, and has launched a new program called Live + Work College Park to support full-time employees working in the city. According to Susan Slingluff Hartmann, the new program offers a forgivable loan of $20,000 to help with down payments and closing costs. As Rachel Romeo, a UMD professor who benefited from the previous program, said, 'it did make it possible for us to buy a house we might not otherwise have been able to' and allowed her to 'live in the community that I work in and do research with'.

key_people: Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember and Structural Engineer
Daniel Oates, President of the Calvert Hills Citizens Association
41817.ai_summary: The Calvert Hills stormwater drainage project in College Park is moving forward after over a decade, with contractor selection underway and work expected to begin by mid-summer. As Prince George’s County Councilmember Eric Olson said, 'It’s unfortunate that this project took this long, but I’m glad that we’re finally at the stage where it [went] to bid,' and 'The sooner we get construction started, the sooner it will finish.' The project aims to alleviate flooding in the area, but as College Park City Councilmember and structural engineer Stuart Adams noted, 'We need to acknowledge that this is going to happen a lot more frequently than people want to believe,' and the project 'is based on the data that’s currently available today. It’s going to bring us up to the current standard, but not up to current climate.'

key_people: Ryna Quiñones, Communications and Events Manager
Betty Colonomos, College Park Senior Advisory Committee member
41846.ai_summary: The city of College Park held its third annual Star Wars-themed parade, which attracted hundreds of residents despite the rain. According to College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, 'It's wonderful, I've met quite a few neighbors, and I'm very pleased that they came with their families.' The city's communications and events manager, Ryna Quiñones, noted that the parade is a community-building event, saying 'There's something for everybody, for every age, and [our offerings are] very diverse.' City resident Betty Colonomos also emphasized the importance of such events, stating 'If you can't stop and smell the roses, or whatever, once in a while, it gets really hard to keep going.'

key_people: Nora Urbieta Eidelman, Church Administrator & Outreach Director
Merrill Hartson, Retired Reporter, Editor and Manager
organizations: Maryland Cannabis Public Health Advisory Council
Community Legal Services of Prince George’s County, Inc.
Human Rights Commission for Prince Georges’ County
42280.ai_summary: The article presents a list of board members, including Marta McLellan Ross as President, Melanie Dzwonchyk as Secretary, and Joe Murchison as Treasurer. These individuals bring a range of experience in media, public service, and community leadership. Nora Urbieta Eidelman, a Church Administrator and Outreach Director, has also been recognized for her outstanding service representing the civil legal needs of low-income Marylanders.

key_people: Kelly Jordan, Co-President of the Berwyn District Civic Association
Bob Catlin, Co-President of the Berwyn District Civic Association
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
41659.ai_summary: The Berwyn community in College Park has been publishing a bilingual newsletter for two years, thanks to a grant from the city to pay for translation services. 'You have to understand your audience and who makes up your audience,' said Bob Catlin, Co-President of the Berwyn District Civic Association. The city's proposed operating budget for fiscal year 2025 includes $7,500 for neighborhood associations to pay for translators, which College Park City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez supports, saying 'I'm all about getting folks who speak Spanish to be a part of the larger community.'

41342.ai_summary: The Hollywood Farmers Market in North College Park will open for the season on April 13, despite concerns from city staff about its cost, with the city considering increasing the market's budget to $30,000 next year. Assistant City Manager Bill Gardiner noted that 'In the big scheme of our budget, it's not very much money,' but added that 'At the same time, in terms of comparable farmers markets, it's still unusual.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated that 'Some of them are very passionate, had very strong comments and strong support for the market,' highlighting the market's value to the Hollywood neighborhood for community engagement.

41366.ai_summary: Mayor Fazlul Kabir delivered the annual State of the City address, highlighting College Park's 'exponential growth' in 2023 with the opening of 10 businesses and expecting nearly 20 new businesses to open in the coming year. The city is also adding two student housing complexes and three affordable housing complexes through grants and partnerships. The mayor noted the city's efforts to improve sustainability, diversity, and safety, and announced donations to community organizations, including $300,000 to fire companies and $500,000 to local food banks.

41323.ai_summary: The College Park city council is considering a 13% increase in residential property taxes, which would be the first hike in 10 years. According to Gary Fields, the city's finance director, 'It’s a reset, we’re doing it for the future. … We feel like that we will not have to be looking at [another] rate increase in the near future.' The proposed tax increase is part of a $27.4 million budget proposal for fiscal year 2025, which aims to balance the city's revenue and expenses.

41358.ai_summary: The application for the student liaison to the College Park City Council is open until April 12. The student liaison and deputy liaison serve one-year terms and participate in weekly council meetings, conducting research and finding experts to speak to the council. As Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'It is important to bridge the gap as much as we can ... Anything we do at the city council level that can potentially impact the campus community in any manner … it is important for [students] to learn that as well and get their input on those issues.'

key_people: Mary Lehman, Del. (D-Prince George’s and Anne Arundel)
41348.ai_summary: The Maryland General Assembly passed two bills to increase penalties for street racing and allow Prince George’s and Montgomery counties to install noise-sensing traffic cameras. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated that street racing and stunt driving have increased in the city, saying 'It is not unusual for groups of motorcycle and ATV drivers to briefly take over sections of Baltimore Avenue on Route 1... These actions harm our communities, and these actions waste … resources, especially the police resources.' Del. Mary Lehman noted that 'The fact is, exhibition driving will continue to disrupt our communities, endanger law enforcement and risk serious bodily harm, or worse, to participants and passersby.'

41374.ai_summary: The City of College Park is investigating its racial equity officer, Kayla Carter, after news reports revealed she posted comments on her personal social media accounts that some might consider offensive. Carter's accounts have since been moved to protected status. City Manager Kenny Young stated, 'Ms. Carter’s views expressed on her personal accounts do not reflect the opinions, beliefs and core values of the City of College Park and its mayor and council.'

key_people: Gregory Slater, Former Maryland Transportation Secretary
Kayla Scott, Manager at Looney’s Pub
41191.ai_summary: The first phase of the Route 1 improvement project is expected to finish this spring after a year-long delay due to staffing and supply shortages during the pandemic. The project, which began in 2020, aims to add bike lanes, raised medians, and turn lanes to Route 1, with a price tag of $28.7 million for the first phase. According to College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, 'The changes, once it’s happened, will make Route 1 very nice... It’s not about making the street pretty, ... It’s for our safety.'

key_people: Danny Copeland, RST Development Vice President
Kate Kennedy, District 1 resident and former city councilmember
organizations: Meals on Wheels of College Park
41187.ai_summary: Developers of the 317-unit affordable apartment building, Flats at College Park, expect to start construction as soon as April, with monthly rents based on income and subsidized by the federal Low-Income Housing Tax Credit Program. As RST Development Vice President Danny Copeland said, 'It’s a redevelopment of some motels that weren’t necessarily a shining light along the Route 1 corridor,' and the new property will 'revitalize the community while putting the space to better use with affordable housing for low-income tenants.' District 1 City Councilmember Alan Hew and resident Kate Kennedy expressed concern about potential traffic congestion, but noted the project's benefits, including affordable housing for University of Maryland employees and others, with Hew saying the city is looking to use similar tax credits to increase affordable housing stock throughout College Park.

key_people: Griffin Limerick, Life & Times managing editor
41131.ai_summary: The city of College Park voted to explore imposing a tax on vacant and/or blighted properties, as it can be more profitable for owners and developers to keep a property vacant. The city is hoping to identify the right tax structure and language to make that math work more towards ending vacancies. Griffin Limerick, Life & Times managing editor, calls for a return of in-person council meetings in Hyattsville, noting that other cities like Takoma Park, Greenbelt, College Park, and Laurel have already returned to some combination of in-person and virtual meetings.

40635.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to form a subcommittee to look into taxing blighted and vacant properties in the city. 'We're in a position now that [we] kind of have to force the issue and get people to take more of an interest in making sure properties are maintained in College Park,' said City Manager Kenny Young. The subcommittee will create a definition of blighted and vacant properties, and decide on a potential tax by studying how other cities have imposed similar taxes, with Councilmember Stuart Adams noting that the intent is to 'mitigate some of the tax incentives that can occur for property owners to keep things vacant'.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Chairwoman of the Restorative Justice Commission
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Joanne Braxton, CEO and President of the Braxton Institute
Nkechi Taifa, RJ Book Club Guest
40745.ai_summary: College Park’s Restorative Justice Commission has partnered with the Braxton Institute to host a series of events in Lakeland about the community’s history and restorative justice. As Maxine Gross, Chairwoman of the Restorative Justice Commission, said, 'if you don’t learn your history, you’re destined to repeat it' and 'it’s important to not just recognize that thing happened, but also to take positive action on assisting that community and heal it.' The events aim to raise awareness about urban renewal in Lakeland and its impact on the community, with College Park City Councilmember Susan Whitney stating that the program will help city residents understand the impact of urban renewal on Lakeland.

key_people: Bonnie McClellan, vice chair of the College Park Woods Neighborhood Watch
Robert Marsili, College Park Director of Public Works
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
40680.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has approved a $1.3 million contract with Rainbow Construction Co. to begin construction on the College Park Woods Clubhouse, which is expected to finish by the end of the year. According to Councilmember Maria Mackie, 'Many of our activities have been underneath the gazebo in the pouring rain, so it’ll be a nice thing to have for the District 4 community, but also for the city.' The new community center will provide indoor space for gatherings and events, and Bonnie McClellan, vice chair of the College Park Woods Neighborhood Watch, says 'This is a dream come true... it will make a very big difference for our community–-and the entire community–-because it’s going to be a very usable space.'

organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
40750.ai_summary: The College Park Restorative Justice Commission has proposed changes to park names to honor Lakeland history, including renaming Lake Artemesia Natural Area and the College Park Community Center. The commission also called for the establishment of a scholarship fund for Lakeland residents, which would 'not only preserve [the Lakeland] legacy but also educate future generations about the important role that this area has played in shaping the city,' according to Kaya Carter, the city's racial equity officer. Councilmember Jacob Hernandez expressed appreciation for the commission's efforts, stating 'I just want to thank you all for the very thoughtful steps to try to address some of the harder parts of our history here in College Park, but also trying to pay homage to the brighter future that we have.'

40533.ai_summary: The City of College Park is expecting the relocation of the Washington Commanders' headquarters to the University of Maryland's Discovery District on River Road, which may attract other prominent companies and impact traffic congestion. The PGCPS board meeting discussed bus problems, with one local parent stating their child's bus had been at least 30 minutes late daily, and board member Pamela Boozer-Strother noted that the district has been unwilling to change school start times despite previous audits recommending it. College Park's Women's History Month celebrations were also criticized for being underwhelming, with the city opting for a static exhibit at city hall.

key_people: Dhruvak Mirani, University of Maryland liaison to the College Park City Council
Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland deputy liaison to the College Park City Council
William Edwards, University of Maryland senior studio art major
40245.ai_summary: University of Maryland student leaders Dhruvak Mirani and Gannon Sprinkle proposed establishing a tenants union for students who rent in College Park, aiming to lower rent costs, improve living conditions, and address maintenance issues. As Mirani said, 'We can’t really address housing prices in a vacuum without talking about all of the other issues that go along with it,' and Sprinkle added, 'We need something to rally behind, a force to fight for better living conditions and lower rent prices.' The median monthly rent in College Park between 2018 and 2022 was $1,838, according to the U.S. Census Bureau, highlighting the need for affordable housing in the area.

key_people: Denise Mitchell, Mayor Pro Tem and Chair of City Council Subcommittee
39395.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has proposed a housing subsidy of $1,500 for 150 low-income University of Maryland undergraduates to help them rent apartments in high-rise Route 1 buildings. Mayor Fazlul Kabir noted that many questions were raised during the public comment period, with residents questioning the plan's dedicating $225,000 to the program, and said 'I'm not quite sure what is going to happen with this proposal at the end, given all the pushback and questions.' The proposal aims to cover one month's rent for each recipient, with the goal of addressing the affordable housing crisis in College Park.

organizations: College Park Committee for a Better Environment (CBE)
39385.ai_summary: The College Park Committee for a Better Environment (CBE) has been leading sustainability efforts in the city, including a project to paint murals and place signs on storm drains to raise awareness about the importance of keeping them clear. As CBE chair Todd Larson said, 'This project gives local artists a chance to show their work in a place where everyone can see it, while also providing an educational message to help residents understand why it’s important not to put anything down the storm drain except water.' The committee has also launched a tree identification program and issues Green Awards to recognize city residents and businesses for their environmental efforts.

key_people: Emily Friend, Berwyn Resident and BDCA Treasurer
39270.ai_summary: The City of College Park is exploring possible uses for a vacant plot of land in Berwyn, including designating it as a pocket park, dog park, or community garden. Resident Emily Friend suggested a rain garden as an alternative, stating it could be used as a teaching tool, while Dan Blasberg expressed doubts about the city's ability to handle storm water management projects, saying 'The city's track record of doing water management projects, to me, is an abject failure.' The city's grants coordinator, Rakhmel Rafi, presented options for the site, which was purchased with funds from the Maryland Department of Natural Resources Program Open Space.

key_people: Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember
39177.ai_summary: New laws are taking effect in Maryland as of January 1, 2024, including a plastic bag ban, minimum wage increase to $15 per hour, and restrictions on telephone solicitations. According to Prince George’s County Councilmember Eric Olson, 'Plastic pollution is a real problem' and the plastic bag ban 'will remove over 300 million plastic bags a year from the waste stream in Prince George’s County alone.' Maryland Del. Joseline Pena-Melnyk said that the higher minimum wage 'is still too low, but it’s a step in the right direction' and 'People are barely getting by. Even $15 an hour is not enough; we should have a living wage.'

key_people: Dr. Lilly, Owner of offices on Route 1
Millard House II, Superintendent of Prince George’s County Public Schools
NKenge Baker, Principal of Northwestern High School
Ashanti Martinez, Member of the state’s House of Delegates
locations: César Chávez Dual Spanish Immersion schools
key_issues: cargo train derailment and environmental concerns
39145.ai_summary: The article reviews 2023, recalling the tragic deaths of two Riverdale Elementary School students struck by a van, and the sentencing of College Park's then-mayor to 30 years in prison for child pornography charges. The year also saw trials such as the PGCPS bus driver shortages, a cyberattack on the school system, and a cargo train derailment along Alternate Route 1. New beginnings included the start of Superintendent Millard House II and Principal NKenge Baker's terms, as well as the opening of the new Hyattsville Middle School.

key_people: Jacob T. Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
39012.ai_summary: Jacob T. Hernandez has joined the College Park City Council as a District 1 representative, bringing a new perspective as the city's only Hispanic American councilmember. Hernandez aims to enhance communication, improve education, and help residents navigate bureaucratic hurdles, stating 'It's only through our united organized voices that we are going to be able to facilitate the greater projects and programs that I have in store'. He hopes to strengthen relationships between the City of College Park and local public education leaders to improve conditions in the city's public schools, and to address overcrowding and provide multilingual information sessions for parents.

key_people: Matt Tedesco, Attorney for Greenhill Capital Corporation
Richard Biffl, Chair of the Historic District Local Advisory Committee
Adele Ellis, Patron of Pho Tom
organizations: Old Town Historic District Local Advisory Committee
39027.ai_summary: A proposed redevelopment in downtown College Park would displace three local restaurants, Pho Tom, Northwest Chinese, and Ritchie’s Colombian Restaurant, to make way for Terrapin House, a six-story mixed-use building with apartments and commercial space. The project has been the subject of multiple public meetings, where residents and officials expressed concerns about the impact on existing restaurants and the historic character of the neighborhood. Adele Ellis, a patron of Pho Tom, said, 'We’re losing too many local restaurants,' and wants the developer to make the rent affordable for the existing restaurants to return to the new building.

key_people: Michael Williams, College Park’s economic development director
39023.ai_summary: The City of College Park is taking steps to help small businesses stay in the city after being displaced by new developments, with a $75,000 business retention fund included in the current annual budget. As Michael Williams, College Park’s economic development director, stated, 'We wanted to retain some of the businesses who make up the fabric and the character of College Park.' Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that around half of the funds raised by a recent commercial property tax increase could be allocated to support local businesses, saying 'it could be more, the council will have to make a determination on that in a future meeting.'

key_people: H.C. 'Curley' Byrd, University of Maryland President
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC)
39017.ai_summary: The author, Maxine Gross, shares the history of Lakeland, a neighborhood in College Park, and its transformation over the years, including the impacts of urban renewal and community displacement. The community's residents, mostly African American, faced many challenges and were eventually forced to sell and leave their homes, with over 100 households displaced. The author notes that 'all of us should care about this history because the prosperity of Lakeland, Berwyn, Hollywood and every neighborhood in the city determines whether the whole city will thrive.'

39032.ai_summary: The Winter Wonderland event was held at city hall plaza on Dec. 2, featuring music, food, and fun for children, families, and college students. Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell and Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters flipped a giant red switch to light up a 20-foot tall holiday tree made of white globes. Old Town resident Kathy Bryant commented that the new city hall was a wonderful venue for the event, saying 'We couldn’t have had something like this before,' and Berwyn resident Emily Friend said her 5-month-old son Miles had a good time, saying 'He was scared of Santa but loved the Grinch,' and 'It’s cool that there was something fun for every group and every age.'

39010.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now is seeking donations to support local news, with a goal of raising $13,000 by the end of the year. As Kit Slack, Executive Director, notes, 'we won’t be able to do it all without your help' and 'if just 90 of our 30,000 readers in College Park chip in $100, we will meet our goal!' The fundraising effort is crucial for the publication to continue covering local events, government, and developments in College Park.

38926.ai_summary: City Councilmember Emily Strab won the inaugural Maryland Catholic Woman of the Year award, presented by the Maryland Catholic Women’s Conference. According to the conference, 'Emily Strab’s selection as the 2023 Maryland Catholic Woman of the Year exemplifies the values of faith, hope, and love in action.' This award recognizes Strab's contributions and values, and she will continue to serve on the city council until her term expires in 2025.

key_people: Sara Rían, Author and Grief Poet
38992.ai_summary: The author, Jaclyn Bruner, is advocating for crossing guards for Hollywood Elementary School after two Riverdale Elementary School students were hit by a car. As the author notes, “don’t you grow up / we say to babies. / don’t you get old / we say to adults. / but please do. / grow up. get old. / what a privilege. / what a gift,” highlighting the importance of keeping children safe. The College Park City Council listened to Bruner's concerns in January, but there are still no crossing guards, with 83 positions open across Prince George’s County.

key_people: Robert "RC" Carter, head of the ElectionGuard program
Benjamin Hovland, member of the College Park Board of Elections Supervisors and federally appointed commissioner to the U.S. Election Assistance Commission
organizations: College Park Board of Elections Supervisors
38704.ai_summary: The City of College Park has implemented new verification technology in its elections, allowing voters to confirm that their votes have been counted. As Robert "RC" Carter, head of the ElectionGuard program, explained, "Think of it as like FedEx saying your package has arrived." The technology, developed by Microsoft, provides voters with a paper confirmation that they can use to verify their vote, and also generates an encrypted public record of the vote that can be inspected and verified by interested parties. This is an opportunity for the ElectionGuard program to test its technology on a larger scale, with the goal of increasing confidence and participation in election outcomes.

key_issues: city ordinance on grass and weeds
38718.ai_summary: Miss Floribunda, a columnist, is away visiting her sister Polyantha in France and has republished a column from 2008 discussing the new city ordinance on grass and weeds that permits alternative gardens and the use of native plants. A resident, Mowed and Manicured, had expressed aesthetic reservations about the new ordinance, but Miss Floribunda argues that native plants and grasses can create beautiful and harmonious gardens, citing examples from France and the British Isles. The ordinance is relevant to College Park governance as it allows for more ecologically friendly and climate-resilient gardening practices.

key_people: Jacob T. Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Kamthorn G. Clay, College Park City Council candidate
Bryan Haddad, College Park City Council candidate
Brian J. Roan, College Park City Council candidate
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Stuart M. Adams, College Park City Councilmember
John B. Rigg III, College Park City Councilmember
A. Perez Abbott, College Park City Council candidate

Susan L. Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Maria E. Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
